# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Similar]`

**State and prove the conjugate symmetry property of the DFT for a real-valued sequence $x(n)$.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Statement:** If $x(n)$ is a real sequence of length $N$, then the DFT coefficients satisfy $X(N-k) = X^*(k)$ for $k = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$.

**Proof:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
X(N-k) &= \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \, e^{-j 2\pi (N-k) n / N} \\
&= \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \, e^{-j 2\pi n} \cdot e^{+j 2\pi kn / N} \\
&= \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \cdot (1) \cdot e^{+j 2\pi kn / N} \\
&= \left[\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \, e^{-j 2\pi kn / N}\right]^* \\
&= X^*(k)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Significance (1 mark):** For real signals, only $\lfloor N/2 \rfloor + 1$ DFT coefficients are independent. This halves the memory and computation required, forming the mathematical basis of the FFT.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Similar]`

**For a real and even sequence $x(n)$, show that the DFT $X(k)$ is purely real.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Given:** $x(n) = x(N-n)$ and $x(n) \in \mathbb{R}$.

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
X(k) &= \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \, e^{-j 2\pi kn / N} \\
&= \frac{1}{2}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \left[e^{-j 2\pi kn/N} + e^{+j 2\pi kn/N}\right] \quad \text{(using even symmetry)} \\
&= \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \cos\left(\frac{2\pi kn}{N}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the cosine term is purely real and $x(n)$ is real, the entire sum is **purely real**. The imaginary part is zero. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length ESE Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** Part B questions on DFT symmetries typically carry sub-parts (a) and (b), each worth 7 marks, with internal choice.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]`

**(a)** State and prove the **conjugate symmetry property** of the DFT for a real sequence $x(n)$. Using this property, explain how the number of computations in computing the DFT can be reduced. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a 4-point real sequence $x(n) = \{1, 1, 1, 1\}$. Compute its 4-point DFT and verify all four symmetry properties (periodicity, conjugate symmetry, real-part even, imaginary-part odd). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Statement:** $X(N-k) = X^*(k)$ for real $x(n)$.

**Proof:** *(See derivation 3.1 above for full 5-step proof — 4 marks for proof, 1 mark for statement)*

**Computational Reduction (2 marks):**
- For real $x(n)$, $X(0)$ and $X(N/2)$ (if $N$ even) are real and self-conjugate.
- The remaining $N - 2$ points form $(N-2)/2$ conjugate pairs.
- Only $\lfloor N/2 \rfloor + 1$ unique DFT values must be stored and computed — a **~50% reduction**.
- This is the foundation of the **radix-2 FFT**, which reduces DFT complexity from $O(N^2)$ to $O(N \log_2 N)$.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Compute the DFT using the definition with $W_4 = e^{-j\pi/2} = -j$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
X(k) &= \sum_{n=0}^{3} x(n) \, W_4^{kn} = \sum_{n=0}^{3} 1 \cdot (-j)^{kn}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate each $X(k)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
X(0) &= 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 4 \\
X(1) &= 1 + (-j) + (-1) + (+j) = 0 \\
X(2) &= 1 + (-1) + (1) + (-1) = 0 \\
X(3) &= 1 + (+j) + (-1) + (-j) = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Verify the symmetry properties:**

| **Property** | **Check** | **Result** |
|:---|:---|:---:|
| Periodicity | $X(4) = X(0) = 4$ | ✅ |
| Conjugate Symmetry | $X(3) = 0 = 0^* = X^*(1)$ | ✅ |
| Real-part Even | $\text{Re}\{X(1)\} = 0 = \text{Re}\{X(3)\}$ | ✅ |
| Imaginary-part Odd | $\text{Im}\{X(1)\} = 0 = -\text{Im}\{X(3)\}$ | ✅ |

> **Note:** The all-ones sequence is both real and even, so the DFT is purely real (as expected from derivation 3.4).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** For a real sequence $x(n)$ of length $N$, show that the **real part of $X(k)$ is even-symmetric** and the **imaginary part is odd-symmetric**. Derive expressions for $\vert X(k) \vert$ and $\angle X(k)$ symmetries. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4-point real sequence has DFT values: $X(0) = 8$, $X(1) = 2 + j3$. Find $X(2)$, $X(3)$ using symmetry properties **without** computing the full DFT. Verify your answer. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1 (3 marks):** From conjugate symmetry, $X(N-k) = X^*(k) = \text{Re}\{X(k)\} - j\,\text{Im}\{X(k)\}$.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** But also $X(N-k) = \text{Re}\{X(N-k)\} + j\,\text{Im}\{X(N-k)\}$. Equating real and imaginary parts:
- $\text{Re}\{X(N-k)\} = +\text{Re}\{X(k)\}$ → **EVEN**
- $\text{Im}\{X(N-k)\} = -\text{Im}\{X(k)\}$ → **ODD**

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Magnitude and phase:
- $\vert X(N-k) \vert = \sqrt{\text{Re}^2 + \text{Im}^2} = \vert X(k) \vert$ → **EVEN**
- $\angle X(N-k) = \arctan(\text{Im}/\text{Re}) = -\angle X(k)$ → **ODD**

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 (2 marks):** For real $x(n)$, $X(0)$ is purely real and self-conjugate: $X(0) = 8$. For $N=4$ even, $X(N/2) = X(2)$ is also real: $X(2) = X^*(2) \in \mathbb{R}$.

**Step 2 (3 marks):** Apply conjugate symmetry: $X(3) = X(4-1) = X^*(1) = (2 + j3)^* = 2 - j3$.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Without further info, $X(2)$ can be any real number (it depends on the specific sequence). The symmetry properties alone do not determine $X(2)$ — we need additional data or the original $x(n)$. For instance, if $x(n)$ is also **real and even**, then $X(2)$ is real (already known) and $X(1) = X^*(3)$ also implies $\text{Re}\{X(1)\}$ is even and $\text{Im}\{X(1)\}$ is odd, which is satisfied: $\text{Re}\{X(1)\} = 2 = \text{Re}\{X(3)\}$ ✅ and $\text{Im}\{X(1)\} = +3 = -\text{Im}\{X(3)\}$ ✅.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing circular and linear reversal:** Students write $x(-n)$ instead of $x((-n))_N = x(N-n)$. Always include the modulo-N operation in circular reversal — **−2 marks deduction if omitted**.
> 2. **Forgetting the condition:** A common error is stating $X(N-k) = X^*(k)$ without specifying "**for real $x(n)$**". Examiners deduct **1–2 marks** for missing the hypothesis.
> 3. **Sign error in odd symmetry:** $\text{Im}\{X(N-k)\} = -\text{Im}\{X(k)\}$, **not** $+\text{Im}\{X(k)\}$. Many students lose marks on this single sign.
> 4. **Periodicity confusion:** $X(k+N) = X(k)$ is for the **DFT only**; for the DTFT, $X(e^{j\omega})$ is periodic with $2\pi$, not $N$.
> 5. **Not stating the symmetry of the unit circle:** $W_N^{-kn} = (W_N^{kn})^*$ requires $\vert W_N \vert = 1$, which is true but rarely stated — losing a half-mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — DFT Symmetries**

- 🔑 **DFT Definition:** $X(k) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) W_N^{kn}$, with $W_N = e^{-j 2\pi/N}$.
- 🔑 **Twiddle Factor Property:** $W_N^{kN} = 1$ and $W_N^{-kn} = (W_N^{kn})^*$ (since $\vert W_N \vert = 1$).
- 🔑 **Master Property:** **Conjugate Symmetry** — $X(N-k) = X^*(k)$ for **real** $x(n)$.
- 🔑 **Real-Even Signal:** $X(k)$ is **purely real** (use cosine formula: $X(k) = \sum x(n)\cos(2\pi kn/N)$).
- 🔑 **Real-Odd Signal:** $X(k)$ is **purely imaginary** (use sine formula: $X(k) = -j \sum x(n)\sin(2\pi kn/N)$).
- 🔑 **Real-part is EVEN:** $\text{Re}\{X(N-k)\} = +\text{Re}\{X(k)\}$.
- 🔑 **Imaginary-part is ODD:** $\text{Im}\{X(N-k)\} = -\text{Im}\{X(k)\}$.
- 🔑 **Magnitude is EVEN:** $\vert X(N-k) \vert = \vert X(k) \vert$.
- 🔑 **Phase is ODD:** $\angle X(N-k) = -\angle X(k)$.
- 🔑 **Time Reversal:** $x((-n))_N \xleftrightarrow{\text{DFT}} X((-k))_N = X(N-k)$.
- 🔑 **Complex Conjugate:** $x^*(n) \xleftrightarrow{\text{DFT}} X^*(N-k)$.
- 🔑 **Periodicity:** Both $x(n)$ and $X(k)$ are periodic with period $N$ (circular world).
- 🔑 **Computational Impact:** Conjugate symmetry → only $\lfloor N/2 \rfloor + 1$ unique bins → halving of storage and computation → basis of the **FFT**.
- 🔑 **Engineering Applications:** OFDM (5G/Wi-Fi), MRI imaging, audio codecs (MP3/AAC), spectrum analyzers.
- 🔑 **Exam Formula to Memorize:** $\frac{W_N^{kn} + W_N^{-kn}}{2} = \cos(2\pi kn/N)$ and $\frac{W_N^{kn} - W_N^{-kn}}{2j} = -\sin(2\pi kn/N)$.
- 🔑 **Always include the hypothesis:** "for real $x(n)$" or "for real-even $x(n)$" — never state a property unconditionally.
- 🔑 **KTU Module 1 Weightage:** Symmetry properties typically contribute **2–3 questions** per exam cycle, worth **15–20 marks** out of 100.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simple FIR Digital Filters (Low Pass and High Pass)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST526 - DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Simple FIR Digital Filters (Low Pass and High Pass)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of an FIR Digital Filter

A **Finite Impulse Response (FIR) Digital Filter** is a discrete-time linear shift-invariant (LSI) system whose output is a weighted, finite sum of the present and a finite number of past input samples. The filter is called "finite" because the impulse response settles to exactly zero in a finite number of samples — there is no recursive feedback path.

The general time-domain **difference equation** of an FIR filter of order $N$ is:

$$y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{N} h(k)\,x(n-k) = h(0)x(n) + h(1)x(n-1) + h(2)x(n-2) + \cdots + h(N)x(n-N)$$

Here, the constants $h(0), h(1), h(2), \dots, h(N)$ are called the **filter coefficients** (or *taps*) and they collectively form the **impulse response** $h(n)$ of the system. The corresponding **transfer function** is obtained by taking the $z$-transform:

$$H(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{N} h(k)\,z^{-k} = h(0) + h(1)z^{-1} + h(2)z^{-2} + \cdots + h(N)z^{-N}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Structural Property of FIR Filters:** Because $H(z)$ is a polynomial in $z^{-1}$ (no positive powers of $z$, no feedback term), an FIR filter has **no poles** in the finite $z$-plane (all poles are at the origin). This guarantees **absolute BIBO (Bounded-Input Bounded-Output) stability** under *any* choice of coefficients.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A "Simple" FIR filter in this module is one whose coefficients can be obtained analytically by sampling the inverse Fourier transform of an **ideal magnitude specification** (low-pass or high-pass), then truncating it with a simple window such as the **rectangular window** — i.e., the *Fourier-series / window-method* design approach.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **water tap fitted with a multi-layered sieve**:

- The *incoming muddy water* is the input signal $x(n)$.
- Each sieve layer (with its own mesh fineness and position) represents a coefficient $h(k)$: thicker, coarser sieves near the front let through most of the water, while progressively finer sieves smooth out the particles.
- The *water dripping out the other side* is the output $y(n)$ — a cleaned, smoothed version of the input.

The crucial idea is that the sieve has **no recycling pipe** — water that has passed through a layer never returns to an earlier layer. That is exactly the "no feedback" property of an FIR filter. Also, the **number of sieve layers** is finite (only 7 sieves? only 50? — you choose $N+1$), which is why the response of the system to a sudden pulse (impulse) eventually stops and becomes exactly zero.

A *low-pass* sieve lets slow, large ripples pass and blocks fast ripples; a *high-pass* sieve does the opposite. The job of FIR filter *design* is simply to decide the right "mesh-sizes" $h(0), h(1), \dots, h(N)$ that produce the desired pass-band and stop-band behaviour.

## 1.3 Classification by Frequency Selectivity

| Filter Type | Function | Ideal Magnitude $\vert H_d(e^{j\omega}) \vert$ |
|---|---|---|
| **Low-Pass Filter (LPF)** | Passes low frequencies, blocks high frequencies | $\begin{cases} 1, & \vert\omega\vert \le \omega_c \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$ |
| **High-Pass Filter (HPF)** | Passes high frequencies, blocks low frequencies | $\begin{cases} 0, & \vert\omega\vert < \omega_c \\ 1, & \omega_c \le \vert\omega\vert \le \pi \end{cases}$ |

where $\omega_c$ is the **digital cutoff frequency** in rad/sample, satisfying $0 < \omega_c < \pi$. The **linear phase** property that we will obtain for a symmetric $h(n)$ gives every frequency component the *same time delay* — the output is just a delayed, undistorted copy of the filtered waveform.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Simple FIR Filter Design

The window-based design of a simple FIR filter proceeds in three systematic steps. We must understand the *why* behind each before applying the formulas.

### Step 1 — Specify the *Ideal* Frequency Response
The ideal filter is non-causal, has infinite length, and has a discontinuous (brick-wall) magnitude. We only need to write down its **ideal impulse response** $h_d(n)$ by taking the inverse Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT) of the ideal magnitude specification.

For an **ideal LPF** with cutoff $\omega_c$:

$$h_d(n) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\omega_c}^{+\omega_c} (1)\cdot e^{j\omega n}\,d\omega = \frac{\sin(\omega_c n)}{\pi n}, \quad n \neq 0$$

$$h_d(0) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\omega_c}^{+\omega_c} d\omega = \frac{\omega_c}{\pi}$$

> **Why?** Because the inverse DTFT of a rectangular pulse of width $2\omega_c$ and height $1$ is a *sinc* function in $n$. This $h_d(n)$ is symmetric, non-causal, and infinitely long.

### Step 2 — Truncate and Shift to Make the Filter Causal
Multiply $h_d(n)$ by a **rectangular window** $w_R(n)$ of length $L = N+1$ to obtain a finite-length sequence, and then **right-shift** the result by $M = N/2$ samples (or $\lfloor N/2 \rfloor$) to make it causal:

$$h(n) = h_d(n - M)\cdot w_R(n), \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \dots, N$$

Explicitly:

$$h(n) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{\sin\!\big(\omega_c (n - M)\big)}{\pi (n - M)}, & n \neq M \\[6pt] \dfrac{\omega_c}{\pi}, & n = M \end{cases}$$

where $M = N/2$ for an odd-length (Type-I) symmetric filter.

> **Why truncate?** A non-causal filter cannot run in real time. Truncation makes it *realizable* at the cost of introducing the well-known **Gibbs phenomenon** (ripples in the pass-band and stop-band) caused by the abrupt edges of the rectangular window.

### Step 3 — Exploit Symmetry for Linear Phase
Because $h_d(n)$ is an *even* function of $n$ (i.e. $h_d(n) = h_d(-n)$), the truncated sequence satisfies the **symmetry condition**:

$$h(n) = h(N - n), \quad n = 0, 1, \dots, N$$

This symmetry is what makes the filter have **exact linear phase** in the pass-band. The output is a pure delayed copy of the input — no phase distortion.

## 2.2 Magnitude and Phase Response of a Symmetric FIR Filter

For a symmetric FIR filter of length $L = N+1$ with midpoint $M = N/2$, the frequency response is:

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j\omega M}\Big[\,h(M) + 2\sum_{k=0}^{M-1} h(k)\cos\big(\omega(M-k)\big)\Big]$$

Therefore the **magnitude** and **phase** are:

$$|H(e^{j\omega})| = \left|\,h(M) + 2\sum_{k=0}^{M-1} h(k)\cos\big(\omega(M-k)\big)\right|$$

$$\angle H(e^{j\omega}) = -\omega M + \angle\Big(\,\cdot\,\Big)$$

For the case where the bracketed term is real and positive (which it is across most of the pass-band for a properly designed LPF), the phase is purely:

$$\theta(\omega) = -\omega M \quad \text{(linear phase, constant group delay } \tau_g = M)$$

## 2.3 Spectral-Inversion Trick: LPF → HPF

A **high-pass FIR filter** with the same cutoff $\omega_c$ and the same length $L = N+1$ can be obtained from the low-pass filter by the elegant **spectral-inversion identity**:

$$h_{HP}(n) = \delta(n - M) - h_{LP}(n), \quad n = 0, 1, \dots, N$$

where $\delta(n-M)$ is the unit impulse located at sample $M$ (value $1$ at $n=M$ and $0$ elsewhere).

> **Why does this work?** Multiplying the LPF by $-1$ in the frequency domain (i.e., $1 \to -1$) flips the spectrum about the $\omega$-axis. Adding $\delta(n-M)$ (which has a flat spectrum of magnitude $1$ and linear phase $-j\omega M$) shifts the flipped spectrum up so the pass-band of the HPF ends up at magnitude $1$. The result is a high-pass shape that retains the linear-phase property.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Remarks / KTU Pitfalls |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | FIR difference equation | $y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{N} h(k)\,x(n-k)$ | Order $N$ ⇒ $N+1$ coefficients |
| 2 | Transfer function | $H(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{N} h(k)\,z^{-k}$ | No denominator ⇒ no finite poles |
| 3 | Symmetry condition (linear phase) | $h(n) = h(N - n)$ | Always holds for the "simple" filters here |
| 4 | Mid-index | $M = N/2$ | Use *integer* $M$; for even-length, $M$ is half-integer |
| 5 | Ideal LPF impulse (continuous form) | $h_d(n) = \dfrac{\sin(\omega_c n)}{\pi n},\; n \ne 0$ | Obtained by inverse DTFT of a rectangle |
| 6 | Value at the centre sample | $h_d(0) = \dfrac{\omega_c}{\pi}$ | Often missed — KTU gives 1 mark for this |
| 7 | Practical LPF coefficient | $h(n) = \dfrac{\sin\!\big(\omega_c(n-M)\big)}{\pi(n-M)},\; n \ne M$ | For $n=0,1,\dots,N$ with $n \ne M$ |
| 8 | Centre coefficient of LPF | $h(M) = \dfrac{\omega_c}{\pi}$ | Equals $h_d(0)$ |
| 9 | LPF → HPF conversion | $h_{HP}(n) = \delta(n-M) - h_{LP}(n)$ | Apply for *every* $n$ in $0,1,\dots,N$ |
| 10 | Linear-phase response | $\theta(\omega) = -\omega M$ | Slope gives **group delay** $\tau_g = M$ samples |
| 11 | Magnitude response | $H(\omega) = h(M) + 2\sum_{k=0}^{M-1} h(k)\cos\big(\omega(M-k)\big)$ | Bracketed factor is the *real* envelope |
| 12 | Pass-band edge constraint | $0 < \omega_c < \pi$ | In rad/sample; $\omega_c = \pi$ ⇒ no stop-band |
| 13 | Stability check | $\sum_{n=0}^{N} \vert h(n) \vert < \infty$ | Always true for finite $N$ |
| 14 | Group delay | $\tau_g = -\dfrac{d\theta(\omega)}{d\omega} = M$ | Symmetric $h(n) \Rightarrow$ constant $\tau_g$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** Simple FIR LPF/HPF filters are used in **digital audio equalizers**, **ECG / EEG biomedical signal denoising** (removing $50$ Hz mains hum with an HPF), **radar/sonar pulse shaping**, **anti-aliasing** in data-acquisition systems, and as **decimation / interpolation** half-band filters in multirate DSP.

## 2.5 Why "Simple" Specifically Means Window-Method (Fourier-Series) Design

In the KTU 2024 scheme, the design tools prescribed at this stage are deliberately limited to the **inverse-DTFT + rectangular-window** method because:

1. It produces *closed-form* analytic expressions — easy to write in an exam.
2. It reveals the direct link between the *cutoff frequency* and the *sinc* impulse response.
3. The student can later upgrade the design by swapping the rectangular window for a Hamming, Hanning, or Blackman window to reduce Gibbs ripples.

A *frequency-sampling* method or *Parks–McClellan optimal* method is a higher-semester topic and is *not* expected here.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Ideal LPF Impulse Response

We want an ideal low-pass filter whose magnitude response is:

$$H_d(e^{j\omega}) = \begin{cases} 1, & -\omega_c \le \omega \le \omega_c \\ 0, & \omega_c < \vert\omega\vert \le \pi \end{cases}$$

with **zero phase** (assumed for the ideal case). By the definition of inverse DTFT:

$$h_d(n) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{+\pi} H_d(e^{j\omega})\,e^{j\omega n}\,d\omega$$

Since $H_d$ is non-zero only on $[-\omega_c, \omega_c]$:

$$h_d(n) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\omega_c}^{+\omega_c} (1)\,e^{j\omega n}\,d\omega$$

For $n \neq 0$, integrate:

$$h_d(n) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\left[\frac{e^{j\omega n}}{jn}\right]_{-\omega_c}^{+\omega_c} = \frac{1}{2\pi jn}\Big(e^{j\omega_c n} - e^{-j\omega_c n}\Big) = \frac{\sin(\omega_c n)}{\pi n}$$

For $n = 0$, the integral becomes:

$$h_d(0) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\omega_c}^{+\omega_c} d\omega = \frac{2\omega_c}{2\pi} = \frac{\omega_c}{\pi}$$

Note that $\displaystyle \lim_{n \to 0}\frac{\sin(\omega_c n)}{\pi n} = \frac{\omega_c}{\pi}$ by L'Hôpital's rule, so the two expressions agree at $n=0$.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Magnitude/Phase Response of a Symmetric FIR Filter

Take a symmetric FIR filter of length $L = N+1$ with $M = N/2$ (assume $N$ even for the cleanest derivation). Then $h(k) = h(N-k)$ for every $k$, and:

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{k=0}^{N} h(k)\,e^{-j\omega k}$$

Split the sum into three pieces — the centre term ($k=M$), the "left" half ($k<M$), and the "right" half ($k>M$). Using $k' = N - k$ in the right half so that when $k$ goes from $M+1$ to $N$, $k'$ goes from $M-1$ down to $0$:

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = h(M)e^{-j\omega M} + \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} h(k)\,e^{-j\omega k} + \sum_{k'=0}^{M-1} h(k')\,e^{-j\omega(N - k')}$$

Since $h(k) = h(N-k)$ and $N = 2M$:

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j\omega M}\left[\,h(M) + \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} h(k)\,e^{j\omega(M-k)} + \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} h(k)\,e^{-j\omega(M-k)}\right]$$

Combine the two sums using the identity $e^{j\theta} + e^{-j\theta} = 2\cos\theta$:

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j\omega M}\left[\,h(M) + 2\sum_{k=0}^{M-1} h(k)\cos\big(\omega(M-k)\big)\right]$$

The term in square brackets is **purely real** (sum of real numbers). Therefore:

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j\omega M} \cdot H_r(\omega), \quad \text{where } H_r(\omega) = h(M) + 2\sum_{k=0}^{M-1} h(k)\cos\big(\omega(M-k)\big)$$

The phase is therefore:

$$\theta(\omega) = \begin{cases} -\omega M, & H_r(\omega) \ge 0 \\ -\omega M + \pi, & H_r(\omega) < 0 \end{cases}$$

Across the pass-band, $H_r(\omega)$ is positive, so $\theta(\omega) = -\omega M$ — **exact linear phase**. The group delay is the constant:

$$\tau_g = -\frac{d\theta}{d\omega} = M \text{ samples}$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Problem)

**Problem (KTU-style):** Design a *simple* low-pass FIR digital filter using a rectangular window with cutoff frequency $\omega_c = \pi/4$ rad/sample and length $L = 7$ (i.e., $N = 6$, $M = 3$). Then:
- (a) Find all coefficients $h(0), h(1), \dots, h(6)$.
- (b) Derive the magnitude and phase response, and convert the LPF to an HPF with the same specifications.

### Part (a) — LPF Coefficients

Using the formula derived in §3.1, with $M = 3$ and $\omega_c = \pi/4$:

$$h(n) = \frac{\sin\!\big(\frac{\pi}{4}(n-3)\big)}{\pi(n-3)}, \quad n \neq 3; \quad h(3) = \frac{\omega_c}{\pi} = \frac{1}{4}$$

Compute each coefficient explicitly:

$$h(0) = \frac{\sin\!\big(\frac{\pi}{4}(0-3)\big)}{\pi(0-3)} = \frac{\sin(-3\pi/4)}{-3\pi} = \frac{-\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}}{-3\pi} = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{6\pi} \approx 0.0750$$

$$h(1) = \frac{\sin\!\big(\frac{\pi}{4}(1-3)\big)}{\pi(1-3)} = \frac{\sin(-\pi/2)}{-2\pi} = \frac{-1}{-2\pi} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \approx 0.1592$$

$$h(2) = \frac{\sin\!\big(\frac{\pi}{4}(2-3)\big)}{\pi(2-3)} = \frac{\sin(-\pi/4)}{-\pi} = \frac{-\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}}{-\pi} = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2\pi} \approx 0.2251$$

$$h(3) = \frac{1}{4} = 0.2500$$

By symmetry $h(n) = h(6-n)$:

$$h(4) = h(2) \approx 0.2251, \quad h(5) = h(1) \approx 0.1592, \quad h(6) = h(0) \approx 0.0750$$

**Tabulated result:**

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
n & 0 & 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 & 5 & 6 \\
\hline
h(n) & 0.0750 & 0.1592 & 0.2251 & 0.2500 & 0.2251 & 0.1592 & 0.0750 \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity check — Symmetry:** $h(0) = h(6)$, $h(1) = h(5)$, $h(2) = h(4)$ — the symmetry condition is satisfied, so the filter *will* have linear phase. KTU examiners award a free mark for stating this explicitly.

### Part (b) — Magnitude, Phase, and HPF Conversion

**Magnitude and phase response.** Using the formula of §2.2 with $M = 3$:

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j3\omega}\Big[\,h(3) + 2h(2)\cos(\omega) + 2h(1)\cos(2\omega) + 2h(0)\cos(3\omega)\Big]$$

Substituting numerical values:

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j3\omega}\Big[\,0.25 + 0.4502\cos(\omega) + 0.3183\cos(2\omega) + 0.1500\cos(3\omega)\Big]$$

The bracketed term is the **real magnitude envelope** $H_r(\omega)$. The phase is therefore:

$$\theta(\omega) = -3\omega \quad \text{(for the pass-band where } H_r(\omega) > 0\text{)}$$

The **group delay** is $\tau_g = 3$ samples — every frequency is delayed by exactly $3$ clock cycles.

**Verification of pass-band behaviour at $\omega = 0$:**

$$H_r(0) = 0.25 + 0.4502 + 0.3183 + 0.1500 = 1.1685$$

So $\vert H(e^{j0})\vert \approx 1.17$, close to the ideal value $1$ (the small excess is the well-known **Gibbs overshoot** caused by the rectangular window).

**HPF coefficients by spectral inversion.** Apply $h_{HP}(n) = \delta(n-3) - h_{LP}(n)$:

$$h_{HP}(n) = \begin{cases} -h_{LP}(n), & n \ne 3 \\ 1 - h_{LP}(3), & n = 3 \end{cases}$$

So:

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
n & 0 & 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 & 5 & 6 \\
\hline
h_{HP}(n) & -0.0750 & -0.1592 & -0.2251 & +0.7500 & -0.2251 & -0.1592 & -0.0750 \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

**Verification of HPF pass-band at $\omega = \pi$:**

$$H_{HP,r}(\pi) = 0.75 - 0.4502 - 0.3183 - 0.1500 = -0.1685$$

Taking the absolute magnitude: $\vert H_{HP}(e^{j\pi})\vert \approx 0.17$ — small, as expected near the Nyquist edge. The HPF still has linear phase $\theta(\omega) = -3\omega$ and group delay $3$ samples.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam shortcut:** After converting LPF → HPF, you can quickly *check* the result by confirming that $H_{HP,r}(\pi) = h_{HP}(3) - 2h_{HP}(2) + 2h_{HP}(1) - 2h_{HP}(0) = 0.75 - 0.4502 + 0.3183 - 0.1500 = 0.4681$. Wait — that gives a different value, because the cosines at $\omega=\pi$ alternate in sign: $\cos(\pi)=-1$, $\cos(2\pi)=+1$, $\cos(3\pi)=-1$. Always substitute carefully!

## 3.4 General-Order Formulas (Quick Reference for Any Length)

For an $L$-tap LPF with $L = N+1$, $M = N/2$:

$$h(k) = \frac{\sin\!\big(\omega_c(k - M)\big)}{\pi(k - M)}, \quad k = 0, 1, \dots, N$$

$$h(M) = \frac{\omega_c}{\pi}$$

$$\theta(\omega) = -\omega M$$

The HPF conversion is:

$$h_{HP}(k) = \delta(k - M) - h_{LP}(k), \quad k = 0, 1, \dots, N$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Direct-Form Realization of a 7-Tap FIR Filter

The block diagram of a generic 7-tap FIR LPF (as derived in §3.3) is shown below. The chain of delay elements $z^{-1}$ is tapped at every node; each tap is multiplied by the corresponding coefficient $h(k)$; all products are summed to produce the output $y(n)$. **This is a non-recursive (no feedback) structure.**

```mermaid
graph LR
    inp[Input xn] --> d0[D1 z-1]
    d0 --> d1[D2 z-1]
    d1 --> d2[D3 z-1]
    d2 --> d3[D4 z-1]
    d3 --> d4[D5 z-1]
    d4 --> d5[D6 z-1]

    inp --> m0[Multiply h0 0.0750]
    d0 --> m1[Multiply h1 0.1592]
    d1 --> m2[Multiply h2 0.2251]
    d2 --> m3[Multiply h3 0.2500]
    d3 --> m4[Multiply h4 0.2251]
    d4 --> m5[Multiply h5 0.1592]
    d5 --> m6[Multiply h6 0.0750]

    m0 --> sumN[SUMMER]
    m1 --> sumN
    m2 --> sumN
    m3 --> sumN
    m4 --> sumN
    m5 --> sumN
    m6 --> sumN

    sumN --> outp[Output yn]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of a Window-Method FIR Design Pipeline

The following flowchart captures the *design process* (not the filter itself) — useful for the KTU "design" questions.

```mermaid
graph TD
    specA[Specify wc and length L] --> idStep[Compute ideal impulse response hd n using inverse DTFT]
    idStep --> winStep[Multiply by rectangular window wR n of length L]
    winStep --> shiftStep[Right-shift by M N slash 2 samples to make causal]
    shiftStep --> chkSym{Check h n equals h N minus n?}
    chkSym -->|Yes| lpfOut[LPF coefficients hLP n]
    chkSym -->|No| fixStep[Re-symmetrize coefficients]
    fixStep --> lpfOut
    lpfOut --> magStep[Derive magnitude and linear phase response]
    lpfOut --> convStep[Apply spectral inversion hHP n equals delta n minus M minus hLP n]
    convStep --> hpfOut[HPF coefficients hHP n]
    magStep --> finalOut[Final Filter Ready for Implementation]
    hpfOut --> finalOut
```

## 4.3 Frequency-Response Behaviour (Schematic)

A pictographic description of the magnitude response of the LPF and the HPF obtained by spectral inversion. The "rectangular window" envelope is shown as a dashed ideal brick-wall, while the realised response is shown as a solid curve with Gibbs ripples.

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph LPF_SHAPE
        A1[omega equals 0] --> A2[Pass-band flat near magnitude 1]
        A2 --> A3[Transition band near omega equals omega c]
        A3 --> A4[Stop-band ripples near magnitude 0]
    end
    subgraph HPF_SHAPE
        B1[omega equals 0] --> B2[Stop-band near magnitude 0]
        B2 --> B3[Transition band near omega equals omega c]
        B3 --> B4[Pass-band flat near magnitude 1 up to omega equals pi]
    end
    LPF_SHAPE -.->|Spectral Inversion| HPF_SHAPE
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Insight:** The realised response is *not* a perfect brick-wall because we used a finite-length rectangular window. Increasing $L$ (i.e., using a longer filter) makes the transition band narrower and pushes the ripples closer to the discontinuity, but it never *eliminates* the ripples — that is the famous **Gibbs phenomenon** of Fourier series.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**State the difference equation of a simple FIR digital filter of order $N$. Why is an FIR filter always stable? Mention any two advantages of FIR filters over IIR filters.**

**Model Answer:**

The difference equation of an FIR filter of order $N$ is:

$$y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{N} h(k)\,x(n-k)$$

**Reason for guaranteed stability:** An FIR filter has *no feedback path*, hence the transfer function $H(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{N} h(k)z^{-k}$ contains *no finite poles*. All its poles are located at the origin of the $z$-plane, which lies strictly inside the unit circle, so the system is **always BIBO stable** regardless of the coefficient values.

**Two advantages over IIR filters:**
1. **Linear phase** is exactly achievable when $h(n)$ is symmetric or antisymmetric, making FIR filters ideal for applications requiring no phase distortion (audio, biomedical, data transmission).
2. **No stability problem during coefficient quantisation** — IIR filters may become unstable when their coefficients are rounded for fixed-point implementation, whereas FIR filters remain stable.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**For a symmetric FIR filter of length $L = 7$ with $h(n) = \{0.0750,\;0.1592,\;0.2251,\;0.2500,\;0.2251,\;0.1592,\;0.0750\}$, determine the group delay. Justify your answer.**

**Model Answer:**

For a symmetric FIR filter of length $L = N+1 = 7$, the order is $N = 6$ and the midpoint index is $M = N/2 = 3$.

The frequency response of a symmetric FIR filter is:

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j\omega M}\,H_r(\omega)$$

where $H_r(\omega)$ is a real-valued function. The phase is therefore $\theta(\omega) = -\omega M$ (in the pass-band where $H_r > 0$), and the **group delay** is:

$$\tau_g = -\frac{d\theta(\omega)}{d\omega} = M = 3 \text{ samples}$$

**Justification:** The linear-phase term $e^{-j3\omega}$ implies that every sinusoidal component of the input is delayed by exactly $3$ clock cycles at the output. This is a direct consequence of the symmetry $h(n) = h(6-n)$ of the given coefficients.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks  [KTU University Exam — Model Question]
**(a)** Design a simple low-pass FIR digital filter using the **rectangular window** technique with cutoff frequency $\omega_c = \pi/3$ rad/sample and filter length $L = 7$ (i.e. $N = 6$, $M = 3$). Find the filter coefficients $h(0), h(1), \dots, h(6)$ and verify the symmetry condition. (7 marks)

**(b)** Obtain the **magnitude response** $|H(e^{j\omega})|$ and the **phase response** $\theta(\omega)$ of the designed filter. What is the **group delay**? Comment on the linear-phase property. (7 marks)

---

#### Solution to Q-A (a)

Given $\omega_c = \pi/3$ and $L = 7$ ⇒ $N = 6$, $M = 3$.

The general coefficient formula (from §2.1) is:

$$h(n) = \frac{\sin\!\big(\omega_c(n - M)\big)}{\pi(n - M)}, \quad n \ne M; \qquad h(M) = \frac{\omega_c}{\pi}$$

**Substitute $M = 3$ and $\omega_c = \pi/3$:**

$$h(n) = \frac{\sin\!\big(\frac{\pi}{3}(n - 3)\big)}{\pi(n - 3)}, \quad n \ne 3; \qquad h(3) = \frac{1}{3}$$

**Compute each coefficient:**

$$h(0) = \frac{\sin\!\big(\frac{\pi}{3}(0-3)\big)}{\pi(0-3)} = \frac{\sin(-\pi)}{-3\pi} = 0$$

$$h(1) = \frac{\sin\!\big(\frac{\pi}{3}(1-3)\big)}{\pi(1-3)} = \frac{\sin(-2\pi/3)}{-2\pi} = \frac{-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}}{-2\pi} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{4\pi} \approx 0.1378$$

$$h(2) = \frac{\sin\!\big(\frac{\pi}{3}(2-3)\big)}{\pi(2-3)} = \frac{\sin(-\pi/3)}{-\pi} = \frac{-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}}{-\pi} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2\pi} \approx 0.2757$$

$$h(3) = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.3333$$

**Symmetry gives the remaining three:**

$$h(4) = h(2) \approx 0.2757, \quad h(5) = h(1) \approx 0.1378, \quad h(6) = h(0) = 0$$

**Tabulated result:**

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
n & 0 & 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 & 5 & 6 \\
\hline
h(n) & 0 & 0.1378 & 0.2757 & 0.3333 & 0.2757 & 0.1378 & 0 \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

**Symmetry check:** $h(0) = h(6) = 0$; $h(1) = h(5) = 0.1378$; $h(2) = h(4) = 0.2757$. ✓ *Symmetry confirmed → the filter has linear phase.*

---

#### Solution to Q-A (b)

Substitute the coefficients into the symmetric-FIR magnitude formula (from §2.2):

$$H_r(\omega) = h(3) + 2h(2)\cos(\omega) + 2h(1)\cos(2\omega) + 2h(0)\cos(3\omega)$$

$$H_r(\omega) = 0.3333 + 0.5513\cos(\omega) + 0.2757\cos(2\omega) + 0\cdot\cos(3\omega)$$

$$H_r(\omega) = 0.3333 + 0.5513\cos(\omega) + 0.2757\cos(2\omega)$$

Hence:

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j3\omega}\big[\,0.3333 + 0.5513\cos(\omega) + 0.2757\cos(2\omega)\big]$$

**Magnitude response:**

$$|H(e^{j\omega})| = \big|\,0.3333 + 0.5513\cos(\omega) + 0.2757\cos(2\omega)\,\big|$$

**Phase response (pass-band region where $H_r > 0$):**

$$\theta(\omega) = -3\omega \text{ rad}$$

**Group delay:**

$$\tau_g = -\frac{d\theta(\omega)}{d\omega} = 3 \text{ samples}$$

**Comment on linear-phase property:** Since $h(n)$ satisfies the symmetry condition $h(n) = h(6-n)$, the filter is a **Type-I linear-phase FIR filter**. The constant group delay of $3$ samples means that all frequency components in the input are delayed by exactly the same amount; therefore, the output is an undistorted, time-shifted version of the filtered input — there is **no phase distortion**.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks  [Alternative Choice]
**(a)** Explain the *window-method* (Fourier-series) design technique for a simple FIR low-pass filter. Derive the closed-form expression for the impulse response $h(n)$ for a length-$L$ filter with cutoff $\omega_c$ and midpoint $M = N/2$. (7 marks)

**(b)** Using the result of part (a), **obtain the impulse response of a high-pass FIR filter** of length $L = 5$ with cutoff $\omega_c = \pi/4$ by spectral inversion. Find the magnitude response at $\omega = 0$ and $\omega = \pi$ and hence identify the pass-band and stop-band. (7 marks)

---

#### Solution to Q-B (a)

**Window-method design — explanation:**

In the window-method (also called the *Fourier-series* method), the design of an FIR filter proceeds in three steps:

1. **Specify the ideal (brick-wall) frequency response** $H_d(e^{j\omega})$ of the desired filter, with the desired magnitude and *zero* phase.
2. **Take the inverse Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT)** of $H_d(e^{j\omega})$ to obtain the *ideal* impulse response $h_d(n)$. This $h_d(n)$ is generally non-causal and infinitely long.
3. **Truncate $h_d(n)$ to a finite length $L = N+1$** by multiplying it with a finite-length window $w(n)$ — in this module, the **rectangular window** $w_R(n) = 1$ for $0 \le n \le N$, zero otherwise. Then **right-shift** the truncated sequence by $M = N/2$ samples to make it causal.

**Derivation of $h(n)$ for an LPF with cutoff $\omega_c$:**

The ideal LPF magnitude (with zero phase) is:

$$H_d(e^{j\omega}) = \begin{cases} 1, & -\omega_c \le \omega \le \omega_c \\ 0, & \omega_c < \vert\omega\vert \le \pi \end{cases}$$

Taking the inverse DTFT:

$$h_d(n) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi} H_d(e^{j\omega})\,e^{j\omega n}\,d\omega = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\omega_c}^{\omega_c} e^{j\omega n}\,d\omega$$

For $n \ne 0$:

$$h_d(n) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\left[\frac{e^{j\omega n}}{jn}\right]_{-\omega_c}^{\omega_c} = \frac{e^{j\omega_c n} - e^{-j\omega_c n}}{2\pi jn} = \frac{\sin(\omega_c n)}{\pi n}$$

For $n = 0$:

$$h_d(0) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\omega_c}^{\omega_c} d\omega = \frac{\omega_c}{\pi}$$

Apply the rectangular window of length $L = N+1$ and shift by $M = N/2$ to get a *causal* filter:

$$h(n) = h_d(n - M)\,w_R(n) = \frac{\sin\!\big(\omega_c(n - M)\big)}{\pi(n - M)}, \quad 0 \le n \le N,\; n \ne M$$

and $h(M) = \omega_c/\pi$ at the centre sample.

**Symmetry:** Because $\sin(-x) = -\sin(x)$ but $\sin(x)/\pi x$ is even in $x$, the coefficients $h(n)$ satisfy $h(n) = h(N - n)$, which guarantees linear phase.

---

#### Solution to Q-B (b)

Given $\omega_c = \pi/4$ and $L = 5$ ⇒ $N = 4$, $M = 2$.

**Step 1 — LPF coefficients:**

$$h_{LP}(n) = \frac{\sin\!\big(\frac{\pi}{4}(n-2)\big)}{\pi(n-2)}, \quad n = 0, 1, 3, 4; \quad h_{LP}(2) = \frac{\pi/4}{\pi} = \frac{1}{4}$$

Compute each:

$$h_{LP}(0) = \frac{\sin(-\pi/2)}{-2\pi} = \frac{-1}{-2\pi} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \approx 0.1592$$

$$h_{LP}(1) = \frac{\sin(-\pi/4)}{-\pi} = \frac{-\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}}{-\pi} = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2\pi} \approx 0.2251$$

$$h_{LP}(2) = 0.25, \quad h_{LP}(3) = h_{LP}(1) \approx 0.2251, \quad h_{LP}(4) = h_{LP}(0) \approx 0.1592$$

**Step 2 — HPF coefficients by spectral inversion:** $h_{HP}(n) = \delta(n - 2) - h_{LP}(n)$:

$$h_{HP}(0) = -0.1592, \quad h_{HP}(1) = -0.2251, \quad h_{HP}(2) = 1 - 0.25 = 0.75$$

$$h_{HP}(3) = -0.2251, \quad h_{HP}(4) = -0.1592$$

**Step 3 — Magnitude response at $\omega = 0$ and $\omega = \pi$:**

For a symmetric FIR of length $5$ with $M = 2$:

$$H_{HP}(\omega) = e^{-j2\omega}\big[\,h_{HP}(2) + 2h_{HP}(1)\cos(\omega) + 2h_{HP}(0)\cos(2\omega)\,\big]$$

At $\omega = 0$: $\cos(0) = 1$, $\cos(0) = 1$:

$$H_{HP,r}(0) = 0.75 + 2(-0.2251)(1) + 2(-0.1592)(1) = 0.75 - 0.4502 - 0.3183 = -0.0185$$

So $\vert H_{HP}(e^{j0})\vert \approx 0.019$ — *very small* (good, since the HPF should suppress DC).

At $\omega = \pi$: $\cos(\pi) = -1$, $\cos(2\pi) = 1$:

$$H_{HP,r}(\pi) = 0.75 + 2(-0.2251)(-1) + 2(-0.1592)(1) = 0.75 + 0.4502 - 0.3183 = 0.8819$$

So $\vert H_{HP}(e^{j\pi})\vert \approx 0.882$ — *large* (good, since the HPF should pass high frequencies).

**Identification of bands:**
- **Pass-band:** $\omega \approx \pi$ (and the range $\omega_c < \omega \le \pi$ where magnitude is close to $1$).
- **Stop-band:** $\omega \approx 0$ (and the range $0 \le \omega < \omega_c$ where magnitude is close to $0$).
- The cutoff frequency is approximately at $\omega_c = \pi/4$, as designed.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Deduction Points:**
> 1. **Forgetting $h(M) = \omega_c/\pi$** — students plug $n = M$ into the general $\frac{\sin(\omega_c(n-M))}{\pi(n-M)}$ formula and get $0/0$. KTU awards a dedicated mark for explicitly writing $h(M) = \omega_c/\pi$. (Lose 1 mark.)
> 2. **Missing the symmetry check** — KTU gives 1 mark for verifying $h(n) = h(N-n)$. Skipping this loses a free mark.
> 3. **Forgetting to right-shift by $M$** — the *truncated but un-shifted* sequence is non-causal and cannot be implemented. Always state the shift explicitly. (Lose 2 marks.)
> 4. **Mixing up LPF → HPF sign** — the formula is $h_{HP}(n) = \delta(n-M) - h_{LP}(n)$, *not* $h_{HP}(n) = h_{LP}(n) - \delta(n-M)$ and *not* $h_{HP}(n) = \delta(n-M) + h_{LP}(n)$. (Lose 2–3 marks.)
> 5. **Reporting the phase as "$-\omega M + \pi$" everywhere** — the $\pi$ phase-jump occurs only in the stop-band where $H_r(\omega) < 0$. In the pass-band, the phase is purely $-\omega M$. (Lose 1 mark.)
> 6. **Forgetting to label the units of $\omega_c$** as "rad/sample" — KTU deducts half a mark for missing units.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **FIR filter difference equation:** $y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{N} h(k)\,x(n-k)$ — *no feedback* term.
- **Transfer function:** $H(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{N} h(k) z^{-k}$ — *no finite poles* ⇒ unconditionally stable.
- **Symmetry condition (linear phase):** $h(n) = h(N-n)$ for a Type-I (odd-length, symmetric) filter.
- **Midpoint index:** $M = N/2$ (integer when $N$ is even).
- **Ideal LPF impulse (continuous-time sinc):** $h_d(n) = \sin(\omega_c n)/(\pi n)$ for $n \ne 0$; $h_d(0) = \omega_c/\pi$.
- **Practical LPF coefficients:** $h(n) = \sin(\omega_c(n-M))/[\pi(n-M)]$, with $h(M) = \omega_c/\pi$.
- **Magnitude response (symmetric FIR):** $H_r(\omega) = h(M) + 2\sum_{k=0}^{M-1} h(k)\cos(\omega(M-k))$.
- **Phase response:** $\theta(\omega) = -\omega M$ across the pass-band — **linear phase**.
- **Group delay:** $\tau_g = M$ samples (a *constant* for symmetric FIR).
- **LPF → HPF conversion:** $h_{HP}(n) = \delta(n-M) - h_{LP}(n)$ — *spectral inversion*.
- **Design pipeline:** specify $\omega_c$ & $L$ → inverse DTFT → rectangular window → right-shift by $M$ → verify symmetry → (optional) spectral inversion to get HPF.
- **Practical limitation:** rectangular window causes **Gibbs ripples** in both pass-band and stop-band; transition width is inversely proportional to $L$.
- **KTU exam hot-spots:** (i) writing $h(M) = \omega_c/\pi$ correctly, (ii) verifying symmetry, (iii) deriving the linear-phase formula, (iv) applying the spectral-inversion identity for the HPF.
- **Real-world applications:** digital audio equalization, biomedical signal denoising, anti-aliasing, multirate half-band filters, communication channel equalizers, audio crossover networks.
- **Numerical sanity check:** $\sum_{n=0}^{N} h_{LP}(n) \approx 1$ (DC gain of LPF is close to unity) and $\sum_{n=0}^{N} h_{HP}(n) \approx 0$ (DC gain of HPF is close to zero). Use this as a quick verification in the exam hall.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simple IIR Digital Filters (Low Pass and High Pass)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST526 - DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Simple IIR Digital Filters (Low Pass and High Pass)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an IIR Digital Filter?

An **Infinite Impulse Response (IIR) Digital Filter** is a discrete-time linear shift-invariant system whose impulse response extends over an *infinite* number of samples. Mathematically, its output depends not only on present and past input samples but also on past output samples, leading to a *recursive* (feedback) structure.

The general difference equation of an IIR filter is:

$$
y(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k \, y(n-k) + \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, x(n-k)
$$

where $a_k$ and $b_k$ are the feedback (recursive) and feed-forward coefficients, respectively.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** An IIR filter is one whose unit sample response has *infinite duration*. Because of the feedback term $\sum a_k y(n-k)$, the filter is recursive, and a finite set of coefficients produces an infinitely long impulse response.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — The Smart Thermostat:** Think of an IIR filter like a *smart room thermostat*:
> - The current room temperature ($y(n)$) depends on **today's set value** $x(n)$ (feed-forward) **and** the **previous room temperature** $y(n-1)$ (feedback).
> - Because the room never "forgets" its thermal inertia, its temperature response to a sudden change *lingers forever* (though it decays).
> - This memory effect is exactly what gives IIR filters their sharp frequency selectivity with very few coefficients — a big reason they are preferred for audio equalizers, biomedical ECG filters, and audio codecs.

## 1.3 Low Pass and High Pass — Quick Functional Picture

| Filter Type | Function | Passes | Attenuates |
|---|---|---|---|
| **LPF (Low Pass Filter)** | Allows low frequencies through | $0 \le \omega \le \omega_c$ | $\omega_c < \omega \le \pi$ |
| **HPF (High Pass Filter)** | Allows high frequencies through | $\omega_c \le \omega \le \pi$ | $0 \le \omega < \omega_c$ |

where $\omega_c$ is the **digital cutoff frequency** in radians/sample, and $\pi$ corresponds to the Nyquist frequency ($f_s/2$).

> [!NOTE]
> The design goal is to find the coefficients $\{a_k, b_k\}$ that realize the desired magnitude response. The most common KTU-approved method is **Bilinear Transformation of an Analog Butterworth Prototype**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Design Philosophy — The Two-Step Process

The IIR filter design is performed in **two distinct stages**:

### Step 1 — Analog Prototype Design
- Convert the *digital specifications* ($ \omega_p, \omega_s, \delta_p, \delta_s $) into *analog specifications* ($ \Omega_p, \Omega_s $) using an appropriate mapping rule.
- Design a stable analog filter $H_a(s)$ that meets these specifications. The KTU syllabus favours the **Butterworth** approximation for its maximally flat passband.

### Step 2 — Analog-to-Digital Conversion
- Apply the **Bilinear Transformation (BLT)** to convert $H_a(s)$ into $H(z)$.
- BLT guarantees a stable digital filter because it maps the *entire* left-half $s$-plane onto the interior of the unit circle in the $z$-plane.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Bilinear Transformation over Impulse Invariance?**
> - The BLT produces a **stable** digital filter from a stable analog prototype.
> - It **eliminates aliasing** of the magnitude response because it is a one-to-one mapping.
> - The only trade-off is **frequency warping**, which is corrected using *pre-warping* (explained below).

## 2.2 The Bilinear Transformation (BLT) — Core Mathematics

The BLT is defined by the conformal mapping:

$$
s = \frac{2}{T} \cdot \frac{z - 1}{z + 1}
$$

Equivalently, its inverse is:

$$
z = \frac{1 + (T/2)s}{1 - (T/2)s}
$$

### Frequency Mapping Rule

$$
\Omega = \frac{2}{T} \tan\!\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)
$$

This is a **non-linear** (tangential) compression of the analog frequency axis $[0, \infty)$ into the digital frequency range $[0, \pi]$. This is the famous *frequency warping* effect.

> [!NOTE]
> When $T = 2$ is chosen (a very common simplification in KTU textbook problems), the formula reduces to $s = (z-1)/(z+1)$ and $\Omega = \tan(\omega/2)$.

## 2.3 Pre-Warping Formula

To force the analog prototype to match the *exact* digital cutoff frequency $\omega_c$, we pre-warp the critical frequencies **before** designing the analog filter:

$$
\Omega_c = \frac{2}{T} \tan\!\left(\frac{\omega_c}{2}\right)
$$

$$
\Omega_p = \frac{2}{T} \tan\!\left(\frac{\omega_p}{2}\right)
$$

$$
\Omega_s = \frac{2}{T} \tan\!\left(\frac{\omega_s}{2}\right)
$$

## 2.4 Butterworth Analog Prototype

The magnitude-squared response of an $N$-th order Butterworth low pass filter is:

$$
\vert H_a(j\Omega) \vert^2 = \frac{1}{1 + \left(\dfrac{\Omega}{\Omega_c}\right)^{2N}}
$$

Key properties:
- **Maximally flat magnitude** at $\Omega = 0$ (first $2N-1$ derivatives are zero).
- At $\Omega = \Omega_c$: $\vert H_a(j\Omega_c) \vert^2 = \frac{1}{2}$, i.e., $-3\,\text{dB}$ point.
- All poles lie on a circle of radius $\Omega_c$ in the left-half $s$-plane, equally spaced.

## 2.5 Order of the Butterworth Filter

For specifications $( \Omega_p, \delta_p, \Omega_s, \delta_s )$, the required order is:

$$
N \ge \frac{\log_{10}\!\left[\dfrac{1/\delta_s^{2} - 1}{1/\delta_p^{2} - 1}\right]}{2 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\Omega_s}{\Omega_p}\right)}
$$

The corresponding $-3\,\text{dB}$ cutoff frequency is:

$$
\Omega_c = \frac{\Omega_p}{\left(2\delta_p^2 - 2\delta_p\right)^{1/(2N)}} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \Omega_c = \frac{\Omega_s}{\left(2\delta_s^2 - 2\delta_s\right)^{1/(2N)}}
$$

## 2.6 Frequency Transformation (LPF → HPF)

To convert a normalized low pass prototype $H_{LP}(s)$ with cutoff $1\,\text{rad/s}$ into a high pass filter with cutoff $\Omega_c$, apply the **low-pass to high-pass transformation**:

$$
s \;\longrightarrow\; \frac{\Omega_c \, s}{s}
$$

(That is, *replace every $s$ by $\Omega_c/s$ in the analog transfer function.*)

> [!NOTE]
> Equivalently, for digital filters after BLT, the LP→HP transformation can also be applied directly in the $z$-domain using $z^{-1} \to -z^{-1}$ combined with the inverse BLT.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — it covers ~80% of KTU IIR filter design problems.**

| # | Formula | Purpose / Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $s = \dfrac{2}{T}\cdot\dfrac{z-1}{z+1}$ | Bilinear Transformation (analog→digital) |
| 2 | $\Omega = \dfrac{2}{T}\tan\!\left(\dfrac{\omega}{2}\right)$ | Frequency mapping (warping relation) |
| 3 | $\Omega_c = \dfrac{2}{T}\tan\!\left(\dfrac{\omega_c}{2}\right)$ | Pre-warping the digital cutoff |
| 4 | $\vert H_a(j\Omega)\vert^2 = \dfrac{1}{1+(\Omega/\Omega_c)^{2N}}$ | Butterworth magnitude response |
| 5 | $N \ge \dfrac{\log_{10}[(1/\delta_s^2-1)/(1/\delta_p^2-1)]}{2\log_{10}(\Omega_s/\Omega_p)}$ | Required filter order |
| 6 | $H_{HP}(s) = H_{LP}(s)\big\vert_{s \to \Omega_c/s}$ | LP→HP analog transformation |
| 7 | $p_k = \Omega_c \, e^{j\pi(2k+N-1)/(2N)}$, $k=1,\dots,N$ | Butterworth pole locations (LHP only) |

**Boundary conditions & units:**
- $\omega, \omega_c, \omega_p, \omega_s$ in **radians/sample** (digital), range $[0, \pi]$.
- $\Omega, \Omega_c, \Omega_p, \Omega_s$ in **radians/second** (analog).
- $\delta_p$ = passband ripple; $\delta_s$ = stopband attenuation (linear, not in dB).
- $T$ = sampling period = $1/f_s$ in seconds.

## 2.8 Real-World Utility

IIR LP and HP filters are the workhorses of:
- **Audio processing** — bass/treble tone controls in music players.
- **Biomedical instrumentation** — removing baseline wander (HPF) and high-frequency EMG noise (LPF) from ECG signals.
- **Telecommunications** — channel filtering, anti-aliasing before A/D conversion.
- **Image processing** — Gaussian smoothing (LPF) and edge sharpening (HPF).
- **Control systems** — low-pass filtering of noisy sensor signals.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Frequency Warping Relation

The bilinear transformation substitutes $z = e^{j\omega}$ (on the unit circle) into the inverse mapping:

$$
z = \frac{1 + (T/2)s}{1 - (T/2)s}
$$

**Step 1:** Substitute $s = j\Omega$ and $z = e^{j\omega}$:

$$
e^{j\omega} = \frac{1 + j\Omega T/2}{1 - j\Omega T/2}
$$

**Step 2:** Recognize that the right-hand side is a complex number of *unit modulus* (numerator and denominator are complex conjugates). Its argument is twice the argument of the numerator:

$$
\omega = 2 \arctan\!\left(\frac{\Omega T}{2}\right)
$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $\Omega$:

$$
\frac{\omega}{2} = \arctan\!\left(\frac{\Omega T}{2}\right) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \tan\!\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right) = \frac{\Omega T}{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\Omega = \frac{2}{T}\tan\!\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** $\tan(\cdot)$ is a non-linear compression. For small $\omega$, $\tan(\omega/2) \approx \omega/2$, so the mapping is approximately linear. As $\omega \to \pi$, $\tan(\omega/2) \to \infty$, meaning the digital Nyquist frequency maps to $\Omega = \infty$ — the entire analog axis fits into one digital period.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Butterworth Pole Locations

Starting from the magnitude-squared response:

$$
H_a(s) H_a(-s) = \frac{1}{1 + (s/j\Omega_c)^{2N}}
$$

**Step 1:** The poles of this function occur where the denominator is zero:

$$
1 + \left(\frac{s}{j\Omega_c}\right)^{2N} = 0
$$

$$
\left(\frac{s}{j\Omega_c}\right)^{2N} = -1 = e^{j\pi(2k+1)}, \quad k = 0,1,\dots,2N-1
$$

**Step 2:** Taking the $2N$-th root:

$$
\frac{s}{j\Omega_c} = e^{j\pi(2k+1)/(2N)}
$$

$$
s = j\Omega_c \, e^{j\pi(2k+1)/(2N)} = \Omega_c \, e^{j\pi(2k+1)/(2N)} \cdot e^{j\pi/2}
$$

$$
s = \Omega_c \, e^{j\pi(2k+2N+1)/(2N)}
$$

**Step 3:** Rewrite index $m = k+N$ to centre the indices symmetrically:

$$
s = \Omega_c \, e^{j\pi(2m-N+1)/(2N)}, \quad m = 1,2,\dots,2N
$$

**Step 4:** For a stable, causal filter, select only the $N$ poles in the **left-half $s$-plane**, i.e., those with negative real parts. With $\Omega_c = 1$ (normalized prototype):

$$
\boxed{\;p_k = e^{j\pi(2k+N-1)/(2N)}, \quad k = 1,2,\dots,N\;}
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 1 — Design of a Butterworth LPF (Complete Solution)

**Problem Statement (KTU standard):**  
Design a digital Butterworth low pass filter with the following specifications using the bilinear transformation. Assume $T = 1$ second.

- Passband edge: $\omega_p = 0.25\pi$ rad/sample, with $\delta_p = 0.2$
- Stopband edge: $\omega_s = 0.55\pi$ rad/sample, with $\delta_s = 0.1$

---

### Step 1: Compute Pre-warped Analog Frequencies

$$
\Omega_p = \frac{2}{1}\tan\!\left(\frac{0.25\pi}{2}\right) = 2\tan(0.125\pi)
$$

$$
\tan(0.125\pi) = \tan(22.5°) \approx 0.4142
$$

$$
\Omega_p \approx 0.8284 \;\text{rad/sec}
$$

$$
\Omega_s = 2\tan(0.275\pi) = 2\tan(49.5°) \approx 2 \times 1.1708 = 2.3417 \;\text{rad/sec}
$$

---

### Step 2: Compute Required Filter Order

$$
N \ge \frac{\log_{10}\!\left[\dfrac{(1/0.1^2)-1}{(1/0.2^2)-1}\right]}{2\log_{10}(2.3417/0.8284)}
$$

Numerator:

$$
\frac{1/0.01 - 1}{1/0.04 - 1} = \frac{99}{24} = 4.125
$$

Denominator:

$$
2\log_{10}(2.826) = 2 \times 0.4512 = 0.9024
$$

$$
N \ge \frac{\log_{10}(4.125)}{0.9024} = \frac{0.6154}{0.9024} \approx 0.682
$$

Since $N$ must be an integer, round **up**: $N = 1$.

> [!NOTE]
> With $N = 1$, the design will not strictly meet the stopband spec — we will accept this or increase $N$ in a refinement step. For textbook problems, $N$ is often increased by 1 to be safe; here we proceed with $N = 2$ to illustrate the method better.

**Refined:** Let $N = 2$.

---

### Step 3: Determine the 3-dB Cutoff Frequency $\Omega_c$

We choose the more stringent of the two:

$$
\Omega_c \ge \frac{\Omega_p}{(2\delta_p^2 - 2\delta_p)^{-1/(2N)}} \quad \text{(using the passband edge)}
$$

**Derivation of the cutoff formula:** Setting $\vert H(j\Omega_p)\vert^2 = (1-\delta_p)^2$:

$$
(1-\delta_p)^2 = \frac{1}{1 + (\Omega_p/\Omega_c)^{2N}}
$$

$$
1 + (\Omega_p/\Omega_c)^{2N} = \frac{1}{(1-\delta_p)^2}
$$

$$
(\Omega_p/\Omega_c)^{2N} = \frac{1}{(1-\delta_p)^2} - 1 = \frac{1 - (1-\delta_p)^2}{(1-\delta_p)^2}
$$

For small $\delta_p$, $1-(1-\delta_p)^2 \approx 2\delta_p$, so:

$$
\Omega_c = \frac{\Omega_p}{(2\delta_p)^{1/(2N)}}
$$

Plugging $N=2$, $\Omega_p = 0.8284$, $\delta_p = 0.2$:

$$
\Omega_c = \frac{0.8284}{(0.4)^{1/4}} = \frac{0.8284}{0.7953} \approx 1.0416\;\text{rad/sec}
$$

---

### Step 4: Locate the Poles of the Analog Filter

For $N = 2$ Butterworth, normalized poles ($p_k$ for $k=1,2$):

$$
p_1 = e^{j\pi(2\cdot1+2-1)/4} = e^{j\pi(3)/4} = e^{j 135°} = -\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} + j\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}
$$

$$
p_2 = e^{j\pi(2\cdot2+2-1)/4} = e^{j\pi(5)/4} = e^{j 225°} = -\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} - j\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}
$$

Denormalize (multiply by $\Omega_c$):

$$
\tilde{p}_1 = \Omega_c \, p_1 = -0.7366 + j0.7366
$$

$$
\tilde{p}_2 = \Omega_c \, p_2 = -0.7366 - j0.7366
$$

---

### Step 5: Construct $H_a(s)$

$$
H_a(s) = \frac{\Omega_c^{\,2}}{(s-\tilde{p}_1)(s-\tilde{p}_2)} = \frac{\Omega_c^2}{s^2 + \sqrt{2}\,\Omega_c\, s + \Omega_c^2}
$$

With $\Omega_c = 1.0416$:

$$
\Omega_c^2 \approx 1.0849, \quad \sqrt{2}\,\Omega_c \approx 1.4732
$$

$$
H_a(s) = \frac{1.0849}{s^2 + 1.4732 s + 1.0849}
$$

---

### Step 6: Apply the Bilinear Transformation

Substitute $s = \dfrac{2(z-1)}{z+1}$ (since $T=1$):

$$
H(z) = \frac{1.0849}{\left(\dfrac{2(z-1)}{z+1}\right)^2 + 1.4732\left(\dfrac{2(z-1)}{z+1}\right) + 1.0849}
$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by $(z+1)^2$:

$$
H(z) = \frac{1.0849(z+1)^2}{4(z-1)^2 + 2.9464(z-1)(z+1) + 1.0849(z+1)^2}
$$

Expand term by term:

**Term A:** $4(z-1)^2 = 4(z^2 - 2z + 1) = 4z^2 - 8z + 4$

**Term B:** $2.9464(z-1)(z+1) = 2.9464(z^2 - 1) = 2.9464z^2 - 2.9464$

**Term C:** $1.0849(z+1)^2 = 1.0849(z^2 + 2z + 1) = 1.0849z^2 + 2.1698z + 1.0849$

**Sum of denominator terms:**

$$
z^2 \text{ coefficient: } 4 + 2.9464 + 1.0849 = 8.0313
$$

$$
z^1 \text{ coefficient: } -8 + 0 + 2.1698 = -5.8302
$$

$$
z^0 \text{ coefficient: } 4 - 2.9464 + 1.0849 = 2.1385
$$

So:

$$
H(z) = \frac{1.0849(z+1)^2}{8.0313 z^2 - 5.8302 z + 2.1385}
$$

**Expand numerator:** $(z+1)^2 = z^2 + 2z + 1$, so numerator $= 1.0849z^2 + 2.1698z + 1.0849$.

---

### Step 7: Normalize to Monic Numerator Coefficient (final form)

Divide numerator and denominator by $8.0313$:

$$
H(z) = \frac{0.1351\, z^2 + 0.2702\, z + 0.1351}{z^2 - 0.7260\, z + 0.2663}
$$

Rewriting with positive powers of $z^{-1}$ (multiply top and bottom by $z^{-2}$):

$$
H(z) = \frac{0.1351 + 0.2702\, z^{-1} + 0.1351\, z^{-2}}{1 - 0.7260\, z^{-1} + 0.2663\, z^{-2}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Difference Equation:**
>
> $$
> y(n) = 0.7260\, y(n-1) - 0.2663\, y(n-2) + 0.1351\, x(n) + 0.2702\, x(n-1) + 0.1351\, x(n-2)
> $$

This is the IIR low pass filter realization.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 2 — High Pass Filter (LPF→HPF Transformation)

**Problem:** Convert the LPF designed above to a **High Pass Filter** with digital cutoff $\omega_c = 0.4\pi$ rad/sample.

### Step 1: Use the Analog Transformation $s \to \Omega_c/s$

Start with the normalized Butterworth LPF prototype at $N=2$:

$$
H_{LP}(s) = \frac{1}{s^2 + \sqrt{2}\,s + 1}
$$

Substitute $s \to \Omega_c/s$:

$$
H_{HP}(s) = \frac{1}{(\Omega_c/s)^2 + \sqrt{2}(\Omega_c/s) + 1}
$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by $s^2$:

$$
H_{HP}(s) = \frac{s^2}{\Omega_c^2 + \sqrt{2}\,\Omega_c\, s + s^2}
$$

### Step 2: Apply Bilinear Transformation with $T = 1$

Replace $s$ by $\dfrac{2(z-1)}{z+1}$:

$$
H(z) = \frac{\left(\dfrac{2(z-1)}{z+1}\right)^2}{\Omega_c^2 + \sqrt{2}\,\Omega_c \left(\dfrac{2(z-1)}{z+1}\right) + \left(\dfrac{2(z-1)}{z+1}\right)^2}
$$

### Step 3: Compute Pre-warped $\Omega_c$

$$
\Omega_c = \tan(0.2\pi) = \tan(36°) \approx 0.7265
$$

The arithmetic proceeds identically to Example 3.3, yielding a final $H(z)$ whose coefficients reflect a high pass response. The key signature: **the numerator coefficients are roughly alternating in sign** $+, -, +$ instead of $+, +, +$ as in the LPF.

> [!NOTE]
> The above HPF derivation is shown conceptually — full numerical completion is a standard exercise for the student. The result has the form $H_{HP}(z) = \dfrac{b_0 - b_1 z^{-1} + b_2 z^{-2}}{1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2}}$ where $b_0, b_2 > 0$ and $b_1 < 0$.

---

## 3.5 Summary of Design Algorithm (Algorithm Card)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step-by-step KTU Algorithm — IIR Filter Design via BLT:**
> 1. Read digital specs $(\omega_p, \delta_p, \omega_s, \delta_s)$ and sampling period $T$.
> 2. Pre-warp: $\Omega_p = (2/T)\tan(\omega_p/2)$, $\Omega_s = (2/T)\tan(\omega_s/2)$.
> 3. Compute $N = \lceil \text{equation from §2.5} \rceil$.
> 4. Compute $\Omega_c$ using either passband or stopband edge.
> 5. Locate LHP Butterworth poles.
> 6. Form $H_a(s)$ and (for HPF) apply $s \to \Omega_c/s$.
> 7. Substitute BLT $s = (2/T)(z-1)/(z+1)$.
> 8. Simplify and express as ratio of polynomials in $z^{-1}$.
> 9. Write the difference equation.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of the IIR Filter Design Pipeline

The following Mermaid diagram captures the *complete functional flow* used in KTU exam answers when designing an IIR digital filter using the Bilinear Transformation of a Butterworth prototype.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Step 1: Read Digital Specs omega_p, omega_s, delta_p, delta_s, T"] --> B["Step 2: Pre-Warping Formula"]
    B --> B1["Compute Omega_p = 2 over T times tan of omega_p over 2"]
    B --> B2["Compute Omega_s = 2 over T times tan of omega_s over 2"]
    B1 --> C["Step 3: Determine Filter Order N"]
    B2 --> C
    C --> D["Step 4: Compute 3 dB Cutoff Omega_c"]
    D --> E["Step 5: Locate LHP Poles of Butterworth Prototype"]
    E --> F["Step 6: Construct Analog Transfer Function H_a of s"]
    F --> G{FILTER TYPE?}
    G -- LPF --> H["Step 7A: Apply BLT directly: s equals 2 over T times z minus 1 over z plus 1"]
    G -- HPF --> I["Step 7B: Apply LP to HP transformation: s becomes Omega_c over s"]
    I --> H
    H --> J["Step 8: Simplify to H of z = B of z over A of z"]
    J --> K["Step 9: Write Difference Equation y of n"]
    K --> L["Step 10: Realize using Direct Form I, II, or Cascade"]

    stepA["Step 1"]:::phaseBox
    stepB["Step 2"]:::phaseBox
    stepC["Step 3"]:::phaseBox
    stepD["Step 4"]:::phaseBox

    classDef phaseBox fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#4a90e2,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff;
```

## 4.2 Mapped IIR Filter Realization — Direct Form II

After deriving $H(z) = \dfrac{b_0 + b_1 z^{-1} + b_2 z^{-2}}{1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2}}$, the canonical (Direct Form II) structure is implemented as follows:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    xn["x of n"] --> sumA["Sum Node: w of n"]
    sumA --> b0["b0"]
    b0 --> out["y of n"]
    sumA --> b1["z to the power minus 1"]
    b1 --> out
    sumA --> b2["z to the power minus 2"]
    b2 --> out

    sumA --> dly1["z to the power minus 1"]
    dly1 --> a1["a1"]
    a1 --> sumA
    dly1 --> dly2["z to the power minus 1"]
    dly2 --> a2["a2"]
    a2 --> sumA

    classDef blockStyle fill:#2d5016,stroke:#90c290,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#ffffff;
    class sumA,b0,b1,b2,a1,a2,dly1,dly2 blockStyle;
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Magnitude H of e to the power j omega"] --> B["LPF Response"]
    A --> C["HPF Response"]
    B --> B1["Passband: omega from 0 to omega_c: gain equals 1"]
    B --> B2["Stopband: omega greater than omega_c: gain rolls off"]
    C --> C1["Stopband: omega from 0 to omega_c: gain equals 0"]
    C --> C2["Passband: omega greater than omega_c: gain equals 1"]

```

    BLT --> Zb["Digital z-plane: poles inside unit circle"]

    jOmega["j omega axis: -infinity to plus infinity"] --> C["Compresses nonlinearly via tan"]
