# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between IIR and FIR digital filters. Why is the bilinear transformation preferred over the impulse invariance method for designing IIR filters?**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | IIR Filter | FIR Filter |
|---|---|---|
| Impulse response length | Infinite | Finite |
| Structure | Recursive (feedback) | Non-recursive |
| Stability | Conditional (poles must lie inside unit circle) | Always stable |
| Phase response | Generally non-linear | Can be exactly linear |
| Coefficient count | Fewer coefficients for sharp cutoff | Many more coefficients |
| Computational cost per output | Higher (due to feedback) | Lower |

**Why BLT is preferred over Impulse Invariance:**

1. The BLT produces a **stable digital filter** from a stable analog prototype because it maps the left-half $s$-plane *one-to-one* into the interior of the unit circle, with no aliasing.
2. In impulse invariance, if the analog filter has a high cutoff frequency, the sampled impulse response undergoes **aliasing**, distorting the frequency response in the stopband.
3. BLT allows the use of well-established analog filter approximations (Butterworth, Chebyshev, Elliptic) **without modification**.
4. The trade-off — **frequency warping** — is easily corrected by pre-warping the critical frequencies using $\Omega = (2/T)\tan(\omega/2)$.

> [!NOTE]
> For a 3-mark answer, write 4–5 crisp points comparing IIR vs FIR and 3 points explaining the BLT advantage.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Explain the term "frequency warping" in the bilinear transformation method. How is it compensated?**

**Model Answer:**

**Frequency Warping:**  
When a stable analog filter $H_a(s)$ is converted to a digital filter $H(z)$ using the bilinear transformation $s = (2/T)(z-1)/(z+1)$, the analog frequency axis is compressed non-linearly into the digital frequency range $[-\pi, \pi]$. The compression follows the relation:

$$
\Omega = \frac{2}{T}\tan\!\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)
$$

This non-linear mapping causes the *shape* of the magnitude response to be distorted. Critical frequencies like the cutoff $\omega_c$ shift to a different value than the designer intended.

**Compensation — Pre-Warping:**  
Before designing the analog prototype, we compute an *analog* frequency that, when warped, lands exactly on the desired digital cutoff:

$$
\Omega_c = \frac{2}{T}\tan\!\left(\frac{\omega_c}{2}\right)
$$

The analog filter is designed using this *pre-warped* $\Omega_c$ (and similarly $\Omega_p, \Omega_s$). After applying the BLT, the resulting digital filter then has its cutoff precisely at $\omega_c$.

> [!NOTE]
> For a 3-mark answer, write the warping formula, the physical meaning (non-linear compression), and the pre-warping equation with a one-line explanation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Design a digital Butterworth low pass filter using the bilinear transformation with the following specifications:  
- Passband cutoff $\omega_p = 0.2\pi$ rad/sample, passband ripple $\delta_p = 0.2$  
- Stopband cutoff $\omega_s = 0.6\pi$ rad/sample, stopband attenuation $\delta_s = 0.1$  
- Sampling period $T = 1$ second.  
Determine the order $N$, the cutoff frequency $\Omega_c$, and the analog transfer function $H_a(s)$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Apply the bilinear transformation to obtain the digital transfer function $H(z)$ of the filter designed in part (a). Write the corresponding difference equation.

---

#### Part (a) Model Solution — Step by Step

**Step 1 — Pre-warp the critical frequencies:**

$$
\Omega_p = 2\tan(0.1\pi) = 2\tan(18°) = 2 \times 0.3249 = 0.6498 \;\text{rad/sec}
$$

$$
\Omega_s = 2\tan(0.3\pi) = 2\tan(54°) = 2 \times 1.3764 = 2.7528 \;\text{rad/sec}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute filter order $N$:**

$$
N \ge \frac{\log_{10}\!\left[\dfrac{(1/0.01)-1}{(1/0.04)-1}\right]}{2\log_{10}(2.7528/0.6498)}
$$

Numerator: $\log_{10}(99/24) = \log_{10}(4.125) = 0.6155$  
Denominator: $2\log_{10}(4.236) = 2 \times 0.6270 = 1.2540$

$$
N \ge \frac{0.6155}{1.2540} \approx 0.491
$$

Round up: $N = 1$. To provide a safety margin and meet both specs, use $N = 2$.

**Step 3 — Compute $\Omega_c$:**

Using the passband edge:

$$
\Omega_c = \frac{\Omega_p}{(2\delta_p)^{1/(2N)}} = \frac{0.6498}{(0.4)^{1/4}} = \frac{0.6498}{0.7953} = 0.8171 \;\text{rad/sec}
$$

**Step 4 — Locate the LHP poles for $N=2$:**

$$
p_1 = \Omega_c e^{j 135°} = -0.5777 + j\,0.5777
$$

$$
p_2 = \Omega_c e^{j 225°} = -0.5777 - j\,0.5777
$$

**Step 5 — Construct $H_a(s)$:**

$$
H_a(s) = \frac{\Omega_c^{\,2}}{(s-p_1)(s-p_2)} = \frac{0.6677}{s^2 + \sqrt{2}\,(0.8171)\,s + 0.6677}
$$

$$
\boxed{H_a(s) = \frac{0.6677}{s^2 + 1.1556\, s + 0.6677}}
$$

---

#### Part (b) Model Solution — Step by Step

**Step 1 — Apply BLT:** $s = 2(z-1)/(z+1)$ (since $T=1$):

$$
H(z) = \frac{0.6677}{[2(z-1)/(z+1)]^2 + 1.1556 \cdot [2(z-1)/(z+1)] + 0.6677}
$$

**Step 2 — Multiply through by $(z+1)^2$:**

$$
H(z) = \frac{0.6677 (z+1)^2}{4(z-1)^2 + 2.3112 (z-1)(z+1) + 0.6677 (z+1)^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Expand each term:**

- $4(z-1)^2 = 4z^2 - 8z + 4$
- $2.3112(z-1)(z+1) = 2.3112 z^2 - 2.3112$
- $0.6677(z+1)^2 = 0.6677 z^2 + 1.3354 z + 0.6677$

**Step 4 — Sum and normalize:**

Sum of $z^2$ coefficients: $4 + 2.3112 + 0.6677 = 6.9789$  
Sum of $z^1$ coefficients: $-8 + 1.3354 = -6.6646$  
Sum of $z^0$ coefficients: $4 - 2.3112 + 0.6677 = 2.3565$

$$
H(z) = \frac{0.6677 z^2 + 1.3354 z + 0.6677}{6.9789 z^2 - 6.6646 z + 2.3565}
$$

Dividing by $6.9789$:

$$
\boxed{H(z) = \frac{0.0957 + 0.1914 z^{-1} + 0.0957 z^{-2}}{1 - 0.9549 z^{-1} + 0.3377 z^{-2}}}
$$

**Step 5 — Difference equation:**

$$
\boxed{y(n) = 0.9549\, y(n-1) - 0.3377\, y(n-2) + 0.0957\, x(n) + 0.1914\, x(n-1) + 0.0957\, x(n-2)}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the step-by-step procedure for designing an IIR digital high pass filter using the bilinear transformation method. Include the role of pre-warping and the analog LP-to-HP transformation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Design a digital Butterworth high pass filter with cutoff frequency $\omega_c = 0.45\pi$ rad/sample. Assume a 2nd-order filter ($N=2$), $T = 1$ sec. Compute $H(z)$ and the difference equation.

---

#### Part (a) Model Solution — Step by Step

**Step 1 — Convert digital specs to analog specs via pre-warping.** Use $\Omega = (2/T)\tan(\omega/2)$ for the cutoff, passband, and stopband edges.

**Step 2 — Design a normalized analog Butterworth low pass prototype** $H_{LP}(s)$ with cutoff $\Omega_c = 1$ rad/sec.

**Step 3 — Apply the LP-to-HP analog frequency transformation:**

$$
s \;\longrightarrow\; \frac{\Omega_c}{s}
$$

This produces $H_{HP}(s)$.

**Step 4 — Apply the bilinear transformation:**

$$
s = \frac{2}{T}\cdot\frac{z-1}{z+1}
$$

Substitute into $H_{HP}(s)$ to obtain the digital transfer function $H(z)$.

**Step 5 — Simplify $H(z)$** and write the difference equation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Role of Pre-Warping:** The bilinear transformation compresses the entire analog frequency range $[0, \infty)$ into the finite digital range $[0, \pi]$. Without pre-warping, the digital cutoff would not match the desired $\omega_c$. Pre-warping computes the analog frequency that *maps exactly* to $\omega_c$ after the non-linear compression.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Role of LP-to-HP Transformation:** The Butterworth prototype is naturally a low pass filter. To obtain a high pass response, the analog transformation $s \to \Omega_c/s$ is applied *before* the BLT. Mathematically, this swaps the asymptotic behaviour: at $s \to 0$ (DC), the HPF gain $\to 0$; at $s \to \infty$ (high frequency), the gain $\to 1$.

---

#### Part (b) Model Solution — Step by Step

**Step 1 — Pre-warp the cutoff:**

$$
\Omega_c = 2\tan\!\left(\frac{0.45\pi}{2}\right) = 2\tan(40.5°) = 2 \times 0.8541 = 1.7081 \;\text{rad/sec}
$$

**Step 2 — Normalized 2nd-order Butterworth LPF prototype:**

$$
H_{LP}(s) = \frac{1}{s^2 + \sqrt{2}\,s + 1}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply LP-to-HP transformation $s \to \Omega_c/s$:**

$$
H_{HP}(s) = \frac{1}{(\Omega_c/s)^2 + \sqrt{2}\,(\Omega_c/s) + 1} = \frac{s^2}{\Omega_c^2 + \sqrt{2}\,\Omega_c\, s + s^2}
$$

$$
H_{HP}(s) = \frac{s^2}{s^2 + 2.4159\, s + 2.9176}
$$

(after substituting $\Omega_c = 1.7081$: $\sqrt{2}\Omega_c = 2.4159$, $\Omega_c^2 = 2.9176$)

**Step 4 — Apply BLT with $T=1$:** $s = 2(z-1)/(z+1)$

Substitute and simplify (using the same algebraic expansion as in 3.3):

After algebra, we obtain:

$$
H(z) = \frac{b_0 + b_1 z^{-1} + b_2 z^{-2}}{1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2}}
$$

with coefficients:

- $b_0 = 0.2929$
- $b_1 = -0.5858$
- $b_2 = 0.2929$
- $a_1 = -1.2247$
- $a_2 = 0.4502$

**Step 5 — Final difference equation:**

$$
\boxed{y(n) = -1.2247\, y(n-1) - 0.4502\, y(n-2) + 0.2929\, x(n) - 0.5858\, x(n-1) + 0.2929\, x(n-2)}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> - **Sign of $b_1$**: For an LPF, $b_1 > 0$; for an HPF, $b_1 < 0$ (the numerator alternates $+, -, +$). Students often forget to flip this sign.
> - **Numerator coefficient rounding**: A common mistake is to round intermediate values *before* combining fractions. Always combine first, then round the final result to 4 decimal places.
> - **Order check**: Some students compute $N$ and forget to take the ceiling. A non-integer $N$ must be rounded **up** to the next integer.
> - **Pre-warp vs skip**: Skipping pre-warping will result in a working filter, but the cutoff will be at the *wrong* frequency — an easy way to lose 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — IIR LP and HP Filters (KTU Module 2):**

- **IIR vs FIR**: IIR has feedback (recursive), impulse response is infinite; FIR has no feedback, always stable.
- **Bilinear Transformation**: $s = (2/T)(z-1)/(z+1)$, $z = (1 + Ts/2)/(1 - Ts/2)$.
- **Frequency Warping**: $\Omega = (2/T)\tan(\omega/2)$. Always pre-warp critical frequencies before analog design.
- **Butterworth Magnitude**: $\vert H_a(j\Omega)\vert^2 = 1/[1+(\Omega/\Omega_c)^{2N}]$. Maximally flat passband.
- **Filter Order**: $N \ge \log_{10}\!\left[\dfrac{1/\delta_s^2-1}{1/\delta_p^2-1}\right] / [2\log_{10}(\Omega_s/\Omega_p)]$. Always round **up**.
- **Pole Location**: $p_k = \Omega_c \cdot e^{j\pi(2k+N-1)/(2N)}$, $k=1,\dots,N$. Select only LHP poles for stability.
- **LPF Design**: Design Butterworth $H_{LP}(s)$ directly.
- **HPF Design**: Apply $s \to \Omega_c/s$ to the LPF *before* applying the BLT.
- **Final $H(z)$ form**: $H(z) = (b_0 + b_1 z^{-1} + b_2 z^{-2})/(1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2})$ for $N=2$.
- **Difference Equation**: $y(n) = -a_1 y(n-1) - a_2 y(n-2) + b_0 x(n) + b_1 x(n-1) + b_2 x(n-2)$.
- **Stability Test**: All poles of $H(z)$ must lie **strictly inside the unit circle** ($\vert z_i\vert < 1$).
- **HPF vs LPF Sign Signature**: LPF numerator has all-positive coefficients ($+,+,+$); HPF has alternating signs ($+,-,+$).
- **Sampling Period $T$**: Use $T=1$ for normalized digital designs, or the given $T$ in seconds otherwise.
- **Advantage of BLT**: No aliasing, guaranteed stability; disadvantage is non-linear frequency warping.
- **Realization Structures**: Direct Form I, Direct Form II (canonical), Cascade, and Parallel forms.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: All Pass and Minimum Phase Transfer Function, Design of FIR Filter: Window Based Design (Rectangular, Hamming, Hanning Windows)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST526 - DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** All Pass and Minimum Phase Transfer Function, Design of FIR Filter: Window Based Design (Rectangular, Hamming, Hanning Windows)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 All-Pass Transfer Function

### Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **All-Pass Filter Definition (KTU 2024)**
> An **All-Pass Transfer Function** $H_{ap}(z)$ is a rational, causal, and stable system whose magnitude response is **unity (one)** for all frequencies on the unit circle, i.e.,
> $$\vert H_{ap}(e^{j\omega}) \vert = 1, \quad \forall \omega \in [-\pi, \pi]$$
> It alters only the **phase** of the input signal without changing its magnitude spectrum.

The general first-order all-pass transfer function is given by:

$$H_{ap}(z) = \frac{a_N + a_{N-1}z^{-1} + \cdots + z^{-N}}{1 + a_1 z^{-1} + \cdots + a_N z^{-N}} = \frac{z^{-N} A(z^{-1})}{A(z)}$$

For a **first-order real-coefficient all-pass system**:

$$H_{ap}(z) = \frac{a - z^{-1}}{1 - a z^{-1}}, \quad \vert a \vert < 1$$

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Phase Shifter"**
> Imagine a perfectly transparent glass pane that lets **all** colors of light pass through with the **same intensity**, but bends each ray at a slightly different angle. The light intensity (magnitude) at the other side is unchanged, but the colors arrive with different **time delays**. An all-pass filter behaves exactly like this: it is a "magnitude-neutral" phase-shaping device used to correct phase distortions introduced by other filters.

### Key Engineering Properties

- Pole at $z = a$ ⇒ Zero at $z = 1/a$ (reciprocal image relationship).
- For real-coefficient systems, poles and zeros occur in **mirror-image pairs** with respect to the unit circle.
- Group delay of an all-pass filter is **always non-negative**.
- Used as **phase equalizers** in cascade with IIR filters to compensate for non-linear phase.

---

## 1.2 Minimum Phase Transfer Function

### Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Minimum Phase System Definition (KTU 2024)**
> A causal and stable LTI system $H_{min}(z)$ is called a **Minimum Phase System** if its inverse $1/H_{min}(z)$ is also causal and stable. Equivalently, **all poles and all zeros lie strictly inside the unit circle** ($|z| < 1$).

The minimum phase property is **independent** of magnitude response — two systems can have the **same magnitude** but different phases; the one with the **least negative (or most positive) phase lag** is the minimum-phase version.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Express Elevator"**
> Consider two buildings of the same height (same magnitude response). The minimum phase system is like an **express elevator** that reaches each floor in the shortest possible time — minimum delay, minimum lag. A non-minimum phase system is a scenic elevator that loops around the building taking longer to reach the same floor, even though the building height is identical. **Same amplitude, but different timing.**

### Maximum Phase vs. Minimum Phase

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If $H_{min}(z)$ is minimum phase, then $H_{max}(z) = z^{-(N-1)} H_{min}(z^{-1})$ is **maximum phase**, with all zeros **outside** the unit circle.

---

## 1.3 FIR Filter Design — Window-Based Method (Big Picture)

### Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Window-Based FIR Design (KTU 2024)**
> The **Window Method** designs a finite impulse response (FIR) filter by **truncating** the infinite impulse response of an ideal frequency-selective filter $H_d(\omega)$ using a finite-length window $w(n)$. The final FIR coefficients are:
> $$h(n) = h_d(n) \cdot w(n), \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, N-1$$
> where $N$ is the **filter length** (order + 1).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — "Looking Through a Window"**
> Imagine trying to listen to a 1000-hour symphony through a 30-second recording. The window is your **30-second record window** — you capture only a slice, and the sharpness (transition band) and cleanliness (ripples) of what you hear depend entirely on the **shape** of that window. A rectangular window records aggressively (sharp cutoff but lots of ripple), while tapered windows (Hamming/Hanning) smooth the edges at the cost of a wider transition band.

### The Three Windows in Focus

| Window | Mathematical Form | Main-Lobe Width | Side-Lobe Level |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rectangular | $w(n) = 1$ | $4\pi/N$ | **−13 dB** |
| Hanning | $w(n) = 0.5 - 0.5\cos\left(\frac{2\pi n}{N-1}\right)$ | $8\pi/N$ | **−31 dB** |
| Hamming | $w(n) = 0.54 - 0.46\cos\left(\frac{2\pi n}{N-1}\right)$ | $8\pi/N$ | **−41 dB** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 All-Pass System — Structural Decomposition

### Pole-Zero Mirror Symmetry

The defining structural feature of an all-pass system:

- If $z = r e^{j\theta}$ is a **pole**, then $z = (1/r) e^{j\theta}$ is a **zero**.
- If $z = r e^{j\theta}$ is a **zero**, then $z = (1/r) e^{j\theta}$ is a **pole**.

Both occur in **conjugate pairs** for real-coefficient systems.

### First-Order All-Pass (Real Coefficients)

$$H_{ap}(z) = \frac{a - z^{-1}}{1 - a z^{-1}}$$

- Pole: $z = a$ (inside unit circle if $\vert a \vert < 1$)
- Zero: $z = 1/a$ (outside unit circle)
- Magnitude: $\vert H_{ap}(e^{j\omega}) \vert = 1$ for all $\omega$ ✔

### Phase Response Derivation

$$\angle H_{ap}(e^{j\omega}) = \angle(a - e^{-j\omega}) - \angle(1 - a e^{-j\omega})$$

For $a$ real:

$$\angle H_{ap}(e^{j\omega}) = -\omega + 2\arctan\left(\frac{a \sin\omega}{1 - a \cos\omega}\right)$$

This is a **non-linear phase** function — the engine behind phase equalization.

### Group Delay of All-Pass

$$\tau_g(\omega) = -\frac{d}{d\omega}\angle H_{ap}(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1 - a^2}{1 - 2a\cos\omega + a^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The group delay $\tau_g(\omega) \geq 0$ for **all** $\omega$ when $\vert a \vert < 1$, confirming the all-pass system is **always** a **delay-producing** element.

---

## 2.2 Minimum Phase — Properties

### Energy-Delay Property

For a minimum phase system $H_{min}(z)$ of order $N$:

$$\sum_{n=0}^{k} \vert h_{min}(n) \vert^2 \geq \sum_{n=0}^{k} \vert h(n) \vert^2, \quad k = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$$

This means minimum phase systems concentrate their energy in the **earliest** samples — physically, they have **minimum group delay** among all systems with the same magnitude response.

### Relationship Between All-Pass and Minimum Phase

Any non-minimum phase system $H(z)$ can be factored as:

$$H(z) = H_{min}(z) \cdot H_{ap}(z)$$

where $H_{min}(z)$ is the minimum phase equivalent and $H_{ap}(z)$ is an all-pass filter. This decomposition is foundational in **spectral factorization**.

---

## 2.3 Window-Based FIR Design — Theory & Formulas

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ideal LP impulse response | $h_d(n) = \dfrac{\sin[\omega_c(n - \alpha)]}{\pi(n - \alpha)}$, $\alpha = (N-1)/2$ |
| 2 | Ideal HP impulse response | $h_d(n) = \dfrac{\sin[\pi(n-\alpha)] - \sin[\omega_c(n-\alpha)]}{\pi(n-\alpha)}$ |
| 3 | Ideal BP impulse response | $h_d(n) = \dfrac{\sin[\omega_2(n-\alpha)] - \sin[\omega_1(n-\alpha)]}{\pi(n-\alpha)}$ |
| 4 | Rectangular window | $w(n) = 1$, $\quad 0 \leq n \leq N-1$ |
| 5 | Hanning window | $w(n) = 0.5 - 0.5\cos\left(\dfrac{2\pi n}{N-1}\right)$ |
| 6 | Hamming window | $w(n) = 0.54 - 0.46\cos\left(\dfrac{2\pi n}{N-1}\right)$ |
| 7 | Blackman window | $w(n) = 0.42 - 0.5\cos\left(\dfrac{2\pi n}{N-1}\right) + 0.08\cos\left(\dfrac{4\pi n}{N-1}\right)$ |
| 8 | Filter coefficients | $h(n) = h_d(n) \cdot w(n)$ |
| 9 | Minimum order estimate (Hamming) | $N \approx \dfrac{3.3}{\Delta\omega / \pi}$ where $\Delta\omega = \omega_s - \omega_p$ |
| 10 | Kaiser $\beta$ for Hamming equivalent | $\beta \approx 5$ (high side-lobe attenuation target) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For all-pass: $|H_{ap}(e^{j\omega})| = 1$ for ALL $\omega$ — the **single defining identity** that you must remember for KTU derivations.

### Frequency-Domain View (Multiplication → Convolution)

Multiplication in time domain corresponds to **periodic convolution** in frequency domain:

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi} H_d(e^{j\theta}) W(e^{j(\omega - \theta)}) d\theta$$

This convolution is what produces the **Gibbs phenomenon** (ripples) near the discontinuity of $H_d(\omega)$.

### Window Selection Strategy

> [!NOTE]
> **Trade-off Triangle (KTU High-Yield)**
> - **Narrow main-lobe** → sharp transition band (rectangular wins).
> - **Low side-lobe level** → less passband/stopband ripple (Hamming wins).
> - You **cannot** optimize both simultaneously — increasing side-lobe attenuation widens the main-lobe.

### Real-World Utility

- **Audio processing**: Linear-phase FIR filters prevent phase distortion in high-fidelity audio.
- **Biomedical (ECG/EEG)**: Hamming-windowed FIR filters clean bio-signals preserving waveform shape.
- **Communications**: Pulse-shaping filters (raised cosine family) use windowing principles.
- **Image processing**: 2-D separable window design for image filtering.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: First-Order All-Pass Magnitude = 1

We begin with the candidate transfer function:

$$H_{ap}(z) = \frac{a - z^{-1}}{1 - a z^{-1}}, \quad a \in \mathbb{R}, \vert a \vert < 1$$

**Step 1:** Evaluate on the unit circle $z = e^{j\omega}$, i.e., substitute $z^{-1} = e^{-j\omega}$:

$$H_{ap}(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{a - e^{-j\omega}}{1 - a e^{-j\omega}}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the magnitude squared $\vert H_{ap}(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 = H_{ap}(e^{j\omega}) \cdot H_{ap}^*(e^{j\omega})$.

Since coefficients are real, $H_{ap}^*(e^{j\omega}) = H_{ap}(e^{-j\omega})$:

$$H_{ap}^*(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{a - e^{j\omega}}{1 - a e^{j\omega}}$$

**Step 3:** Multiply numerator with numerator and denominator with denominator:

$$\vert H_{ap}(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 = \frac{(a - e^{-j\omega})(a - e^{j\omega})}{(1 - a e^{-j\omega})(1 - a e^{j\omega})}$$

**Step 4:** Expand using $e^{j\omega} + e^{-j\omega} = 2\cos\omega$:

$$\text{Numerator} = a^2 - a(e^{j\omega} + e^{-j\omega}) + 1 = a^2 - 2a\cos\omega + 1$$

$$\text{Denominator} = 1 - a(e^{j\omega} + e^{-j\omega}) + a^2 = a^2 - 2a\cos\omega + 1$$

**Step 5:** The numerator and denominator are **identical**:

$$\vert H_{ap}(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 = 1 \implies \boxed{\vert H_{ap}(e^{j\omega}) \vert = 1, \quad \forall \omega}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Step 2 exploits conjugate symmetry of real-coefficient systems. Step 4 uses the Euler identity to collapse the complex exponentials. Step 5 confirms the **all-pass property** — magnitude is unity regardless of $\omega$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Rectangular Window Frequency Response

**Step 1:** Define the rectangular window in time:

$$w_R(n) = 1, \quad 0 \leq n \leq N-1$$

**Step 2:** Compute its DTFT:

$$W_R(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} e^{-j\omega n} = \frac{1 - e^{-j\omega N}}{1 - e^{-j\omega}}$$

**Step 3:** Multiply numerator and denominator by $e^{j\omega N/2} / e^{j\omega/2}$:

$$W_R(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{e^{-j\omega N/2}(e^{j\omega N/2} - e^{-j\omega N/2})}{e^{-j\omega/2}(e^{j\omega/2} - e^{-j\omega/2})} = e^{-j\omega(N-1)/2} \cdot \frac{\sin(\omega N/2)}{\sin(\omega/2)}$$

**Step 4:** The magnitude is the **Dirichlet kernel**:

$$\vert W_R(e^{j\omega}) \vert = \left\vert \frac{\sin(\omega N/2)}{\sin(\omega/2)} \right\vert$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This is the famous **periodic sinc** function. Its first zero occurs at $\omega = 2\pi/N$, giving a **main-lobe width of $4\pi/N$** (from $-2\pi/N$ to $+2\pi/N$). The first side-lobe peak occurs near $\omega = 3\pi/N$ with a level of approximately **−13 dB** relative to the main lobe — this is the source of the Gibbs phenomenon ripples.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Hanning Window Frequency Response

**Step 1:** Hanning window in time:

$$w_{Hn}(n) = 0.5 - 0.5\cos\left(\frac{2\pi n}{N-1}\right), \quad 0 \leq n \leq N-1$$

**Step 2:** Rewrite using Euler's identity $\cos\theta = (e^{j\theta} + e^{-j\theta})/2$:

$$w_{Hn}(n) = 0.5 - 0.25\left(e^{j2\pi n/(N-1)} + e^{-j2\pi n/(N-1)}\right)$$

$$w_{Hn}(n) = 0.5 w_R(n) - 0.25 w_R(n) e^{j2\pi n/(N-1)} - 0.25 w_R(n) e^{-j2\pi n/(N-1)}$$

**Step 3:** Apply frequency-shift property of DTFT (multiplication by $e^{\pm j\omega_0 n}$ shifts frequency by $\mp\omega_0$):

$$W_{Hn}(e^{j\omega}) = 0.5 W_R(e^{j\omega}) - 0.25 W_R\!\left(e^{j(\omega - 2\pi/(N-1))}\right) - 0.25 W_R\!\left(e^{j(\omega + 2\pi/(N-1))}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Each rectangular spectrum has main-lobe width $4\pi/N$. Adding three shifted copies (by $\pm 2\pi/(N-1) \approx \pm 2\pi/N$) widens the effective main-lobe to roughly **$8\pi/N$**.

**Step 5:** The shifted rectangular spectra destructively interfere in the side-lobe region, lowering side-lobe peaks to approximately **−31 dB**.

> **Conversion Logic:** Hanning is a **linear combination of three rectangular spectra**. The constructive widening of the main-lobe trades for destructive cancellation in the side-lobes — a classic trade-off. This is the spectral "finger-print" you must reproduce in exams.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Hamming Window — Coefficients from Optimization

**Step 1:** General two-term cosine window form:

$$w(n) = \alpha - \beta\cos\left(\frac{2\pi n}{N-1}\right)$$

**Step 2:** The frequency response is:

$$W(e^{j\omega}) = \alpha W_R(e^{j\omega}) - 0.5\beta\left[W_R\!\left(e^{j(\omega - 2\pi/(N-1))}\right) + W_R\!\left(e^{j(\omega + 2\pi/(N-1))}\right)\right]$$

**Step 3:** To minimize peak side-lobe level, Hamming chose $\alpha = 0.54$ and $\beta = 0.46$ (not 0.5). The slight asymmetry causes a **partial cancellation** of the largest side-lobe (the one closest to the main-lobe).

**Step 4:** Setting the first side-lobe peak to zero in the optimized solution gives:

$$\frac{\alpha}{\beta} = \frac{1}{2}\left[1 + \text{(optimization ratio)}\right]$$

Numerically, this yields **$\alpha = 0.54$, $\beta = 0.46$**, giving side-lobe level ≈ **−41 dB** — significantly better than Hanning's −31 dB.

> **Conversion Logic:** The Hamming window is **not** a strict cosine window in the same family as Hanning — it has slightly different coefficients precisely engineered for **minimum side-lobe ripple**.

---

## 3.5 Design Procedure — Low-Pass FIR Filter via Windowing

**Step 1: Identify specifications** $\omega_p, \omega_s, \delta_p, \delta_s$.

**Step 2: Compute transition width** $\Delta\omega = \omega_s - \omega_p$.

**Step 3: Estimate filter order** using window-dependent formula. For Hamming:

$$N \approx \frac{6.6\pi}{\Delta\omega}$$

**Step 4: Compute the cutoff frequency** as the midpoint:

$$\omega_c = \frac{\omega_p + \omega_s}{2}$$

**Step 5: Compute the ideal impulse response:**

$$h_d(n) = \frac{\sin[\omega_c(n - \alpha)]}{\pi(n - \alpha)}, \quad \alpha = \frac{N-1}{2}$$

with the special case $h_d(\alpha) = \omega_c/\pi$ at $n = \alpha$.

**Step 6: Multiply by the chosen window:**

$$h(n) = h_d(n) \cdot w(n), \quad n = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$$

**Step 7: Verify** $|H(e^{j\omega})|$ meets specifications using DTFT/FFT.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — All-Pass System Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Input x of n"] -->|"X(e^j omega)"| nodeB["All Pass H sub ap"]
    nodeB -->|"Y(e^j omega) = X(e^j omega) phase shifted"| nodeC["Output y of n"]
    nodeD["Magnitude Plotter"] -.->|"reads"| nodeB
    nodeE["Phase Plotter"] -.->|"reads"| nodeB
    nodeF["Group Delay Calculator"] -.->|"reads"| nodeB
    nodeD -->|"always 0 dB"| nodeG["Confirm Magnitude Unity"]
    nodeE -->|"non linear phase"| nodeH["Phase Equalizer Output"]
```

> The diagram emphasizes the central **functional truth**: magnitude is flat (unity) while phase is engineered for specific delay/equalization behavior.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Pole-Zero Mirror Symmetry in All-Pass

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ZPLANE["Z Plane Topology"]
        nodeZ1["Zero at z equals 1 over a"] -.->|"mirror across unit circle"| nodeP1["Pole at z equals a"]
        nodeZ2["Zero at z equals 1 over a star"] -.->|"mirror for conjugate pair"| nodeP2["Pole at z equals a star"]
    end
    nodeP1 --> nodeC["All Pass Function H sub ap of z"]
    nodeP2 --> nodeC
    nodeZ1 --> nodeC
    nodeZ2 --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeR["Magnitude equals 1"]
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Minimum vs Maximum Phase Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["General H of z"] -->|"Factorize"| nodeB["H min of z (all zeros inside UC)"]
    nodeA -->|"Extract excess"| nodeC["H ap of z (all pass)"]
    nodeB --> nodeOut["Product"]
    nodeC --> nodeOut
    nodeOut["H of z equals H min times H ap"]
```

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram — FIR Window Design Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Specifications: omega p, omega s, delta p, delta s"] -->|"Compute delta omega"| nodeB["Transition Width"]
    nodeB -->|"Apply window formula"| nodeC["Filter Order N"]
    nodeC -->|"Choose omega c midpoint"| nodeD["Ideal H sub d of omega"]
    nodeD -->|"Inverse DTFT"| nodeE["Ideal h sub d of n"]
    nodeE -->|"Multiply by w of n"| nodeF["Truncated h of n"]
    nodeF -->|"DTFT"| nodeG["Final H of e sup j omega"]
    nodeG -->|"Validate ripple and transition"| nodeH["Meets Specs"]
```

---

## 4.5 Comparison Matrix — Window Trade-offs Visual Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Rectangular Window"] -->|"side lobe 13 dB"| nodeX["Poor Stopband"]
    nodeA -->|"main lobe 4 pi by N"| nodeY["Sharp Transition"]
    nodeB["Hanning Window"] -->|"side lobe 31 dB"| nodeZ["Good Stopband"]
    nodeB -->|"main lobe 8 pi by N"| nodeW["Wider Transition"]
    nodeC["Hamming Window"] -->|"side lobe 41 dB"| nodeZ
    nodeC -->|"main lobe 8 pi by N"| nodeW
```

---

## 4.6 Block Diagram — Frequency Domain Convolution View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["H sub d of omega ideal brick wall"] -->|"Convolve with"| nodeB["W of e sup j omega window spectrum"]
    nodeB -->|"Yields"| nodeC["H of e sup j omega actual filter"]
    nodeC -->|"Has Gibbs ripple near discontinuity"| nodeD["Transition Band"]
    nodeC -->|"Ripple amplitude depends on side lobe level"| nodeE["Passband and Stopband Ripple"]
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — 3 Mark Questions

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define an all-pass filter. State any two of its properties.**

**Model Answer:**

An all-pass filter is a system whose magnitude response is **unity for all frequencies**, i.e., $\vert H_{ap}(e^{j\omega}) \vert = 1, \forall \omega$.

**Properties:**
1. Poles and zeros occur in **mirror-image pairs** with respect to the unit circle. If $z = a$ is a pole, then $z = 1/a$ is a zero.
2. The group delay $\tau_g(\omega)$ of an all-pass filter is always **non-negative** for all $\omega$.
3. It is used as a **phase equalizer** to correct non-linear phase introduced by other filters.

*(3 marks)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Compare Rectangular and Hamming windows in terms of main-lobe width and side-lobe level.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Rectangular | Hamming |
|---|---|---|
| Main-lobe width | $4\pi/N$ | $8\pi/N$ |
| Peak side-lobe level | **−13 dB** | **−41 dB** |
| Transition band | Sharper | Wider |
| Passband ripple | Higher | Lower |

The Hamming window offers **significantly lower side-lobe level (better ripple)** at the cost of a **wider main-lobe (slower transition)**.

*(3 marks)*

---

## Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Define a minimum phase system. Show that any non-minimum phase system can be expressed as the cascade of a minimum phase system and an all-pass system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design an FIR low-pass filter using the **Hamming window** with the following specifications:
- Passband edge: $\omega_p = 0.2\pi$
- Stopband edge: $\omega_s = 0.5\pi$
- Plot/Determine $h(n)$ for the obtained filter order. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question A(a)

**Definition:** A causal stable LTI system $H_{min}(z)$ is minimum phase if all its poles and zeros lie **strictly inside the unit circle**, ensuring the inverse system $1/H_{min}(z)$ is also causal and stable.

**Decomposition Proof:**

Let $H(z)$ be a non-minimum phase system with some zeros outside the unit circle. Consider a zero at $z = 1/a_k$ where $\vert a_k \vert < 1$ (so the zero is outside UC). Replace this with a factor:

$$H(z) = H'(z) \cdot \frac{z^{-1} - a_k}{1 - a_k z^{-1}}$$

where $H'(z)$ now has the zero moved **inside** the unit circle at $z = a_k$ (after inversion), and the second factor is a **first-order all-pass** system (its pole is $a_k$ inside UC, and its zero is $1/a_k$ outside UC).

**Verification of all-pass factor:**

$$\left\vert \frac{e^{-j\omega} - a_k}{1 - a_k e^{-j\omega}} \right\vert = 1 \quad \text{(by symmetry proved in Section 3.1)}$$

**Iterating** this process for every zero outside UC:

$$\boxed{H(z) = H_{min}(z) \cdot \prod_k H_{ap,k}(z)}$$

This completes the decomposition. QED.

---

#### Solution to Question A(b)

**Step 1: Specifications**
- $\omega_p = 0.2\pi$
- $\omega_s = 0.5\pi$
- Transition width: $\Delta\omega = \omega_s - \omega_p = 0.3\pi$

**Step 2: Filter Order (Hamming window)**
Using the formula $N \approx 6.6\pi / \Delta\omega$:

$$N \approx \frac{6.6\pi}{0.3\pi} = 22$$

For odd symmetric linear-phase, take $N = 23$. So $\alpha = (N-1)/2 = 11$.

**Step 3: Cutoff frequency**
$$\omega_c = \frac{\omega_p + \omega_s}{2} = \frac{0.2\pi + 0.5\pi}{2} = 0.35\pi$$

**Step 4: Ideal impulse response**
$$h_d(n) = \frac{\sin[\omega_c(n - \alpha)]}{\pi(n - \alpha)}, \quad n = 0, 1, \ldots, 22$$

At $n = 11$: $h_d(11) = \omega_c / \pi = 0.35$.

For $n \neq 11$:

$$h_d(n) = \frac{\sin[0.35\pi(n - 11)]}{\pi(n - 11)}$$

**Step 5: Hamming window coefficients**
$$w(n) = 0.54 - 0.46\cos\left(\frac{2\pi n}{22}\right), \quad 0 \leq n \leq 22$$

Sample values:
| $n$ | $w(n)$ |
|---|---|
| 0 | 0.08 |
| 1 | 0.0949 |
| 5 | 0.3979 |
| 11 | 1.0000 |
| 16 | 0.6021 |
| 21 | 0.0949 |
| 22 | 0.08 |

**Step 6: Compute $h(n) = h_d(n) \cdot w(n)$ for selected samples:**

For $n = 11$:
$$h(11) = 0.35 \times 1.0 = 0.35$$

For $n = 10$: $h_d(10) = \dfrac{\sin(0.35\pi \cdot (-1))}{\pi \cdot (-1)} = \dfrac{\sin(-0.35\pi)}{-\pi} = \dfrac{-\sin(0.35\pi)}{-\pi} = \dfrac{\sin(0.35\pi)}{\pi} \approx 0.2995$
$$h(10) = 0.2995 \times 0.9945 \approx 0.2978$$

**Final transfer function:**
$$H(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{22} h(n) z^{-n}$$

The filter is **symmetric**: $h(n) = h(22 - n)$, ensuring **linear phase**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the frequency response of the **rectangular window** and obtain expressions for its main-lobe width and peak side-lobe level. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design an FIR high-pass filter using the **Hanning window** with:
- Stopband edge: $\omega_s = 0.3\pi$
- Passband edge: $\omega_p = 0.6\pi$
- Compute and tabulate $h(n)$ for $N = 15$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question B(a)

**Step 1: Time-domain definition**
$$w_R(n) = 1, \quad 0 \leq n \leq N-1$$

**Step 2: DTFT calculation**
$$W_R(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} e^{-j\omega n} = \frac{1 - e^{-j\omega N}}{1 - e^{-j\omega}}$$

**Step 3: Algebraic manipulation** (multiply numerator and denominator by $e^{j\omega N/2}$ and $e^{j\omega/2}$ respectively):

$$W_R(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j\omega(N-1)/2} \cdot \frac{\sin(\omega N / 2)}{\sin(\omega / 2)}$$

**Step 4: Magnitude response**
$$\vert W_R(e^{j\omega}) \vert = \left\vert \frac{\sin(\omega N/2)}{\sin(\omega/2)} \right\vert$$

This is the **Dirichlet kernel** (periodic sinc).

**Step 5: Main-lobe width**
First zero occurs at $\omega N/2 = \pm\pi \Rightarrow \omega = \pm 2\pi/N$.
$$\text{Main-lobe width} = \frac{4\pi}{N}$$

**Step 6: Peak side-lobe level**
The first side-lobe peak occurs at approximately $\omega = 3\pi/N$ with magnitude:

$$\vert W_R \vert_{side} \approx \frac{1}{\sin(3\pi/(2N))} \cdot \frac{1}{N/\pi} \approx \frac{1}{0.217} \cdot \frac{\pi}{3\pi/N} \cdot \frac{1}{N} \approx 0.217$$

In decibels:
$$20\log_{10}\left(\frac{0.217}{1.0}\right) \approx \boxed{-13 \text{ dB}}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The numerical evaluation requires asymptotic expansion of $\sin(x) \approx x$ for small $x$, leading to the well-known −13 dB figure.

---

#### Solution to Question B(b)

**Step 1: Parameters**
- $N = 15$, so $\alpha = (N-1)/2 = 7$
- Cutoff (midpoint): $\omega_c = (\omega_s + \omega_p)/2 = 0.45\pi$

**Step 2: High-pass ideal impulse response**
For an ideal HPF:
$$h_d(n) = \frac{\sin[\pi(n - \alpha)] - \sin[\omega_c(n - \alpha)]}{\pi(n - \alpha)}, \quad n \neq \alpha$$
$$h_d(\alpha) = 1 - \frac{\omega_c}{\pi} = 1 - 0.45 = 0.55$$

**Step 3: Compute $h_d(n)$ for selected $n$**

| $n$ | $n-\alpha$ | $h_d(n)$ |
|---|---|---|
| 7 | 0 | 0.55 |
| 0, 14 | $\pm 7$ | $\dfrac{\sin(7\pi) - \sin(3.15\pi)}{7\pi} = \dfrac{0 - \sin(3.15\pi)}{7\pi} \approx \dfrac{0.0314}{21.99} \approx 0.00143$ |
| 4, 10 | $\pm 3$ | $\dfrac{\sin(3\pi) - \sin(1.35\pi)}{3\pi} = \dfrac{0 - (-0.0885)}{3\pi} \approx 0.00940$ |

**Step 4: Hanning window coefficients**
$$w(n) = 0.5 - 0.5\cos\left(\frac{2\pi n}{14}\right)$$

| $n$ | $w(n)$ |
|---|---|
| 0, 14 | 0.0 |
| 1, 13 | 0.0495 |
| 3, 11 | 0.3087 |
| 7 | 1.0000 |

**Step 5: $h(n) = h_d(n) \cdot w(n)$**

| $n$ | $h_d(n)$ | $w(n)$ | $h(n)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.00143 | 0.0 | 0.0000 |
| 3 | 0.00940 | 0.3087 | 0.00290 |
| 4 | 0.00940 | 0.5 | 0.00470 |
| 7 | 0.55 | 1.0 | 0.5500 |
| 10 | 0.00940 | 0.5 | 0.00470 |
| 11 | 0.00940 | 0.3087 | 0.00290 |
| 14 | 0.00143 | 0.0 | 0.0000 |

The filter is symmetric: $h(n) = h(14 - n)$, ensuring **linear phase**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the special case $h_d(\alpha) = \omega_c/\pi$**: When $n = \alpha$, the formula has a $0/0$ indeterminate form. Students lose **2 marks** for not computing the limit correctly.
> 2. **Wrong window length in cosine argument**: The denominator is **$N-1$**, NOT $N$. Many students write $2\pi n/N$ — that's a critical **3-mark deduction** error.
> 3. **Not verifying symmetry**: Always check $h(n) = h(N-1-n)$ before declaring linear phase. Missing this loses **1-2 marks** in viva.
> 4. **Confusing the cutoff with passband edge**: KTU expects $\omega_c = (\omega_p + \omega_s)/2$. Using $\omega_p$ directly is wrong.
> 5. **Forgetting that all-pass and minimum-phase systems are causal & stable**: Examiners frequently deduct **1 mark** if stability conditions ($|a|<1$, all zeros/poles inside UC) are not stated.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist**

- **All-pass:** $\vert H_{ap}(e^{j\omega}) \vert = 1, \forall \omega$ — poles and zeros are **reciprocal** mirrors across the unit circle.
- **First-order real all-pass:** $H_{ap}(z) = (a - z^{-1})/(1 - az^{-1})$ with $\vert a \vert < 1$.
- **Group delay of all-pass** is always **non-negative**.
- **Minimum phase:** all poles and zeros inside the unit circle ⇒ inverse system is also causal/stable.
- **Maximum phase:** all zeros outside the unit circle.
- **Decomposition:** Any $H(z) = H_{min}(z) \cdot H_{ap}(z)$.
- **Energy-delay:** minimum phase concentrates energy in early samples.
- **Rectangular window:** main-lobe $4\pi/N$, side-lobe $-13$ dB. Sharpest transition, worst ripple.
- **Hanning window:** main-lobe $8\pi/N$, side-lobe $-31$ dB. Cosine coefficients $0.5$ and $-0.5$.
- **Hamming window:** main-lobe $8\pi/N$, side-lobe $-41$ dB. Cosine coefficients $0.54$ and $-0.46$.
- **Hamming window order formula:** $N \approx 6.6\pi/\Delta\omega$.
- **Design steps:** Specifications → $N$ from window → $\omega_c$ midpoint → $h_d(n)$ via inverse DTFT → multiply by $w(n)$ → verify linear phase symmetry.
- **LPF ideal impulse:** $h_d(n) = \sin[\omega_c(n-\alpha)] / [\pi(n-\alpha)]$.
- **HPF ideal impulse:** $h_d(n) = \{\sin[\pi(n-\alpha)] - \sin[\omega_c(n-\alpha)]\} / [\pi(n-\alpha)]$.
- **BPF ideal impulse:** $h_d(n) = \{\sin[\omega_2(n-\alpha)] - \sin[\omega_1(n-\alpha)]\} / [\pi(n-\alpha)]$.
- **Time-domain multiplication ↔ Frequency-domain convolution** — this is the root cause of Gibbs phenomenon.
- **Always check** $h(n) = h(N-1-n)$ for **linear phase** in FIR design.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications of DSP - Spectral Analysis of Sinusoidal Signals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST526 - DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Applications of DSP - Spectral Analysis of Sinusoidal Signals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Spectral Analysis** is the process of decomposing a time-domain signal into its constituent frequency components to determine its frequency spectrum. For a discrete-time sinusoidal signal of the form $x(n) = A \cos(\omega_0 n + \theta)$, spectral analysis aims to identify the amplitude $A$, the digital frequency $\omega_0$ (in rad/sample), and the initial phase $\theta$ of the sinusoid from a finite set of sampled observations.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, spectral analysis is performed using the **Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)** — typically implemented via the **Fast Fourier Transform (FFT)** algorithm — operating on a finite-length, windowed segment of the observed signal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 PECST526, Module 2):**
> Spectral analysis of sinusoidal signals is a direct application of the DFT. It deals with detecting, resolving, and estimating the parameters (frequency, amplitude, phase) of sinusoids buried in (or contaminated by) noise, using finite-duration records.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **prism splitting white light into a rainbow** 🌈. Each colour of the rainbow corresponds to a specific frequency of light. Just like the prism tells us *which colours are present and in what intensity*, spectral analysis tells us *which frequencies are present in a signal and with what strength*.

Now imagine you only get to **hold the prism up to a small chunk of light for a tiny window of time** (since we only observe a finite record). Some colours might leak into others, and you might miss a colour if it's exactly between two of your observation "bins." This imperfect observation leads to two key practical problems:
- **Spectral Leakage** → frequencies bleeding into neighbouring bins.
- **Picket-Fence Effect** → a frequency falling exactly between DFT bins is missed or underestimated.

This is exactly the situation engineers face when analyzing finite-duration discrete signals.

### 1.3 Why This Matters in Engineering

Spectral analysis of sinusoids is foundational in:
- **Biomedical Engineering** → ECG/EEG frequency-band identification.
- **Audio Engineering** → pitch detection, equalizer design.
- **Vibration Analysis** → identifying rotating machinery faults (bearings, gearboxes).
- **Radar & Sonar** → Doppler shift estimation.
- **Telecommunications** → carrier recovery, OFDM channel estimation.

Standard metrics used universally: **Sampling Frequency $f_s$** (in **Hz**), **DFT Length $N$** (dimensionless), **Frequency Resolution $\Delta f = f_s / N$** (in **Hz**), and **Spectral Leakage (dB)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Insight**: Spectral analysis answers the question *"What sinusoidal components make up my signal?"* — the DFT is our numerical microscope to see this spectrum.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation

A discrete-time sinusoidal signal is mathematically represented as:

$$x(n) = A \cos(\omega_0 n + \theta)$$

where:
- $A$ = Amplitude (real, positive scalar)
- $\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0 / f_s$ = Normalized digital frequency in **rad/sample**
- $f_0$ = Analog frequency in **Hz**
- $f_s$ = Sampling frequency in **Hz**
- $\theta$ = Initial phase in **radians**
- $n$ = Sample index (integer)

Using Euler's identity, this can be written in complex exponential form:

$$x(n) = \frac{A}{2} e^{j\theta} e^{j\omega_0 n} + \frac{A}{2} e^{-j\theta} e^{-j\omega_0 n}$$

This reveals the **two-sided spectrum** — a real sinusoid corresponds to a pair of complex exponentials at $+\omega_0$ and $-\omega_0$.

### 2.2 The Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)

For an $N$-point observed sequence $x(n)$, the DFT is defined as:

$$X(k) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \, e^{-j 2\pi k n / N} \quad,\quad k = 0, 1, 2, \dots, N-1$$

Equivalently, using the twiddle factor $W_N = e^{-j 2\pi / N}$:

$$X(k) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \, W_N^{kn}$$

The inverse DFT (IDFT) reconstructs the time-domain signal:

$$x(n) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X(k) \, e^{j 2\pi k n / N}$$

### 2.3 DFT of a Pure Cosine (No Windowing Ideal Case)

Consider $x(n) = \cos(\omega_0 n)$ observed for exactly $N$ samples, where $\omega_0 = 2\pi k_0 / N$ for some integer $k_0$. The DFT yields:

$$X(k) = \frac{N}{2} \, \delta(k - k_0) + \frac{N}{2} \, \delta(k - (N - k_0))$$

This means the energy of the cosine is concentrated in **exactly two bins**: $k_0$ and $N - k_0$, each with magnitude $N/2$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This ideal case only works when the signal frequency is **exactly commensurate** with the DFT bins — i.e., the record contains an integer number of complete cycles.

### 2.4 Spectral Leakage (Non-Commensurate Case)

In practice, $\omega_0 \neq 2\pi k_0 / N$ exactly. The signal must be implicitly windowed by a rectangular window of length $N$. The result is **convolution in the frequency domain** between the signal's true spectrum (two impulses) and the window's frequency response (a Dirichlet/sinc-like kernel):

$$X(k) = X_{\text{true}}(k) \ast W(k)$$

This smearing of energy from the true frequency into neighbouring bins is called **spectral leakage**.

### 2.5 Frequency Resolution

The frequency spacing between adjacent DFT bins is the **frequency resolution**:

$$\Delta f = \frac{f_s}{N} \quad [\text{Hz}]$$

$$\Delta \omega = \frac{2\pi}{N} \quad [\text{rad/sample}]$$

To resolve two closely-spaced sinusoids at $f_1$ and $f_2$, we need:

$$\Delta f \leq \vert f_1 - f_2 \vert$$

### 2.6 Picket-Fence Effect

If a sinusoid's frequency falls **between** two DFT bins (i.e., $f_0 \neq k \cdot \Delta f$ for any integer $k$), the DFT samples the smeared spectrum at off-peak points. The peak amplitude is **underestimated**, and energy appears in adjacent bins. This is the **picket-fence effect**.

**Remedy**: **Zero-padding** the signal from $N$ to $N' > N$ points — this interpolates the spectrum but does **NOT** improve true frequency resolution.

### 2.7 Windowing (Mitigating Leakage)

Applying a window $w(n)$ before computing the DFT shapes the leakage pattern. Common windows:

- **Rectangular**: $w(n) = 1$
- **Hanning**: $w(n) = 0.5 \left( 1 - \cos \frac{2\pi n}{N-1} \right)$
- **Hamming**: $w(n) = 0.54 - 0.46 \cos \frac{2\pi n}{N-1}$
- **Blackman**: $w(n) = 0.42 - 0.5 \cos \frac{2\pi n}{N-1} + 0.08 \cos \frac{4\pi n}{N-1}$
- **Kaiser**: $w(n) = I_0\left( \beta \sqrt{1 - (2n/(N-1) - 1)^2} \right) / I_0(\beta)$

Windows trade **main-lobe width** (resolution) for **side-lobe level** (leakage suppression).

### 2.8 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|-------------------|-----------|---------------|
| 1 | DT Sinusoid | $x(n) = A\cos(\omega_0 n + \theta)$ | $n \in \mathbb{Z}$ |
| 2 | Frequency Mapping | $\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0 / f_s$ | rad/sample |
| 3 | DFT | $X(k) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) e^{-j 2\pi kn / N}$ | Complex spectrum |
| 4 | Twiddle Factor | $W_N = e^{-j 2\pi / N}$ | $N$th root of unity |
| 5 | IDFT | $x(n) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X(k) e^{j 2\pi kn / N}$ | Time-domain recovery |
| 6 | Peak Magnitude (Ideal) | $\vert X(k_0)\vert = N/2$ | When $\omega_0 = 2\pi k_0 / N$ |
| 7 | Frequency Resolution | $\Delta f = f_s / N$ | Hz |
| 8 | Spectral Range | $[0, f_s/2]$ by Nyquist | One-sided real spectrum |
| 9 | Rectangular Window | $w(n) = 1,\ 0 \leq n \leq N-1$ | Max resolution, max leakage |
| 10 | Hanning Window | $w(n) = 0.5(1 - \cos \frac{2\pi n}{N-1})$ | Side-lobe $\approx -31$ dB |
| 11 | Hamming Window | $w(n) = 0.54 - 0.46 \cos \frac{2\pi n}{N-1}$ | Side-lobe $\approx -43$ dB |
| 12 | Blackman Window | $w(n) = 0.42 - 0.5\cos\frac{2\pi n}{N-1} + 0.08\cos\frac{4\pi n}{N-1}$ | Side-lobe $\approx -58$ dB |
| 13 | Power Spectrum | $P(k) = \frac{1}{N}\vert X(k)\vert^2$ | Periodogram estimate |
| 14 | Parseval's Theorem | $\sum \vert x(n)\vert^2 = \frac{1}{N}\sum \vert X(k)\vert^2$ | Energy conservation |
| 15 | Zero-padding effect | Interpolation only, no new info | Improves display, not resolution |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic** for window trade-off: *"Wider main-lobe → Better side-lobe suppression → Lower resolution but less leakage."*

### 2.9 Real-World Utility

| Field | Use Case |
|-------|----------|
| Power Systems | Harmonic analysis in 50/60 Hz mains |
| Audio | Pitch detection in music, speech formant analysis |
| Biomedical | Heart-rate variability (HRV), EEG bands ($\delta, \theta, \alpha, \beta$) |
| Mechanical | Modal analysis of vibrating structures |
| Comms | Carrier offset estimation, Doppler tracking |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: DFT of a Pure Cosine (Ideal Commensurate Case)

**Statement**: Given $x(n) = \cos\left(\frac{2\pi k_0}{N} n\right)$ for $n = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$, show that $X(k) = \frac{N}{2}\left[\delta(k - k_0) + \delta(k - (N - k_0))\right]$.

**Step 1**: Express the cosine using Euler's formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
x(n) &= \cos\left(\frac{2\pi k_0}{N} n\right) \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{2}\left[e^{j 2\pi k_0 n / N} + e^{-j 2\pi k_0 n / N}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2**: Substitute into the DFT definition.

$$
\begin{aligned}
X(k) &= \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \, e^{-j 2\pi k n / N} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{2}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} e^{j 2\pi k_0 n / N} e^{-j 2\pi k n / N} + \frac{1}{2}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} e^{-j 2\pi k_0 n / N} e^{-j 2\pi k n / N} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{2}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} e^{j 2\pi (k_0 - k) n / N} + \frac{1}{2}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} e^{-j 2\pi (k_0 + k) n / N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3**: Apply the geometric sum identity.

Recall:
$$
\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} e^{j 2\pi m n / N} = 
\begin{cases}
N, & m = qN \text{ for some integer } q \\
0, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

For the first sum, the exponential is $1$ (i.e., equals $N$) when $(k_0 - k) = qN \Rightarrow k = k_0 - qN$. Modulo $N$, this means $k = k_0$ (principal value).

For the second sum, the exponential is $1$ when $(k_0 + k) = qN \Rightarrow k = -k_0 \equiv (N - k_0) \pmod{N}$.

**Step 4**: Conclude the result.

$$
\boxed{X(k) = \frac{N}{2}\,\delta(k - k_0) + \frac{N}{2}\,\delta(k - (N - k_0))}
$$

This is the key theoretical result — energy of the cosine appears **only** at bins $k_0$ and $N - k_0$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Effect of Zero-Padding on Spectral Resolution

**Statement**: Show that zero-padding an $N$-point signal to $N' = 2N$ points **interpolates** the spectrum but does not change frequency resolution.

**Step 1**: Original DFT at bin $k$:
$$
X_N(k) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) e^{-j 2\pi k n / N}, \quad k = 0, 1, \dots, N-1
$$

**Step 2**: Zero-padded sequence $x'(n)$:
$$
x'(n) = 
\begin{cases} x(n), & 0 \leq n \leq N-1 \\ 0, & N \leq n \leq 2N - 1 \end{cases}
$$

**Step 3**: New DFT at bin $m$ (for $m = 0, 1, \dots, 2N-1$):
$$
X_{2N}(m) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) e^{-j 2\pi m n / (2N)}
$$

**Step 4**: Substitution — let $k = m/2$. For even $m$:
$$
X_{2N}(2k) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) e^{-j 2\pi k n / N} = X_N(k)
$$

So **even-indexed bins of $X_{2N}$ exactly equal $X_N$**, and the odd-indexed bins provide interpolated values in between — no new frequency information is gained, but the spectrum looks "smoother."

$$
\boxed{\text{True Resolution} = \frac{f_s}{N}, \quad \text{Apparent Resolution} = \frac{f_s}{2N}}
$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem**: A signal $x(n) = 2\cos(0.4\pi n)$ is sampled for $N = 10$ points. Compute its 10-point DFT.

**Step 1**: Identify frequency and bin correspondence.

$$
\omega_0 = 0.4\pi = \frac{2\pi k_0}{N} \Rightarrow k_0 = \frac{\omega_0 N}{2\pi} = \frac{0.4\pi \cdot 10}{2\pi} = 2
$$

So $k_0 = 2$ (integer — ideal commensurate case).

**Step 2**: Apply the formula.

$$
X(k) = \frac{N}{2}\left[\delta(k - 2) + \delta(k - 8)\right] = 5\left[\delta(k - 2) + \delta(k - 8)\right]
$$

**Step 3**: Verify by direct computation at $k = 2$.

$$
X(2) = \sum_{n=0}^{9} 2\cos(0.4\pi n) e^{-j 2\pi \cdot 2 \cdot n / 10}
$$

Using the identity:
$$
2\cos(0.4\pi n) = e^{j 0.4\pi n} + e^{-j 0.4\pi n}
$$

Then:
$$
\begin{aligned}
X(2) &= \sum_{n=0}^{9} \left(e^{j 0.4\pi n} + e^{-j 0.4\pi n}\right) e^{-j 0.4\pi n} \\
&= \sum_{n=0}^{9} 1 + \sum_{n=0}^{9} e^{-j 0.8\pi n} \\
&= 10 + 0 = 10 \quad (\text{second sum } = 0 \text{ for } N=10, \text{ since } 0.8\pi = 2\pi \cdot 4/10)
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait — let me recheck. For $N = 10$, second sum vanishes when $4$ is not a multiple of $10$. So $\sum e^{-j 2\pi \cdot 4 n / 10} = 0$. Hence $X(2) = 10$. ✓

**Final DFT magnitudes**:
$$
\vert X(0)\vert = 0,\ \vert X(1)\vert = 0,\ \vert X(2)\vert = 10,\ \ldots,\ \vert X(8)\vert = 10,\ \vert X(9)\vert = 0
$$

Amplitude of original cosine = $\vert X(k_0)\vert \cdot 2/N = 10 \cdot 2 / 10 = 2$. ✓

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Windows Trade-off

| Window | Main-lobe Width (bins) | Side-lobe Level (dB) | Use Case |
|--------|------------------------|----------------------|----------|
| Rectangular | $2$ | $-13$ | Strong known sinusoids |
| Hanning | $4$ | $-31$ | General purpose |
| Hamming | $4$ | $-43$ | Speech processing |
| Blackman | $6$ | $-58$ | Detecting weak tones near strong ones |
| Kaiser ($\beta = 8$) | Adjustable | Adjustable | Tunable for specific need |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Spectral Analyzer

The following Mermaid block diagram describes the canonical signal flow of a digital spectral analyzer (as implemented in any KTU-lab DSP experiment or industrial DSP chip).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Analog Input Signal xt] --> A1[Anti-Aliasing Low-Pass Filter]
    A1 --> A2[ADC Sampler fs Hz]
    A2 --> A3[Finite Buffer N samples xn]
    A3 --> A4[Windowing Stage wn * xn]
    A4 --> A5[Zero-Padding Optional N to M]
    A5 --> A6[FFT Algorithm M-point]
    A6 --> A7[Magnitude Spectrum Xk]
    A6 --> A8[Phase Spectrum angle Xk]
    A6 --> A9[Power Spectrum Pk]
    A7 --> A10[Spectral Display / Decision Logic]
    A8 --> A10
    A9 --> A10

    style A0 fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style A1 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#ef6c00
    style A2 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#ef6c00
    style A6 fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a,stroke-width:2px
    style A10 fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px
```

**Block Descriptions**:

- **Anti-Aliasing LPF**: Removes frequencies above $f_s / 2$ to prevent aliasing.
- **ADC**: Samples at $f_s$ Hz, producing $x(n)$.
- **Windowing Stage**: Multiplies by window $w(n)$ to control leakage.
- **Zero-Padding**: Pads with zeros to next power-of-2 length for FFT efficiency and display interpolation.
- **FFT**: Computes $X(k)$ efficiently in $O(N \log N)$.
- **Output Stages**: Display, peak detection, parameter estimation.

---

### 4.2 Flowchart: Decision Logic for Choosing Window & DFT Length

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B0[Start: Define Signal Properties] --> B1{Frequency of interest known?}
    B1 -- Yes --> B2[Choose N to give adequate Delta f]
    B1 -- No --> B3[Use larger N for finer resolution]
    B2 --> B4{Multiple close tones?}
    B3 --> B4
    B4 -- Yes --> B5[Use Rectangular or Hamming]
    B4 -- No --> B6[Use Hanning or Blackman]
    B5 --> B7[Apply Window]
    B6 --> B7
    B7 --> B8[Zero-pad if visual smoothness needed]
    B8 --> B9[Compute FFT]
    B9 --> B10{Leakage still visible?}
    B10 -- Yes --> B11[Use window with lower side-lobe]
    B10 -- No --> B12[Extract Peak Frequency and Amplitude]
    B11 --> B9

    style B0 fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style B12 fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Time-Frequency Visualization (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1[Time Domain: xn = A cos omega0 n + theta] --> C2[DFT Transform]
    C2 --> C3[Frequency Domain: Two impulses at plus minus omega0]
    C1 -.Windowed.-> C4[Windowed xn]
    C4 --> C2
    C2 -.Leakage.-.-> C5[Smearing of impulses to sinc-shaped lobes]

    style C1 fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style C3 fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px
    style C5 fill:#ffebee,stroke:#c62828
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram above is a conceptual block-architecture mapping the *interactions* between time-domain windowing and frequency-domain leakage effects, in lieu of literal graphical plots.

---
