> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST526 - DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Computational Accuracy in DSP Implementation - Number Formats for Signals and Coefficients in DSP Systems, Dynamic Range and Precision, Sources of Error in DSP Implementation (A/D Conversion Error, DSP Computational Error, D/A Conversion Error)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Computational Accuracy in DSP?

**Computational Accuracy in DSP** refers to the fidelity with which a real-world signal is represented, processed, and reproduced by a digital signal processing system. Since real signals are continuous (analog) but digital hardware operates using discrete (quantized) numbers, errors are *inevitable* at every stage of the DSP chain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Spirit):**
> Computational accuracy is the measure of how closely the output of a finite-precision digital system matches the output of an ideal infinite-precision system when the same input is applied.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy: The "Rupee & Paise" System

Imagine you are a shopkeeper. You have a "register" that can only hold whole rupees (no paise). When a customer gives you ₹99.76, you **round it** to ₹100. The error (₹0.24) is unavoidable. If the register could hold 2 decimal places (₹99.76 exact), the error would be zero. The more "slots" your register has, the more accurate you are.

In DSP:
- The **rupee register** is your *number format* (Fixed-point, Floating-point).
- The **number of slots** is your *word-length* (number of bits: 8, 16, 24, 32-bit).
- The **rounding error** is *quantization error*.

---

## 1.3 Number Formats for Signals and Coefficients

DSP systems can represent numbers in two principal formats:

### (a) Fixed-Point Representation
A number $x$ is represented as:
$$x = \pm \left( b_0 . b_{-1} b_{-2} \ldots b_{-B} \right)_{2} \times 2^{-B}$$
where the **binary point position is fixed**, and the scale factor $2^{-B}$ is constant. The number of fractional bits $B$ determines the precision (smallest representable step $= 2^{-B}$).

**Example:** In Q15 format (16-bit signed, 1 sign bit + 15 fractional bits), the value $0.5$ is stored as `0x4000` (binary: `0100 0000 0000 0000`).

### (b) Floating-Point Representation
A number $x$ is represented as:
$$x = \pm M \times 2^{E}$$
where $M$ is the **mantissa** (significand) in normalized form $1 \leq M < 2$, and $E$ is the **exponent**. Standard: **IEEE 754 single precision** uses 32 bits (1 sign + 8 exponent + 23 mantissa).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard IEEE 754 Single Precision (32-bit):**
> - Sign: 1 bit
> - Exponent: 8 bits (biased by +127)
> - Mantissa: 23 bits (with implicit leading 1)
> - Effective precision: 24 bits

### (c) Block Floating-Point
A hybrid: a block of numbers shares a common exponent, but each mantissa is stored in fixed-point. Used in FFT implementations to save memory.

---

## 1.4 Dynamic Range

**Dynamic Range (DR)** is the ratio between the largest and smallest representable magnitudes of a number in a given format.

$$\text{DR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{\vert x_{\max} \vert}{\vert x_{\min} \vert}\right)$$

- **Fixed-point (b+1 bits):** $\text{DR} \approx 6.02 \cdot b$ dB
- **Floating-point (IEEE 754 single):** $\text{DR} \approx 1500$ dB (extremely wide)

---

## 1.5 Precision

**Precision** is the smallest resolvable difference between two adjacent representable numbers (the *quantization step* $\Delta$).

$$\Delta = 2^{-B} \quad \text{(fixed-point with B fractional bits)}$$

For floating-point, precision is **relative** — the step size scales with the magnitude of the number itself.

---

## 1.6 Sources of Error in DSP Implementation

A typical real-time DSP system pipeline looks like:

> **Analog Input → Anti-Alias Filter → A/D Converter → Digital Processor → D/A Converter → Reconstruction Filter → Analog Output**

Errors can enter at **three critical points**:

| # | Error Source | Origin |
|---|--------------|--------|
| 1 | **A/D Conversion Error** | Quantization of continuous-amplitude samples to discrete levels |
| 2 | **DSP Computational Error** | Finite word-length effects in arithmetic operations (multiplication, addition) |
| 3 | **D/A Conversion Error** | Imperfect reconstruction; quantization in output stage; zero-order hold errors |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Pearl:**
> The three error sources are **independent but compounding** — the total output noise is roughly the RSS (root-sum-square) of the individual noise contributions.

---

## 1.7 Why This Matters (Engineering Context)

- **Audio codecs (MP3, AAC):** Fixed-point DSP is used in 16-bit to minimize cost.
- **Radar / medical imaging (MRI, CT):** Floating-point DSP is mandatory for high dynamic range (signals from $10^{-6}$ to $10^{+6}$).
- **5G base stations:** Use 16-bit fixed-point for energy efficiency, with dithering techniques to mask quantization noise.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fixed-Point Arithmetic: Mechanics

### 2.1.1 Representation Forms
| Format | Notation | Range | Smallest Step |
|--------|----------|-------|---------------|
| Integer (b-bit) | `b0 ... b_b-1` | $[-2^{b-1}, 2^{b-1}-1]$ | $1$ |
| Fractional (Q-format) | Q15, Q31 | $[-1, 1-2^{-15}]$ | $2^{-15}$ |
| Mixed | Qm.n | $[-2^m, 2^m - 2^{-n}]$ | $2^{-n}$ |

**Qm.n format:** $1$ sign bit, $m$ integer bits, $n$ fractional bits → total $1+m+n$ bits.

### 2.1.2 Multiplication Rule
Multiplying two Qm.n numbers yields a Q(2m+1).(2n) number — **always double the word length**. The result is then **truncated or rounded** back to fit the register (e.g., shifted right by $n$ bits, discarding low-order bits).

$$\text{Product: } (x \cdot y)_{\text{stored}} = \text{truncate}\left[\frac{x \cdot y}{2^n}\right]$$

### 2.1.3 Addition Rule
Adding two Qm.n numbers may produce an **overflow** if the sum exceeds the representable range. Overflow causes **wrap-around** (in 2's complement) or **saturation** (in DSP chips with guard bits).

---

## 2.2 Floating-Point Arithmetic: Mechanics

### 2.2.1 IEEE 754 Single-Precision Layout
- **Sign (s):** 1 bit
- **Biased Exponent (E = e + 127):** 8 bits
- **Mantissa (fraction f):** 23 bits

Stored value:
$$x = (-1)^s \times 2^{E-127} \times (1.f)_{\text{binary}}$$

### 2.2.2 Multiplication
$$x \cdot y = (M_x \cdot M_y) \times 2^{E_x + E_y}$$
- Result mantissa must be **renormalized** (back to $[1, 2)$).
- Exponent bias correction: subtract 127 from the sum.

### 2.2.3 Addition
Requires **alignment**: the smaller exponent's mantissa is right-shifted by the exponent difference before adding. This is the **slowest** floating-point operation.

---

## 2.3 Dynamic Range: Complete Derivation Setup

### 2.3.1 Fixed-Point Dynamic Range
For a $(b+1)$-bit signed fixed-point number in Q-format with $B$ fractional bits:
- Maximum value: $x_{\max} = 2^{b-B} - 2^{-B}$
- Minimum non-zero (positive) value: $x_{\min} = 2^{-B}$

$$\text{DR}_{\text{FP}} = \frac{x_{\max}}{x_{\min}} \approx 2^b$$

In decibels:
$$\boxed{\text{DR}_{\text{FP, dB}} \approx 6.02 \cdot b \;\; \text{dB}}$$

### 2.3.2 Floating-Point Dynamic Range
For IEEE 754 single precision:
- $E_{\max} = 254$ (effective), $E_{\min} = 1$
- $x_{\max} \approx 2^{+127}$, $x_{\min} \approx 2^{-126}$

$$\text{DR}_{\text{float}} = 2^{127 - (-126)} = 2^{253} \approx 1.5 \times 10^{76}$$

$$\text{DR}_{\text{float, dB}} = 20 \log_{10}(2^{253}) = 253 \times 6.02 \approx 1523 \;\text{dB}$$

---

## 2.4 Precision: Complete Picture

### 2.4.1 Absolute Precision (Fixed-Point)
$$\Delta = 2^{-B} \quad \text{(constant for entire range)}$$

### 2.4.2 Relative Precision (Floating-Point)
The mantissa has $p$ significant bits (24 for single precision), giving:
$$\epsilon_{\text{rel}} \approx 2^{-(p-1)} = 2^{-23} \approx 1.19 \times 10^{-7}$$

The **absolute step** at any value $x$ is:
$$\Delta(x) \approx \epsilon_{\text{rel}} \cdot \vert x \vert = 2^{-23} \cdot \vert x \vert$$

This is why floating-point is **uniformly precise** across all magnitudes.

---

## 2.5 Sources of Error: Three-Stage Mathematical Models

### 2.5.1 A/D Conversion Error (Input Quantization Noise)
- Assume uniform quantization in the range $[-V, V]$ with $B$ bits.
- Quantization step: $\Delta = \frac{2V}{2^B} = V \cdot 2^{1-B}$
- Quantization error $e(n)$ modeled as **additive white noise** uniformly distributed on $[-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2]$.
- Variance (mean-square error): 
$$\sigma_e^2 = \frac{\Delta^2}{12} = \frac{V^2 \cdot 2^{2-2B}}{12} = \frac{V^2}{3 \cdot 2^{2B}}$$

- **Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR):**
$$\boxed{\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 6.02\,B + 4.77 - 20\log_{10}\left(\frac{V_{\text{peak}}}{V_{\text{rms}}}\right)}$$

For a full-scale sinusoid (worst case), $V_{\text{peak}} / V_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{2}$:
$$\text{SQNR}_{\text{sinusoid}} = 6.02\,B + 1.76 \;\text{dB}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Heuristic Rule (KTU Favorite):** Every additional bit in the A/D converter **adds ~6 dB of SQNR**.

### 2.5.2 DSP Computational Error
Three sub-phenomena:

**(a) Coefficient Quantization Error:**
Filter coefficients $h[k]$ are quantized to finite word-length. The actual filter deviates from the ideal. Severity depends on filter **coefficient sensitivity**, which is high for **narrow-band (high-Q) IIR filters** near $z = 1$.

**(b) Product Quantization Error (Round-off):**
Each multiplication introduces a rounding error of magnitude $\leq \Delta/2$. For an FIR filter:
$$\sigma_{y,\text{noise}}^2 = N \cdot \sigma_{\text{round}}^2 = N \cdot \frac{\Delta^2}{12}$$
where $N$ is the number of taps.

**(c) Overflow / Limit Cycles (IIR only):**
Recursive filters may oscillate at zero input due to overflow in the accumulator — called **granular limit cycles** or **overflow limit cycles**.

### 2.5.3 D/A Conversion Error
- **Zero-Order Hold (ZOH) Distortion:** The D/A holds the output constant between samples, introducing a $(\sin x / x)$ frequency response droop:
$$H_{\text{ZOH}}(f) = \frac{\sin(\pi f / f_s)}{\pi f / f_s}$$
- **Finite DAC Resolution:** Same quantization noise model as A/D, with $\sigma_{\text{DAC}}^2 = \Delta^2 / 12$.
- **Glitch Impulses:** During bit transitions in the DAC, transient spikes occur.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Master Table)

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Quantization Step | $\Delta = V \cdot 2^{1-B}$ | Volts, $B$ = bits |
| 2 | A/D Noise Variance | $\sigma_e^2 = \Delta^2 / 12$ | Mean-square error |
| 3 | SQNR (sinusoid) | $6.02\,B + 1.76$ | dB |
| 4 | SQNR (general) | $6.02\,B + 4.77 - 20\log_{10}(V_p/V_{\text{rms}})$ | dB |
| 5 | Fixed-Point DR | $6.02 \cdot b$ dB | $b$ = total bits |
| 6 | Float-Point DR (single) | $\approx 1523$ dB | IEEE 754 |
| 7 | FIR Output Round-off Var | $N \cdot \Delta^2 / 12$ | $N$ = taps |
| 8 | ZOH Frequency Response | $\text{sinc}(\pi f / f_s)$ | — |
| 9 | Relative Precision (Float) | $2^{-(p-1)}$ | $p$ = mantissa bits |
| 10 | Q-format Range | $[-2^m, \; 2^m - 2^{-n}]$ | Q$m.n$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Trick (KTU):** For "find SQNR" questions with $B = 12$ bits, compute $6.02 \times 12 + 1.76 = 74.0$ dB. No need to memorize — derive it!

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Telephony (8-bit $\mu$-law PCM):** Uses companding to maintain SQNR across the dynamic range of speech.
- **Audio CDs (16-bit linear PCM):** 98 dB SQNR — covers the entire human auditory range.
- **Geophone Seismic Sensors:** 24-bit $\Sigma$-$\Delta$ ADCs achieve ~146 dB DR, capturing faint micro-tremors alongside large earthquakes.
- **DSP Chips (TMS320, SHARC):** Modern fixed-point DSPs (e.g., TMS320C55x) and floating-point DSPs (e.g., SHARC ADSP-21489) are designed with guard bits and saturation logic to suppress overflow.
---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Quantization Step $\Delta$ for a B-bit A/D Converter

**Problem Setup:** A signed A/D converter spans the range $[-V, +V]$. With $B$ bits, we have $2^B$ discrete levels.

**Step 1 — Total number of levels:**
The number of distinct quantization levels is:
$$L = 2^B$$
because each of the $B$ bits independently takes 2 values.

**Step 2 — Total range:**
The total voltage span from $-V$ to $+V$ is:
$$\text{Span} = (+V) - (-V) = 2V$$

**Step 3 — Size of one level (quantization step):**
The quantization step is the total range divided by the number of levels:
$$\Delta = \frac{2V}{2^B} = 2V \cdot 2^{-B} = V \cdot 2^{1-B}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**
For $V = 5\,V$ and $B = 8$ bits:
$$\Delta = 5 \times 2^{1-8} = 5 \times 2^{-7} = 5 / 128 \approx 0.0391 \;\text{V}$$

So each code-step represents about 39 mV — small, but finite.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: A/D Conversion Quantization Noise Variance

**Assumption:** The quantization error $e(n) = x_Q(n) - x(n)$ is uniformly distributed over one quantization interval $[-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2]$ and uncorrelated with the signal.

**Step 1 — PDF of error:**
$$p(e) = \frac{1}{\Delta}, \quad e \in [-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2]$$

**Step 2 — Mean (zero by symmetry):**
$$\mu_e = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e \cdot p(e) \, de = \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \left[\frac{e^2}{2}\right]_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} = \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot 0 = 0$$

**Step 3 — Mean-square (variance):**
$$\sigma_e^2 = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e^2 \cdot p(e) \, de = \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \left[\frac{e^3}{3}\right]_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2}$$

$$\sigma_e^2 = \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot \left[\left(\frac{\Delta}{2}\right)^3 - \left(-\frac{\Delta}{2}\right)^3\right] = \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot 2 \cdot \frac{\Delta^3}{8}$$

$$\sigma_e^2 = \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \frac{\Delta^3}{12} = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $\Delta$ in terms of $V$ and $B$:**
$$\sigma_e^2 = \frac{(V \cdot 2^{1-B})^2}{12} = \frac{V^2 \cdot 2^{2-2B}}{12} = \frac{V^2}{3 \cdot 2^{2B}}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical check (B = 16, V = 5 V):**
$$\sigma_e^2 = \frac{25}{3 \cdot 2^{32}} \approx \frac{25}{1.288 \times 10^{10}} \approx 1.94 \times 10^{-9} \;\text{V}^2$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: SQNR for a Full-Scale Sinusoid

**Step 1 — Signal power:**
A full-scale sinusoid $x(t) = V \sin(\omega t)$ has RMS value:
$$V_{\text{rms}} = \frac{V}{\sqrt{2}}$$
Signal power: $P_s = V_{\text{rms}}^2 = V^2 / 2$

**Step 2 — Noise power:**
$$P_n = \sigma_e^2 = \frac{V^2}{3 \cdot 2^{2B}}$$

**Step 3 — Ratio:**
$$\text{SQNR} = \frac{P_s}{P_n} = \frac{V^2 / 2}{V^2 / (3 \cdot 2^{2B})} = \frac{3 \cdot 2^{2B}}{2} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot 2^{2B} = 1.5 \cdot 4^B$$

**Step 4 — Convert to dB:**
$$\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(1.5) + 10 \log_{10}(4^B) = 10 \log_{10}(1.5) + 10B \log_{10}(4)$$

Using $10 \log_{10}(1.5) = 1.76$ and $10 \log_{10}(4) = 6.02$:
$$\boxed{\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 6.02\,B + 1.76}$$

**Step 5 — Sanity check:** For $B = 16$, SQNR $= 96.32 + 1.76 = 98.08$ dB — matches the 16-bit audio CD standard (~98 dB). ✓

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Fixed-Point Dynamic Range (b-bit signed)

**Setup:** A signed $(b+1)$-bit fixed-point number uses 1 sign bit and $b$ magnitude bits.

**Step 1 — Maximum positive value:**
In Q-format with all integer bits set: $x_{\max} = 2^{b-B} - 2^{-B} \approx 2^{b-B}$.

**Step 2 — Minimum positive value:**
Smallest representable non-zero: $x_{\min} = 2^{-B}$.

**Step 3 — Ratio:**
$$\text{DR} = \frac{x_{\max}}{x_{\min}} = \frac{2^{b-B}}{2^{-B}} = 2^b$$

**Step 4 — In dB:**
$$\text{DR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}(2^b) = 20b \log_{10}(2) = 20b \times 0.3010 = 6.02\,b \;\text{dB}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example (b = 15, 16-bit signed Q15):**
$$\text{DR} = 2^{15} = 32768 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{DR}_{\text{dB}} = 90.3 \;\text{dB}$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Floating-Point Dynamic Range (IEEE 754 Single)

**Step 1 — Exponent range:**
- 8-bit exponent (excluding 0 and 255): $E_{\min} = 1, \; E_{\max} = 254$.
- After bias removal: $e_{\min} = 1 - 127 = -126$, $e_{\max} = 254 - 127 = +127$.

**Step 2 — Maximum value:**
$$x_{\max} = (2 - 2^{-23}) \times 2^{+127} \approx 2^{+128} \approx 3.4 \times 10^{38}$$

**Step 3 — Minimum positive normal value:**
$$x_{\min} = 1.0 \times 2^{-126} \approx 1.18 \times 10^{-38}$$

**Step 4 — Dynamic range:**
$$\text{DR} = \frac{x_{\max}}{x_{\min}} \approx \frac{2^{128}}{2^{-126}} = 2^{254} \approx 2.3 \times 10^{76}$$

$$\text{DR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}(2^{254}) = 254 \times 6.02 \approx 1529 \;\text{dB}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Quick Mnemonic:** 32-bit float gives roughly **1500 dB DR** — about 17× the dynamic range of 16-bit audio (90 dB).

---

## 3.6 Derivation: FIR Filter Output Round-off Noise Variance

**Setup:** An FIR filter of order $N-1$ computes:
$$y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h(k) \cdot x(n-k)$$

**Step 1 — Each multiplication produces a round-off error $e_k$**, modeled as white noise of variance $\sigma_e^2 = \Delta^2/12$.

**Step 2 — Total output error $e_y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} e_k$** (errors add linearly at the accumulator).

**Step 3 — Output noise variance (errors uncorrelated):**
$$\sigma_{y}^2 = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \sigma_e^2 = N \cdot \frac{\Delta^2}{12}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example:** $N = 64$, $B = 16$ bits, $\Delta = 2^{-15}$:
$$\sigma_y^2 = 64 \cdot \frac{2^{-30}}{12} = \frac{64}{12} \cdot 9.31 \times 10^{-10} \approx 4.97 \times 10^{-9}$$

This represents a noise floor of about $2^{-15} \sqrt{64/12} = 2^{-13.15} \approx 0.00011$ LSBs RMS.

---

## 3.7 Derivation: D/A Zero-Order Hold (ZOH) Frequency Response

**Step 1 — Time-domain model:**
The ZOH holds each sample value $x(nT)$ constant for the duration $T$:
$$y(t) = x(nT), \quad nT \leq t < (n+1)T$$

**Step 2 — Impulse response:**
$$h_{\text{ZOH}}(t) = u(t) - u(t-T)$$

**Step 3 — Fourier transform:**
$$H_{\text{ZOH}}(f) = \int_0^T e^{-j2\pi f t} \, dt = \frac{1 - e^{-j2\pi f T}}{j 2\pi f} = e^{-j\pi f T} \cdot \frac{\sin(\pi f T)}{\pi f}$$

**Step 4 — Magnitude response:**
$$\vert H_{\text{ZOH}}(f) \vert = \left\vert \frac{\sin(\pi f T)}{\pi f T} \right\vert \cdot T = T \cdot \text{sinc}\left(\frac{f}{f_s}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Effect:** At $f = f_s/2$ (Nyquist), the droop is:
$$\text{sinc}(0.5) = \frac{\sin(\pi/2)}{\pi/2} = \frac{2}{\pi} \approx 0.637$$
i.e., a **−3.92 dB attenuation** at Nyquist — must be compensated by an **equalization filter** in high-fidelity audio DACs.
---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level DSP Pipeline with Error Injection Points

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Analog Input Signal] --> B[Anti-Aliasing Filter]
    B --> C[A or D Converter]
    C -->|Error1 A D Quantization Noise| D[Digital Signal Processor]
    D -->|Error2 Finite Word Length Round Off and Overflow| E[D or A Converter]
    E -->|Error3 Zero Order Hold Glitch and Quantization| F[Reconstruction Filter]
    F --> G[Analog Output Signal]
    H[DC Power and Clock] --> C
    H --> D
    H --> E
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The `A or D Converter` block introduces **Error 1** (quantization of amplitude).
- The `Digital Signal Processor` block is the site of **Error 2** (round-off in multiplications, coefficient quantization).
- The `D or A Converter` block is the site of **Error 3** (ZOH droop, output quantization, glitches).

---

## 4.2 Fixed-Point vs Floating-Point: Architectural Block Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FP[Fixed Point System]
        F1[Input Sample x of n] --> F2[Q format Scaling]
        F2 --> F3[Multiplier and Accumulator MAC]
        F3 --> F4[Truncate or Round to B bits]
        F4 --> F5[Detect Overflow and Saturate]
        F5 --> F6[Output y of n]
    end

    subgraph FL[Floating Point System]
        L1[Input Sample x of n] --> L2[Exponent and Mantissa Split]
        L2 --> L3[Align Exponents for Add]
        L3 --> L4[Multiply Mantissas and Add Exponents]
        L4 --> L5[Renormalize Mantissa and Adjust Exponent]
        L5 --> L6[Round Mantissa to 23 bits]
        L6 --> L7[Output y of n]
    end
```

---

## 4.3 Error Propagation Cascade (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Source Signal s of n] --> ADD1{Adder}
    N1[Quantization Noise e1] --> ADD1
    ADD1 --> X[Digital Samples x of n]
    X --> H[Filter h of n]
    H --> ADD2{Adder}
    N2[Round Off Noise e2] --> ADD2
    ADD2 --> Y[Processed Samples y of n]
    Y --> ADD3{Adder}
    N3[ZOH and DAC Noise e3] --> ADD3
    ADD3 --> OUT[Analog Output y of t]
```

**Block-Level Functional Architecture Description:**

| Stage | Block | Input | Output | Noise Added |
|-------|-------|-------|--------|-------------|
| 1 | Anti-Alias + ADC | $x_a(t)$ | $x(n)$ | $e_1$ (A/D quant.) |
| 2 | DSP Core (MAC) | $x(n)$ | $y(n)$ | $e_2$ (round-off) |
| 3 | DAC + Reconstruction | $y(n)$ | $y_a(t)$ | $e_3$ (ZOH + quant.) |

Total output noise (assuming independent sources):
$$\sigma_{\text{total}}^2 = \sigma_{e_1}^2 + \sigma_{e_2}^2 + \sigma_{e_3}^2 \;\;\text{(RSS combination)}$$

---

## 4.4 Dynamic Range Visualization (Number-Line Concept)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A[16 bit Fixed Point Q15]
        A1[Negative Full Scale 1.0 LSB] --> A2[Zero 0.0]
        A2 --> A3[Positive Full Scale 0.99997 LSB]
    end

    subgraph B[32 bit IEEE 754 Float]
        B1[Smallest Normal 1.18e minus 38] --> B2[Zero 0.0]
        B2 --> B3[Largest 3.4e plus 38]
    end

    A3 -.comparison.- B1
    A1 -.comparison.- B3
```

**Engineering Interpretation:** The 16-bit fixed-point system spans only ~6 decades (factor of 32,768) of magnitude, while the 32-bit float spans ~76 decades — a difference of about 12 orders of magnitude in usable range.

---

## 4.5 Quantization Error Probability Distribution (PDF)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Error e axis] --> B[Negative Delta over 2]
    B --> C[Zero mean]
    C --> D[Positive Delta over 2]
    E[Probability Density 1 over Delta] --> B
    E --> D
```

**Description:** The error is uniformly distributed over $[-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2]$ with mean 0 and variance $\Delta^2/12$. This is the foundation for the A/D noise model used in SECTION 3.2.
---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** Define the term "Dynamic Range" in the context of DSP number formats. Calculate the dynamic range in dB for a 12-bit signed fixed-point system.  `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

Dynamic Range (DR) is the ratio of the maximum to the minimum representable non-zero magnitude in a number format, expressed in dB.

For a $b$-bit signed fixed-point system with $b = 11$ magnitude bits (12 total signed bits):
$$\text{DR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}(2^{b}) = 20 \times 11 \times 0.3010 = 66.22 \;\text{dB}$$

(Equivalent: $6.02 \times 11 \approx 66.2$ dB)

---

### **Q2.** List the three main sources of error in a real-time DSP implementation. Which error source dominates in a high-Q (narrow-band) IIR filter?  `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The three main sources of error in a DSP implementation are:

1. **A/D Conversion Error** — input quantization noise due to finite amplitude resolution of the ADC.
2. **DSP Computational Error** — round-off noise in multiplications, coefficient quantization, and overflow in fixed-point arithmetic.
3. **D/A Conversion Error** — output quantization noise and zero-order-hold distortion in the DAC stage.

**Dominant Error in a High-Q IIR Filter:**
In a high-Q IIR filter, **coefficient quantization error (part of DSP computational error)** dominates. Narrow-band poles are located very close to the unit circle, and any small perturbation in the coefficients can drastically shift the pole location — potentially causing instability or severe frequency response distortion. This is referred to as **high coefficient sensitivity**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### (a) [7 Marks] Derive the expression for SQNR of a $B$-bit A/D converter when the input is a full-scale sinusoid. What is the SQNR in dB for $B = 14$?

#### (b) [7 Marks] Explain the three sub-types of DSP computational error. For a 16-tap FIR filter using 12-bit fixed-point arithmetic, compute the output round-off noise variance.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### (a) [7 Marks] Compare fixed-point and floating-point number formats in terms of range, precision, and hardware complexity. Derive the dynamic range in dB for both formats.

#### (b) [7 Marks] With a neat block diagram, explain the three sources of error in a real-time DSP system. State the zero-order-hold (ZOH) frequency response and compute its droop at $f = f_s / 4$.

---

## 5.3 Complete Solutions

### **Solution to Question A:**

#### Part (a) — SQNR Derivation

**Step 1:** For a full-scale sinusoid $x(t) = V \sin(\omega t)$, RMS value:
$$V_{\text{rms}} = V / \sqrt{2} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; P_s = V^2 / 2$$

**Step 2:** Quantization noise variance (from §3.2):
$$\sigma_e^2 = \frac{V^2}{3 \cdot 2^{2B}}$$

**Step 3:** SQNR ratio:
$$\text{SQNR} = \frac{V^2/2}{V^2/(3 \cdot 2^{2B})} = \frac{3 \cdot 2^{2B}}{2} = 1.5 \cdot 4^B$$

**Step 4:** In dB:
$$\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(1.5) + 10B \log_{10}(4) = 1.76 + 6.02 B$$

$$\boxed{\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 6.02\,B + 1.76}$$

**Step 5:** For $B = 14$:
$$\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 6.02 \times 14 + 1.76 = 84.28 + 1.76 = 86.04 \;\text{dB}$$

---

#### Part (b) — DSP Computational Error Analysis

**Three Sub-types:**

1. **Coefficient Quantization Error:** The designed filter coefficients $h[k]$ (or $a[k]$, $b[k]$ for IIR) are stored in finite word-length registers. This causes the actual filter to deviate from the ideal — most critical in IIR filters with poles near the unit circle.

2. **Product Quantization (Round-off) Error:** Every multiplication $h[k] \cdot x[n-k]$ produces a wider result that is truncated or rounded to fit the accumulator's word-length. This is the dominant round-off source.

3. **Overflow Error:** When the accumulator exceeds the maximum representable value, wrap-around or saturation occurs. Wrap-around (in 2's complement without saturation) creates large-magnitude spurious outputs.

**Numerical Computation — Output Round-off Noise Variance:**

Given: $N = 16$ taps, $B = 12$ bits → $2^{-B} = 2^{-12}$.

**Step 1:** Quantization step (assuming Q12 format with 1 sign bit and 11 fractional bits stored in 12-bit register):
$$\Delta = 2^{-11}$$

(Assuming the actual stored word is 12 bits with $B = 11$ fractional bits.)

**Step 2:** Round-off variance per multiplication:
$$\sigma_e^2 = \frac{\Delta^2}{12} = \frac{2^{-22}}{12}$$

**Step 3:** Output variance for 16-tap FIR:
$$\sigma_y^2 = N \cdot \sigma_e^2 = 16 \cdot \frac{2^{-22}}{12} = \frac{16}{12 \cdot 2^{22}} = \frac{4}{3 \cdot 2^{22}}$$

**Step 4:** Numerical evaluation:
$$\sigma_y^2 = \frac{4}{3 \times 4{,}194{,}304} = \frac{4}{12{,}582{,}912} \approx 3.18 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$\sigma_y = \sqrt{3.18 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 5.64 \times 10^{-4} \;\;\text{(RMS value)}$$

---

### **Solution to Question B:**

#### Part (a) — Fixed-Point vs Floating-Point Comparison

| Parameter | Fixed-Point | Floating-Point (IEEE 754 single) |
|-----------|-------------|----------------------------------|
| Word length | 16/24/32 bits | 32 bits |
| Range | $\pm 2^{b-1}$ (limited) | $\pm 3.4 \times 10^{38}$ (very large) |
| Precision (absolute) | Constant $\Delta = 2^{-B}$ | Scales with value: $\Delta(x) = \epsilon_{\text{rel}} \vert x \vert$ |
| Precision (relative) | Deteriorates for small values | Uniform: $2^{-23} \approx 1.19 \times 10^{-7}$ |
| Dynamic Range | $6.02 b$ dB | $\sim 1523$ dB |
| Hardware complexity | Simple, low power | Complex (needs alignment, renormalization) |
| Speed | Fast (single-cycle MAC) | Slower (multi-cycle, especially add) |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Overflow | Yes (must guard) | Rare (only for extreme values) |
| Best for | Embedded, real-time audio, control | Scientific, radar, medical imaging |

**Dynamic Range Derivation (already in §3.4 and §3.5):**

Fixed-point (b-bit signed):
$$\text{DR}_{\text{dB}} = 6.02 b$$

Floating-point (IEEE 754 single):
$$\text{DR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}(2^{254}) = 1529 \;\text{dB} \;\;\text{(approximate)}$$

---

#### Part (b) — Block Diagram of Error Sources

**Block Diagram (textual description — see §4.1 Mermaid for visual):**

$$\text{Analog Input} \rightarrow \text{Anti-Alias Filter} \rightarrow \boxed{\text{A/D Converter} \atop (+e_1)} \rightarrow \text{DSP Core} \rightarrow \boxed{\text{D/A Converter} \atop (+e_3)} \rightarrow \text{Reconstruction Filter} \rightarrow \text{Analog Output}$$

The DSP Core is where round-off error $e_2$ is added during MAC operations.

**Zero-Order Hold Frequency Response:**
$$H_{\text{ZOH}}(f) = T \cdot \frac{\sin(\pi f T)}{\pi f T} = T \cdot \text{sinc}\left(\frac{f}{f_s}\right)$$

**Droop at $f = f_s/4$:**

Substituting $f = f_s/4$:
$$\frac{f}{f_s} = \frac{1}{4}$$

$$\text{sinc}\left(\frac{1}{4}\right) = \frac{\sin(\pi/4)}{\pi/4} = \frac{\sqrt{2}/2}{\pi/4} = \frac{4 \cdot \sqrt{2}}{2 \pi} = \frac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi}$$

Numerical:
$$\text{sinc}(0.25) = \frac{2 \times 1.4142}{3.1416} = \frac{2.8284}{3.1416} \approx 0.9003$$

In dB:
$$20 \log_{10}(0.9003) = -0.916 \;\text{dB}$$

So the ZOH droop at $f_s/4$ is approximately **−0.92 dB** (about 10% signal loss). This is why professional audio DACs include a **$\sin(x)/x$ equalization filter** in the reconstruction stage.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **SQNR formula confusion:** Many students use $6.02 B + 1.76$ for general signals, but the **1.76 dB term is only for full-scale sinusoids**. For arbitrary signals, use $6.02 B + 4.77 - 20 \log_{10}(V_p/V_{\text{rms}})$.
> 2. **Bit count confusion:** "12-bit A/D" means 12 total bits. When asked for $b$ in the fixed-point DR formula, use $b = 11$ for signed 12-bit (one bit is sign).
> 3. **Units mix-up:** SQNR is in **dB**, while $b \cdot 6.02$ for dynamic range is also **dB** — but never use the same constant for both without context.
> 4. **ZOH error attribution:** Students often attribute ZOH droop to A/D error. **ZOH droop is a D/A error** (it occurs in the reconstruction stage).
> 5. **Forgetting the $1/\sqrt{2}$ factor:** In the SQNR derivation, students frequently forget the RMS conversion, missing the crucial 1.76 dB term.
> 6. **Confusing $\Delta$ (step) with $\Delta^2/12$ (variance):** The quantization noise **power** is $\Delta^2/12$, not $\Delta/12$.
> 7. **Writing $2^B$ instead of $2^{2B}$:** In the variance formula $\sigma_e^2 = V^2/(3 \cdot 2^{2B})$, the exponent is $2B$, not $B$.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

### **Number Formats**
- [ ] **Fixed-point:** Binary point is fixed; constant precision $\Delta = 2^{-B}$; range limited by $b$ bits; DR = $6.02 b$ dB.
- [ ] **Floating-point (IEEE 754 single):** 1 sign + 8 exponent + 23 mantissa = 32 bits; DR ≈ 1500 dB; relative precision $2^{-23}$.
- [ ] **Block floating-point:** Shared exponent, fixed-point mantissas — used in FFT.

### **Dynamic Range vs Precision**
- [ ] **Dynamic range** = max/min representable value (overall span).
- [ ] **Precision** = smallest step size (granularity).
- [ ] Fixed-point has **constant absolute precision** but **varying relative precision**.
- [ ] Floating-point has **constant relative precision** but **varying absolute precision**.

### **Sources of Error**
- [ ] **A/D Error:** Quantization noise variance $\sigma_e^2 = \Delta^2/12$; SQNR $= 6.02B + 1.76$ dB (sinusoid).
- [ ] **DSP Computational Error:** Coefficient quantization, product round-off, overflow.
- [ ] **D/A Error:** ZOH droop ($\sin x / x$), output quantization, glitches.

### **Critical Numerical Formulas (Must Memorize)**
- [ ] $\Delta = 2V / 2^B = V \cdot 2^{1-B}$
- [ ] $\sigma_e^2 = \Delta^2 / 12$
- [ ] $\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 6.02 B + 1.76$ (sinusoid)
- [ ] $\text{DR}_{\text{FP, dB}} = 6.02 b$
- [ ] $\text{DR}_{\text{FL, dB}} \approx 1500$ dB (IEEE 754 single)
- [ ] $\sigma_{y,\text{FIR}}^2 = N \cdot \Delta^2 / 12$

### **Design Rules of Thumb**
- [ ] Adding 1 bit to ADC → +6 dB SQNR.
- [ ] Doubling $N$ in FIR → output round-off noise increases by 3 dB.
- [ ] High-Q IIR filters are most sensitive to coefficient quantization.
- [ ] ZOH droop at Nyquist $= -3.92$ dB; at $f_s/4 \approx -0.92$ dB.

### **Real-World Anchors**
- [ ] 8-bit audio: $\sim 50$ dB SQNR (telephony).
- [ ] 16-bit audio CD: $\sim 98$ dB SQNR.
- [ ] 24-bit $\Sigma$-$\Delta$ ADC: $\sim 146$ dB DR (professional audio, seismic).
- [ ] IEEE 754 single: $\sim 1523$ dB DR.
- [ ] 32-bit fixed-point: $\sim 193$ dB DR.

### **Common Exam Keywords to Recognize**
- "Calculate SQNR" → use $6.02 B + 1.76$.
- "Find dynamic range" → use $6.02 b$ (fixed) or $\approx 1500$ dB (float).
- "Output noise of FIR" → use $N \cdot \Delta^2/12$.
- "ZOH droop at frequency f" → compute $\sin(\pi f / f_s) / (\pi f / f_s)$ in dB.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: FFT and FIR Filter Realization on a Fixed Point Processor - Finite Wordlength Effects (Quantization, Rounding and Truncation, Overflow and Scaling)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST526 - DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** FFT and FIR Filter Realization on a Fixed Point Processor - Finite Wordlength Effects (Quantization, Rounding and Truncation, Overflow and Scaling)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition: Finite Wordlength Effects in Fixed-Point DSP

In real-time Digital Signal Processing (DSP) on a **fixed-point processor**, every signal sample, filter coefficient, and intermediate arithmetic product must be stored in a register of **finite bit-width** (typically **16-bit** for commercial DSPs like the TMS320C5x, or **32-bit** accumulators). Because real-world analog signals and exact mathematical products produce values that would require an **infinite number of bits** to represent perfectly, they must be **truncated** or **rounded** to fit the register. This unavoidable approximation introduces **Finite Wordlength (FWL) effects** — small but accumulating numerical errors that degrade the fidelity of FFT analysis and FIR filtering.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Finite Word Length effects refer to the quantization effects that arise when a discrete-time signal (originally an infinite-precision mathematical entity) is implemented on a digital hardware with a finite number of bits. These include: (i) **Input/ADC Quantization**, (ii) **Coefficient Quantization**, (iii) **Arithmetic Round-off/Truncation**, (iv) **Overflow**, all of which introduce **Quantization Noise** and **Limit Cycles** into the output.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bucket" Model

Imagine you are filling water from a giant ocean (the **infinite-precision** mathematical world) into a small fixed-size bucket (the **b-bit register**):

- **Quantization** is the act of scooping water — you simply **cannot fit more** than the bucket's capacity. Any overflow spills.
- **Truncation** is like chopping off the top of the wave with a knife — whatever sticks above the rim is **discarded silently**.
- **Rounding** is like measuring to the **nearest centimetre** — you choose the closest valid level, biasing equally in both directions.
- **Overflow** is the spill itself — when arithmetic `+` overflows, the sign may **flip incorrectly** (a catastrophic error in two's complement, called **wrap-around**).
- **Scaling** is the engineering act of **pre-emptively shrinking** the wave so it never reaches the rim — you trade dynamic range for safety.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does KTU care about this topic?**
> In a KTU 2024 Scheme semester exam, you will be asked to compute the **Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR)** of an N-bit ADC, derive the **variance of rounding/truncation error**, apply **scaling rules** for L-norm to prevent overflow, and analyze how the **butterfly structure of DIT-FFT** propagates these errors. Mastering this topic secures marks in **Part A (2–3 marks)** and frequently the (a)/(b) sub-parts of a **14-mark Part B** question.

## 1.3 Fixed-Point Number Representation Formats

A generic **b-bit fixed-point** two's-complement number is partitioned as:

| Bit Position | b-1 | b-2 | ... | 1 | 0 |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Role | Sign bit (MSB) | Integer bits | ... | Fractional bit | LSB |

If the binary point is placed after the sign bit with **b-1 fractional bits**, the representable range is:

$$
-1 \le x < 1 - 2^{-(b-1)}
$$

The **resolution** or **quantization step size** is:

$$
\Delta = 2^{-(b-1)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard KTU convention: For a `Q15` format on a 16-bit DSP, there are 15 fractional bits and 1 sign bit, giving $\Delta = 2^{-15} \approx 3.05 \times 10^{-5}$.

## 1.4 The Three Sources of Finite Wordlength Effects (KTU Triangle)

```
                Finite Wordlength Effects
                          /\
                         /  \
                        /    \
                       /      \
                      /________\
            A/D Input      Coefficient     Arithmetic
           Quantization    Quantization   Round-off/Truncation
             (e_A)           (e_C)            (e_arith)
```

- **Input (A/D) Quantization** — discrete-time continuous-amplitude signal $x(n)$ is mapped to finite levels $x_Q(n)$.
- **Coefficient Quantization** — designed filter coefficients $h(n)$ are rounded to $b$ bits, shifting poles/zeros and altering the frequency response.
- **Product Quantization (Arithmetic)** — every multiplication $x(n) \cdot h(k)$ produces a $2b$-bit product, which must be re-quantized back to $b$ bits.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown

### Step 1 — A/D Input Quantization Model

The quantization noise model treats the quantizer as a **linear gain + additive noise** system:

$$
x_Q(n) = x(n) + e_A(n)
$$

**Assumptions (KTU assumes the following four conditions — memorize them):**
1. $e_A(n)$ is a **stationary** random process.
2. $e_A(n)$ is **uncorrelated** with $x(n)$.
3. The samples $e_A(n)$ are **uncorrelated** with each other (white noise).
4. The error is **uniformly distributed** in the interval $[-\Delta/2, \Delta/2]$.

For **mid-tread rounding** on two's-complement:

$$
e_A \sim \mathcal{U}\left(-\frac{\Delta}{2}, \frac{\Delta}{2}\right)
$$

The **mean** of $e_A$ is **zero** (rounding is unbiased), and the **variance** is:

$$
\sigma_e^2 = \frac{\Delta^2}{12} = \frac{2^{-2(b-1)}}{12} = \frac{2^{-2b}}{3}
$$

The **Quantization Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SQNR in dB)** for a sinusoidal full-scale input of amplitude $A$ is:

$$
\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 6.02\,b + 1.76 \quad \text{(dB)}
$$

> This is the famous **"6 dB per bit" rule** — KTU examiners love to test this.

### Step 2 — Coefficient Quantization

The designed FIR filter $H(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h(k) z^{-k}$ is implemented with quantized coefficients $h_Q(k)$:

$$
h_Q(k) = h(k) + e_C(k)
$$

The implemented transfer function becomes:

$$
H_Q(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h_Q(k) z^{-k} = H(z) + E_C(z)
$$

where $E_C(z) = \sum e_C(k) z^{-k}$. Since $E_C(z)$ is a polynomial whose roots (zeros) are uncorrelated with the original $H(z)$, coefficient quantization **moves the zeros** of an FIR filter. The **sensitivity** of frequency response to coefficient errors is bounded by:

$$
\vert \Delta H(e^{j\omega}) \vert \le \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \vert e_C(k) \vert
$$

### Step 3 — Arithmetic Round-off (Product Quantization)

Each MAC (Multiply-Accumulate) operation $y(n) = \sum h(k) x(n-k)$ requires the product $h(k) \cdot x(n-k)$ to be stored. If both are $b$-bit two's-complement fractions, the product is up to $2b$ bits. When re-quantized to $b$ bits by **rounding**, the round-off error is bounded by $\vert e_{\text{round}} \vert \le \Delta/2$.

The **Output Noise Variance** due to round-off in a direct-form FIR filter is:

$$
\sigma_{y,e}^2 = N \cdot \sigma_{B,e}^2 = N \cdot \frac{2^{-2b}}{12}
$$

where $N$ is the filter order. This assumes each of the $N$ products is rounded **independently** before accumulation.

### Step 4 — Truncation Error

**Truncation** simply discards the lower bits. The sign behavior depends on representation:

| Number Format | Range of Truncation Error |
|:---:|:---:|
| Sign-Magnitude | $\left[-\Delta(1 - 2^{-(b-1)}),\ 0\right]$ |
| Two's Complement | $\left[-\Delta,\ \Delta(1 - 2^{-(b-1)})\right]$ |
| One's Complement | $\left[-\Delta(1 - 2^{-b}),\ \Delta(1 - 2^{-b})\right]$ |

**Variance of Truncation Error (uniform distribution over the range):**

$$
\sigma_{e,\text{trunc}}^2 = \frac{\Delta^2}{12} = \frac{2^{-2b}}{12} \quad \text{(two's complement, fractional part)}$$

**Key contrast:** Rounding and truncation have the **same variance** for two's-complement fractions, but truncation in sign-magnitude has a **non-zero mean** (negative bias), while rounding is unbiased.

### Step 5 — Overflow and the Two's-Complement "Wrap-Around"

When a $b$-bit register is exceeded, two's-complement arithmetic produces a value that **wraps around** — adding 1 to the maximum positive number $+0.111...1$ yields $-1.0$ (a sign flip). This is catastrophic and can drive an IIR filter into **large-scale oscillations (overflow limit cycles)**.

**Saturation Arithmetic** (used in modern DSPs like the TMS320C55x) clamps the result to the maximum representable value, preventing the wrap-around but introducing a **non-linearity** that acts as a soft limiter.

### Step 6 — Scaling (KTU Standard $L_p$-Norm Rules)

To prevent overflow, the input is scaled by a factor $A$ before processing. For a filter with impulse response $h(n)$ and input $x(n)$:

**$L_\infty$ (Peak-Value) Scaling Rule:**

$$
A = \frac{1}{x_{\max} \cdot \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \vert h(k) \vert}
$$

This guarantees the **output never exceeds 1** in magnitude, i.e., no overflow.

**$L_2$ (Energy) Scaling Rule:**

$$
A = \frac{1}{x_{\max} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h^2(k)}}
$$

This prevents overflow for inputs whose total energy is bounded, allowing a **larger input dynamic range** at the cost of occasional clipping.

**$L_1$ Scaling Rule (used as a compromise):**

$$
A = \frac{1}{x_{\max} \cdot \max_\omega \vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert}
$$

### Step 7 — FFT on a Fixed-Point Processor

The **DIT-FFT (Decimation-In-Time Fast Fourier Transform)** butterfly computes:

$$
X_m(p) = X_{m-1}(p) + W_N^r X_{m-1}(q)
$$

$$
X_m(q) = X_{m-1}(p) - W_N^r X_{m-1}(q)
$$

where $W_N^r = e^{-j 2\pi r/N}$ is the twiddle factor. Each butterfly involves:
- One **complex multiplication** (output is $2b + 2$ bits for a 2-b-input complex product)
- Two **complex additions**

The output must be re-quantized after each stage. For an $N$-point FFT with $M = \log_2 N$ stages, the total **output noise variance** is:

$$
\sigma_{X,\text{FFT}}^2 = \frac{2M \cdot 2^{-2b}}{12} = \frac{M \cdot 2^{-2b+1}}{12}
$$

**Scaling for FFT:** Since the output magnitude grows as $\log_2 N$ bits, divide by $2$ at each stage ("block floating point" or "auto-scaling") to prevent overflow.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Concept | Formula | Boundary / Units |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Quantization Step Size | $\Delta = 2^{-(b-1)}$ | Volts (or normalized) |
| Rounding Error Variance | $\sigma_e^2 = \Delta^2 / 12 = 2^{-2b}/3$ | Power (V²) |
| SQNR for Sinusoid | $\text{SQNR} = 6.02b + 1.76$ | dB |
| Output Noise (DF FIR) | $\sigma_y^2 = N \cdot 2^{-2b}/3$ | Power |
| Output Noise (FFT) | $\sigma_X^2 = 2M \cdot 2^{-2b}/3$ | Power |
| Overflow Condition | $\vert y(n) \vert \ge 1$ | — |
| $L_\infty$ Scale Factor | $A = 1 / (x_{\max} \cdot \sum \vert h(k) \vert)$ | Dimensionless |
| $L_2$ Scale Factor | $A = 1 / (x_{\max} \cdot \sqrt{\sum h^2(k)})$ | Dimensionless |
| Truncation Error Range (TC) | $[-\Delta,\ \Delta(1 - 2^{-(b-1)})]$ | — |
| Rounding Error Range | $[-\Delta/2,\ \Delta/2]$ | — |
| FFT Growth Factor | $2^{M/2}$ for complex | — |
| Butterfly Output Variance | $2 \sigma_{\text{in}}^2$ | Power |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Variance of Rounding Error

**Goal:** Show that the variance of a uniformly distributed rounding error in $[-\Delta/2, \Delta/2]$ is $\Delta^2/12$.

**Starting definition** of variance for a uniform random variable $E \sim \mathcal{U}(a,b)$:

$$
\sigma_E^2 = \frac{(b-a)^2}{12}
$$

**Step 1.** For rounding, $a = -\Delta/2$ and $b = \Delta/2$:

$$
b - a = \frac{\Delta}{2} - \left(-\frac{\Delta}{2}\right) = \Delta
$$

**Step 2.** Substitute into the uniform variance formula:

$$
\sigma_E^2 = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}
$$

**Step 3.** Substitute $\Delta = 2^{-(b-1)}$:

$$
\sigma_E^2 = \frac{2^{-2(b-1)}}{12} = \frac{2^{-2b+2}}{12} = \frac{2^{-2b}}{3}
$$

**Step 4.** (Validation) For $b = 16$ bits: $\sigma_E^2 = 2^{-32}/3 \approx 1.24 \times 10^{-10}$, RMS noise $\sigma_E \approx 1.11 \times 10^{-5}$, which matches $\Delta/2\sqrt{3}$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — SQNR of an N-bit Quantizer for a Sinusoid

**Goal:** Derive $\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 6.02\,b + 1.76$.

**Step 1.** Signal power for a sinusoid of amplitude $A$:

$$
P_{\text{signal}} = \frac{A^2}{2}
$$

**Step 2.** Full-scale amplitude uses the entire dynamic range, $A = 2^{b-1} \Delta$:

$$
P_{\text{signal}} = \frac{(2^{b-1} \Delta)^2}{2} = \frac{2^{2b-2} \Delta^2}{2} = 2^{2b-3} \Delta^2
$$

**Step 3.** Quantization noise power is $\sigma_e^2 = \Delta^2/12$:

$$
P_{\text{noise}} = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}
$$

**Step 4.** Form the ratio:

$$
\text{SQNR} = \frac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}} = \frac{2^{2b-3} \Delta^2}{\Delta^2/12} = 12 \cdot 2^{2b-3} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot 2^{2b} = 1.5 \cdot 2^{2b}
$$

**Step 5.** Convert to dB:

$$
\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(1.5) + 20 b \log_{10}(2)
$$

**Step 6.** Evaluate constants:

$$
10 \log_{10}(1.5) \approx 1.76 \text{ dB}, \qquad 20 \log_{10}(2) \approx 6.02 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 7.** Final result:

$$
\boxed{\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 6.02\,b + 1.76 \text{ dB}}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Output Noise of Direct-Form FIR Filter

**Goal:** Show $\sigma_{y,e}^2 = N \cdot \sigma_{B,e}^2$ for a length-$N$ FIR filter.

**Step 1.** FIR output equation:

$$
y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h(k) x(n-k)
$$

**Step 2.** Each MAC introduces a rounding error $e_k$ at the product node. Quantized output:

$$
y_Q(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \left[h(k) x(n-k) + e_k(n)\right] = y(n) + \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} e_k(n)
$$

**Step 3.** Output noise is the sum:

$$
e_{\text{out}}(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} e_k(n)
$$

**Step 4.** Assuming $e_k(n)$ are **independent, zero-mean** with variance $\sigma_B^2 = 2^{-2b}/3$:

$$
\sigma_{y,e}^2 = \text{Var}\left(\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} e_k\right) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \text{Var}(e_k) = N \sigma_B^2
$$

**Step 5.** Substitute:

$$
\boxed{\sigma_{y,e}^2 = \frac{N \cdot 2^{-2b}}{3}}
$$

**Step 6.** The output SNR is therefore:

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{out,dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{\sigma_y^2}{N \cdot 2^{-2b}/3}\right) = 6.02\,b - 10\log_{10}(3N) + 10\log_{10}(\sigma_y^2)
$$

> Every doubling of the filter length $N$ costs you ~3 dB in output SNR — a key KTU trade-off point.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Round-off Noise in DIT-FFT

**Goal:** Compute total output noise variance for an $N = 2^M$ point DIT-FFT.

**Step 1.** Each butterfly performs one complex multiplication $W_N^r \cdot X_{m-1}(q)$. A complex multiply of two $b$-bit complex numbers yields a $2b$-bit complex product, and after re-quantization of real and imaginary parts, contributes 2 independent round-off errors, each of variance $\sigma_B^2 = 2^{-2b}/12$.

**Step 2.** Per-butterfly noise variance:

$$
\sigma_{\text{bf}}^2 = 2 \cdot \frac{2^{-2b}}{12} = \frac{2^{-2b}}{6}
$$

**Step 3.** Total butterflies in $M$ stages = $N/2 \cdot M$. But due to **noise propagation paths** in the FFT graph, the standard result (KTU expected) is that each input noise source reaches the output along a **unique path** of butterflies, contributing in cascade. The widely accepted result is:

$$
\sigma_{X,\text{total}}^2 = M \cdot \sigma_{\text{bf}}^2 \cdot 1 = \frac{M \cdot 2^{-2b}}{6}
$$

**Step 4.** Converting to amplitude form (using real and imaginary parts):

$$
\sigma_X^2 = 2 M \cdot \frac{2^{-2b}}{12} = \frac{M \cdot 2^{-2b+1}}{12}
$$

**Step 5.** For a normalized input with variance $\sigma_x^2$, the output SNR is:

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{out}} = \frac{N^2 \sigma_x^2}{\sigma_X^2} = \frac{N^2 \sigma_x^2 \cdot 12}{M \cdot 2^{-2b+1}}
$$

> For KTU, note that **doubling N (i.e., adding one FFT stage) gains 3 dB in signal but adds ~0.5 dB in noise**, so net SNR increases by ~2.5 dB per stage.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — $L_\infty$ Scaling Factor

**Goal:** Find the scale factor $A$ such that $\vert y(n) \vert \le 1$ for all $n$.

**Step 1.** FIR output with scaled input $x'(n) = A x(n)$:

$$
y(n) = A \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h(k) x(n-k)
$$

**Step 2.** Take absolute value and apply triangle inequality:

$$
\vert y(n) \vert \le A \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \vert h(k) \vert \cdot \vert x(n-k) \vert
$$

**Step 3.** Bound $\vert x(n-k) \vert \le x_{\max}$:

$$
\vert y(n) \vert \le A \cdot x_{\max} \cdot \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \vert h(k) \vert
$$

**Step 4.** Require this to be $\le 1$:

$$
A \cdot x_{\max} \cdot \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \vert h(k) \vert \le 1
$$

**Step 5.** Solve for the maximum allowable $A$:

$$
\boxed{A = \frac{1}{x_{\max} \cdot \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \vert h(k) \vert}}
$$

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A 4-bit ADC samples a sinusoid $x(t) = 5 \cos(2\pi \cdot 100 t)$ with peak amplitude $5$ V. The full-scale range is $\pm 8$ V. Compute the SQNR.

**Step 1.** Number of bits: $b = 4$.

**Step 2.** Apply the SQNR formula:

$$
\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 6.02(4) + 1.76 = 24.08 + 1.76 = 25.84 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 3.** For 16-bit, it would be: $6.02(16) + 1.76 = 96.32 + 1.76 = 98.08$ dB — explains why CD audio uses 16-bit.

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Rounding vs Truncation vs Overflow

| Property | Rounding | Truncation (Two's Comp.) | Overflow (Wrap-Around) | Saturation |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Error Range | $[-\Delta/2, \Delta/2]$ | $[-\Delta, \Delta(1-2^{-(b-1)})$] | Wraps to opposite sign | Clamped to $\pm 1$ |
| Mean | 0 | $-\Delta/2$ | 0 (after wrap) | 0 (sym) |
| Variance | $\Delta^2/12$ | $\Delta^2/12$ | Catastrophic | Nonlinear |
| Use Case | General DSP | Hardware cheap | Legacy two's comp | Modern DSPs |
| KTU Weight | **High** | **High** | **Medium** | **Low–Medium** |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — A/D Quantization Noise Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Analog Input xa(t)"] --> B["Sampler x(n)"]
    B --> C["Quantizer Q step Delta"]
    C --> D["Digital Output xQ(n)"]
    E["Additive Noise eA(n)"] -.-> C
    C --> F["Quantization Error Model xQ = x + eA"]
    D --> F
    style A fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#000
    style C fill:#90ee90,stroke:#000
    style E fill:#ffb6c1,stroke:#000
    style F fill:#add8e6,stroke:#000
```

> The quantizer is modeled as a **sum junction**: ideal input + additive uniform white noise. KTU expects this **statistical linear model** in any finite wordlength problem.

## 4.2 Architecture — FIR Filter with Quantization Injection Points

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X["Input x(n)"] --> M1
    H0["h0 b-bit"] --> M1["Multiplier"]
    M1 --> R1["Round to b bits e0"]
    R1 --> A1["Adder +"]
    H1["h1 b-bit"] --> M2["Multiplier"]
    X1["x(n-1) delay"] --> M2
    M2 --> R2["Round to b bits e1"]
    R2 --> A1
    H2["hN-1 b-bit"] --> MN["Multiplier"]
    XN["x(n-N+1) delay"] --> MN
    MN --> RN["Round to b bits eN-1"]
    RN --> AN["Adder +"]
    A1 --> AN
    AN --> Y["Output y(n)"]
    style M1 fill:#fffacd,stroke:#000
    style M2 fill:#fffacd,stroke:#000
    style MN fill:#fffacd,stroke:#000
    style R1 fill:#ffcccb,stroke:#000
    style R2 fill:#ffcccb,stroke:#000
    style RN fill:#ffcccb,stroke:#000
```

> The **red nodes** are the round-off noise sources $e_0, e_1, \ldots, e_{N-1}$. KTU expects you to identify these as **N independent noise sources** and sum their variances.

## 4.3 Architecture — DIT-FFT Butterfly with Re-quantization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P["Xm-1 upper"] --> A1["+"]
    Q["Xm-1 lower"] --> M["Complex Multiply by Wr"]
    M --> A1
    M --> A2["-"]
    A1 --> R1["Round/Re-quant to b bits"]
    A2 --> R2["Round/Re-quant to b bits"]
    R1 --> O1["Xm upper output"]
    R2 --> O2["Xm lower output"]
    Q --> A2
    style M fill:#ffd700,stroke:#000
    style R1 fill:#ffcccb,stroke:#000
    style R2 fill:#ffcccb,stroke:#000
```

> Each butterfly has **one complex multiplier** and **two complex adders**. The multiplier output is $2b$ bits and must be **re-quantized** to $b$ bits — this is the primary source of round-off noise in FFT.

## 4.4 Process Flow — Overall Fixed-Point DSP Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Analog Signal"] --> S2["Anti-Aliasing LPF"]
    S2 --> S3["Sample and Hold"]
    S3 --> S4["A/D Converter b bits"]
    S4 --> S5["Digital Input xQ n"]
    S5 --> S6["Scaling Stage A xQ n"]
    S6 --> S7["FIR / FFT MAC Engine b bits"]
    S7 --> S8["Overflow Check / Saturation"]
    S8 --> S9["Output yQ n"]
    style S4 fill:#ffb6c1,stroke:#000
    style S6 fill:#90ee90,stroke:#000
    style S7 fill:#add8e6,stroke:#000
    style S8 fill:#ffd700,stroke:#000
```

> This is the canonical KTU pipeline. Each colored node is a **distinct error source**:
> - **Pink (S4):** Input quantization error $e_A$.
> - **Green (S6):** Scaling noise (deliberate attenuation of SNR).
> - **Blue (S7):** Round-off noise from MAC operations.
> - **Yellow (S8):** Overflow handling — saturation vs wrap-around.
