# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define quantization error. State the four assumptions made in the statistical model of A/D quantization noise.**

**Model Answer:**
Quantization error $e_A(n) = x_Q(n) - x(n)$ is the difference between the quantized and true sample value. **Four assumptions:**
1. $e_A(n)$ is a stationary white noise process.
2. $e_A(n)$ is uncorrelated with the input $x(n)$.
3. Successive samples $e_A(n), e_A(n-1), \ldots$ are mutually uncorrelated.
4. The PDF of $e_A(n)$ is uniform over $[-\Delta/2, \Delta/2]$, giving mean $0$ and variance $\sigma_e^2 = \Delta^2/12$.

**(3 marks)** — Each assumption: 0.5 marks. Definition: 1 mark.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**For a 12-bit ADC with full-scale sinusoidal input, compute the SQNR. What is the effect of adding 4 more bits of resolution?**

**Model Answer:**

$$
\text{SQNR} = 6.02(12) + 1.76 = 72.24 + 1.76 = 74.00 \text{ dB}
$$

For 16 bits: $\text{SQNR} = 6.02(16) + 1.76 = 98.08$ dB. **Increase = 24.08 dB**, i.e., **4 bits → +24 dB** (rule: **6 dB per bit**).

**(3 marks)** — Calculation: 2 marks. Justification: 1 mark.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — KTU ESE 2024 Pattern

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the statistical model of A/D quantization. Derive the expression for SQNR of a sinusoidal input. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For an FIR filter of length $N = 32$ implemented on a 16-bit fixed-point processor using direct form, find the output noise variance due to product round-off. Assume input is uniformly distributed in $[-1, 1]$ and compute the output SNR. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Quantization Model and SQNR Derivation (7 marks)

**Block diagram (2 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["x(n) ideal"] --> Q["Quantizer"]
    Q --> B["xQ(n) = x(n) + eA(n)"]
    E["eA ~ U(-Delta/2, Delta/2)"] -.-> Q
    style Q fill:#90ee90,stroke:#000
    style E fill:#ffb6c1,stroke:#000
```

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Step 1: For a sinusoid $x(t) = A \cos(\omega t)$, the signal power is $P_s = A^2/2$.

Step 2: The quantization step $\Delta = V_{\text{FS}}/2^{b-1}$, and full-scale amplitude $A = 2^{b-1} \Delta$, so:

$$
P_s = \frac{(2^{b-1} \Delta)^2}{2} = 2^{2b-3} \Delta^2
$$

Step 3: Quantization noise power:

$$
P_n = \sigma_e^2 = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}
$$

Step 4: Ratio:

$$
\text{SQNR} = \frac{P_s}{P_n} = \frac{2^{2b-3} \Delta^2}{\Delta^2/12} = 12 \cdot 2^{2b-3} = 1.5 \cdot 2^{2b}
$$

Step 5: Converting to dB:

$$
\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(1.5) + 20 b \log_{10}(2) = 6.02 b + 1.76 \text{ dB}
$$

---

#### Part (b) — Output Noise Variance of 32-tap FIR on 16-bit Fixed-Point (7 marks)

**Step 1: Quantization step** (1 mark)

$$
\Delta = 2^{-(16-1)} = 2^{-15}
$$

**Step 2: Variance of each round-off error** (1 mark)

$$
\sigma_B^2 = \frac{\Delta^2}{12} = \frac{2^{-30}}{12} = \frac{1}{12 \cdot 2^{30}}
$$

**Step 3: Total output noise variance** (2 marks)

$$
\sigma_{y,e}^2 = N \cdot \sigma_B^2 = 32 \cdot \frac{2^{-30}}{12} = \frac{32}{12 \cdot 2^{30}} = \frac{8}{3 \cdot 2^{30}}
$$

Numerically: $\sigma_{y,e}^2 \approx 9.93 \times 10^{-9}$.

**Step 4: Input signal variance** (1 mark)

For $x \sim \mathcal{U}(-1, 1)$: $\sigma_x^2 = (1 - (-1))^2/12 = 4/12 = 1/3$.

**Step 5: Output signal variance for unit-gain FIR** (1 mark)

Assume $\sum h^2(k) = 1$ (normalized). Then $\sigma_y^2 = \sigma_x^2 = 1/3$.

**Step 6: Output SNR** (1 mark)

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{out}} = \frac{\sigma_y^2}{\sigma_{y,e}^2} = \frac{1/3}{32/12 \cdot 2^{-30}} = \frac{12}{3 \cdot 32} \cdot 2^{30} = \frac{2^{30}}{8} = 2^{27}
$$

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{out,dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(2^{27}) = 27 \times 3.01 = 81.3 \text{ dB}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. Students often forget to **square the order $N$** in the variance formula — it is **linear** in $N$, not $N^2$.
> 2. Many students write $\Delta = 2^{-b}$ instead of $2^{-(b-1)}$. For a $b$-bit two's-complement number, the LSB weight is $2^{-(b-1)}$, not $2^{-b}$.
> 3. The factor 12 in the variance comes from $\int_{-\Delta/2}^{\Delta/2} e^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\Delta} de = \Delta^2/12$. Show the integration or write the formula directly.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the effect of **coefficient quantization** in an FIR filter. How does the choice of filter structure (direct form vs cascade form) affect coefficient sensitivity? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the concept of **scaling** in fixed-point DSP. Derive the $L_\infty$ and $L_2$ scaling rules and explain when each is used. Apply $L_\infty$ scaling to a filter with $h = [0.5, 0.3, 0.2, 0.1]$ and input peak $x_{\max} = 2$. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Coefficient Quantization Effects (7 marks)

**Concept (3 marks):**
When filter coefficients are quantized from infinite precision to $b$ bits, the implemented transfer function $H_Q(z)$ deviates from $H(z)$. The deviation $\Delta H(e^{j\omega}) = H_Q(e^{j\omega}) - H(e^{j\omega})$ satisfies:

$$
\vert \Delta H(e^{j\omega}) \vert \le \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \vert e_C(k) \vert
$$

For an FIR filter, coefficient quantization moves the **zeros** of $H(z)$ (since $H(z) = h_0 \prod (1 - z_k z^{-1})$). For an IIR filter, coefficient quantization can **move poles** — a serious problem because poles near the unit circle may migrate **outside**, causing instability.

**Structure comparison (4 marks):**

| Structure | Pole/Zero Sensitivity | Recommended Use |
|:---|:---:|:---|
| Direct Form I/II | High for high-order filters | Low-order only |
| Cascade (Biquad Sections) | **Low** — each 2nd-order section has independent coefficients | Preferred for IIR |
| Parallel | Moderate | Stable realization |
| Lattice | **Very Low** | High-precision applications |

In **cascade form**, each 2nd-order section has coefficients of similar magnitude, so quantization affects each section uniformly. The total sensitivity is **distributed**, reducing worst-case deviation.

---

#### Part (b) — Scaling Rules (7 marks)

**Concept (2 marks):**
Scaling is the deliberate attenuation of the input signal to prevent overflow in the output. Trade-off: scaling reduces SNR by $20 \log_{10}(A)$ dB but guarantees no overflow.

**$L_\infty$ Rule Derivation (2 marks):** [Already shown in Section 3.5]

**$L_2$ Rule Derivation (2 marks):** For input with bounded energy $E_x = \sum x^2(n) \le E_{\max}$:

$$
E_y = E_x \cdot \sum h^2(k) \implies A = \frac{1}{\sqrt{E_{\max} \cdot \sum h^2(k)}}
$$

If $x_{\max}$ is the peak: $A = 1 / (x_{\max} \sqrt{\sum h^2(k)})$.

**Numerical Application (1 mark):** For $h = [0.5, 0.3, 0.2, 0.1]$, $x_{\max} = 2$:

$$
\sum \vert h(k) \vert = 0.5 + 0.3 + 0.2 + 0.1 = 1.1
$$

$$
A = \frac{1}{2 \cdot 1.1} = \frac{1}{2.2} \approx 0.4545
$$

Output of scaled filter satisfies $\vert y(n) \vert \le 1$ for all $n$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfalls — Scaling & Overflow:**
> 1. Students often **omit the $x_{\max}$ factor** in the denominator — it is mandatory, not optional.
> 2. For IIR filters, the relevant impulse response is the **infinite-length** $h(n)$, not just the numerator. Use $\|H\|_\infty = \max_\omega \vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert$ for IIR.
> 3. $L_2$ scaling does **not** guarantee zero overflow — it bounds **energy**, not peak value. Use $L_\infty$ when peak-bounded inputs are expected.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🎯 Rapid-Revision Checklist (Memorize Before Exam)

- [ ] **Quantization step** $\Delta = 2^{-(b-1)}$ — NOT $2^{-b}$ — for $b$-bit two's complement fraction.
- [ ] **SQNR formula:** $6.02b + 1.76$ dB — derive from signal power $A^2/2$ vs noise power $\Delta^2/12$.
- [ ] **Rounding error** in $[-\Delta/2, \Delta/2]$, mean **0**, variance $\Delta^2/12$.
- [ ] **Truncation error** in $[-\Delta, \Delta - 2^{-(b-1)}\Delta]$, mean **non-zero** for sign-magnitude, variance $\Delta^2/12$ for two's complement.
- [ ] **Output noise of FIR DF:** $N \cdot 2^{-2b}/3$ — variance scales **linearly** with filter length.
- [ ] **Output noise of FFT:** $2M \cdot 2^{-2b}/12$ where $M = \log_2 N$ — grows slowly with $N$.
- [ ] **$L_\infty$ scaling:** $A = 1/(x_{\max} \cdot \sum \vert h(k) \vert)$ — guarantees **no overflow**, conservative.
- [ ] **$L_2$ scaling:** $A = 1/(x_{\max} \cdot \sqrt{\sum h^2(k)})$ — allows more dynamic range, occasional clipping.
- [ ] **Overflow** in two's complement = **wrap-around** (catastrophic); saturation = **clamp** (modern, nonlinear).
- [ ] **Coefficient quantization** moves zeros of FIR and can destabilize IIR — use cascade/lattice for low sensitivity.
- [ ] **FFT growth:** output magnitude grows as $N$ for fixed-point, requiring **block-floating-point** or per-stage scaling.
- [ ] **Block-floating-point (BFP):** scale by $2^{-1}$ after each butterfly to keep dynamic range bounded.

### 📌 Critical Numerical Values to Remember

| $b$ (bits) | $\Delta$ | $\sigma_e^2 = \Delta^2/12$ | SQNR (dB) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 4 | $1.25 \times 10^{-1}$ | $1.30 \times 10^{-3}$ | 25.84 |
| 8 | $7.81 \times 10^{-3}$ | $5.09 \times 10^{-6}$ | 49.92 |
| 12 | $4.88 \times 10^{-4}$ | $1.99 \times 10^{-8}$ | 74.00 |
| 16 | $3.05 \times 10^{-5}$ | $7.77 \times 10^{-11}$ | 98.08 |

### 🧠 KTU Cognitive Levels Tested

| Bloom's Level | Typical Question Type | Marks |
|:---|:---|:---:|
| Remember | State formula for SQNR | 2 |
| Understand | Explain quantization model | 3 |
| Apply | Compute SQNR for given $b$ | 7 |
| Analyze | Derive output noise variance | 7 |
| Evaluate | Compare rounding vs truncation | 7 |
| Create | Design scaling for given filter | 14 |

> **Final KTU Tip:** In the 14-mark question, always begin with a **diagram** (block diagram of quantizer or FIR), state the **assumptions** of the noise model, **derive** the key formula step-by-step, and finish with a **numerical example**. KTU examiners reward structured answers with sub-headings.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DSP Algorithm Representations - Data Flow, Control Flow, Signal Flow Graphs, Block Diagrams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST526 - DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** DSP Algorithm Representations - Data Flow, Control Flow, Signal Flow Graphs, Block Diagrams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a DSP Algorithm Representation?

A **DSP Algorithm Representation** is a structured, visual, or textual way to describe how a digital signal processing computation flows from input samples to output samples. Every DSP algorithm — whether it is an **FIR filter**, an **IIR filter**, a **DFT**, or an **FFT butterfly** — can be drawn, simulated, or programmed only after it has been represented in a formal way.

The four most fundamental representation techniques mandated by the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for Module 4 are:

| Representation | What It Captures | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Block Diagram** | Functional operations on signals | Hardware realization & teaching |
| **Signal Flow Graph (SFG)** | Branch gains and signal nodes | Deriving $H(z)$ via Mason's rule |
| **Data Flow Graph (DFG)** | Precedence/dependency between operations | Parallel & VLSI implementation |
| **Control Flow** | Program execution order | Software / DSP processor coding |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under Outcome-Based Education (OBE), every representation must lead to the same transfer function $H(z)$ for a given filter. Representations differ in *appearance* — never in *mathematical content*.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🏭

Imagine a **water purification plant** as a DSP system:

- **Block Diagram** = The architect's blueprint — every pipe (delay), valve (multiplier), and tank (adder) is drawn pictorially.
- **Signal Flow Graph** = An engineer's schematic showing exactly how many litres/sec flow through each pipe, with arrows.
- **Data Flow Graph** = The plant supervisor's dependency chart — "Tank B cannot fill until Tank A is full."
- **Control Flow** = The shift operator's checklist — "Step 1: open valve, Step 2: wait 5 minutes, Step 3: close valve."

All four describe the **same plant** but optimize for **different questions** — design, analysis, parallelism, or programming.

---

## 1.3 Formal Definitions (KTU Board Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition 1 — Block Diagram**
> A *block diagram* is a graphical representation of a system in which the system components are represented by **interconnected blocks**, each block performing a specific mathematical operation (multiply, add, or delay).

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition 2 — Signal Flow Graph (Mason, 1953)**
> A *signal flow graph (SFG)* is a network of directed branches connecting nodes, in which each node represents a system variable and each branch represents a linear dependence between two nodes (a multiplication by the branch transmittance).

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition 3 — Data Flow Graph (DFG)**
> A *data flow graph* is a directed graph in which nodes denote computational tasks and edges denote data dependencies; the graph explicitly captures which operations must complete before others may begin.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition 4 — Control Flow**
> *Control flow* specifies the **order** in which individual statements, instructions, or function calls of a DSP algorithm are executed — including sequential execution, branching (`if-else`), and looping (`for`, `while`).

---

## 1.4 Primitive Building Blocks (Universal Vocabulary)

Every DSP representation is built from **four atomic operations**:

| Symbol | Operation | Mathematical Form | Hardware Equivalent |
|---|---|---|---|
| **→[ a ]→** | Multiplier | $y = a \cdot x$ | One MAC unit |
| **→⊕→** | Adder | $y = x_1 + x_2$ | One ALU add |
| **→[ z⁻¹ ]→** | Unit Delay | $y(n) = x(n-1)$ | One memory register |
| **•** (node) | Take-off / Sum-point | Splits or sums signal | Wire junction |

> [!TIP]
> Every block diagram, SFG, DFG, or control-flow description is **just a permutation of these four primitives** — nothing more, nothing less. Recognizing this immediately simplifies the KTU board questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The General N-th Order IIR Difference Equation

The starting point for every representation in Module 4 is the LCCDE (Linear Constant-Coefficient Difference Equation) of a causal IIR filter:

$$
y(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{N} a(k)\, y(n-k) + \sum_{k=0}^{M} b(k)\, x(n-k)
$$

Taking the **Z-transform** of both sides (assuming zero initial conditions):

$$
Y(z)\!\left[1 + \sum_{k=1}^{N} a(k)\, z^{-k}\right] = X(z)\!\left[\sum_{k=0}^{M} b(k)\, z^{-k}\right]
$$

Hence the **transfer function** is the ratio:

$$
H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{M} b(k)\, z^{-k}}{\displaystyle 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{N} a(k)\, z^{-k}} = \frac{B(z)}{A(z)}
$$

This single equation is the **mother equation** of all the representations discussed below.

---

## 2.2 Representation #1 — Block Diagram (Direct Form I)

Direct Form I follows the difference equation **literally**:

- One **adder** collects the delayed input terms $b(k)x(n-k)$.
- Another **adder** combines delayed output terms $-a(k)y(n-k)$.
- The sum of the two produces $y(n)$.

**Why Direct Form I is rarely used:** It needs **N + M** delay elements. For N = M (typical), this is wasteful — many delays can be shared.

---

## 2.3 Representation #2 — Direct Form II (Canonical Form)

By applying the **delay-sharing / transpose principle**, Direct Form I can be reduced to use only **max(N, M)** delay elements. The intermediate signal $w(n)$ is defined by:

$$
w(n) = x(n) - \sum_{k=1}^{N} a(k)\, w(n-k)
$$

$$
y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b(k)\, w(n-k)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Canonical form** = Direct Form II. It uses the **minimum possible number of delay elements** = $\max(N, M)$. KTU often asks: *"Which form is canonical?"* — Answer: **Direct Form II**.

---

## 2.4 Representation #3 — Transposed Direct Form II

Apply the **Transposition Theorem**:

1. Reverse the direction of every branch.
2. Exchange input ↔ output nodes.
3. Replace adders with take-off nodes (and vice-versa).

The transfer function $H(z)$ remains **identical** — this is mathematically guaranteed.

**Advantages for VLSI:**

- Same number of delays as Direct Form II (canonical).
- Better numerical behaviour — feedback loop has fewer critical-path multipliers.
- Preferred for pipelined / folded implementations.

---

## 2.5 Representation #4 — Signal Flow Graph (SFG)

Developed by **Samuel Jefferson Mason (1953)** at MIT, the SFG has these **rules**:

| Element | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Node** | A signal variable |
| **Directed Branch** | Linear dependence with branch *gain* |
| **Input (Source) Node** | Has only outgoing branches |
| **Output (Sink) Node** | Has only incoming branches |
| **Sum Node** | Value = sum of incoming branch values |
| **Path** | Continuous succession of branches with arrows aligned |
| **Loop** | Closed path that does not pass through any node more than once |

---

## 2.6 Mason's Gain Formula — The Crown Jewel 🔑

For *any* linear SFG, the transfer function between a source $X(z)$ and a sink $Y(z)$ is:

$$
T = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = \frac{1}{\Delta} \sum_{k=1}^{K} P_k\, \Delta_k
$$

where:

- $P_k$ = **path transmittance** of the $k^{th}$ forward path (source → sink).
- $\Delta$ = graph **determinant** of the entire SFG.
- $\Delta_k$ = cofactor of path $k$ = $\Delta$ of the subgraph with **all loops touching $P_k$ removed**.
- $K$ = total number of forward paths.

The graph determinant is:

$$
\Delta = 1 - \sum_{i} L_i + \sum_{i,j} L_i L_j - \sum_{i,j,k} L_i L_j L_k + \dots
$$

where $L_i$ are **individual loop gains**, and the products are restricted to **pairs / triplets of mutually non-touching loops**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Forgetting to *exclude* loops that **touch** the forward path when computing $\Delta_k$. Two loops *touch* if they share even a single node.

---

## 2.7 Representation #5 — Data Flow Graph (DFG)

A DFG for a DSP algorithm has:

- **Nodes** = atomic tasks (one MAC operation, one addition, etc.).
- **Directed edges** = data tokens flowing from producer → consumer.

Two tasks may execute **in parallel** if and only if **no path exists between them** in the DFG (i.e., they are independent). This is the foundation of:

- **Pipelining**
- **Parallel / VLIW DSP processors** (TMS320C6x, SHARC)
- **Hardware synthesis** (FPGA, ASIC)

---

## 2.8 Representation #6 — Control Flow Graph (CFG)

A CFG describes the **algorithmic sequencing**, not the data movement. Nodes represent basic blocks of statements; directed edges represent possible transfer of control. Constructs:

- **Sequential** → straight-line edges
- **Conditional (branch)** → diamonds, `if-else`
- **Iterative (loop)** → back-edges, `for`, `while`

The CFG is what a **C compiler** actually builds before generating assembly for a DSP processor.

---

## 2.9 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet 🧾

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression / Definition | Validity / Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | IIR LCCDE | $y(n) = -\sum a(k)y(n-k) + \sum b(k)x(n-k)$ | Causal LTI system |
| 2 | Transfer Function | $H(z) = B(z)/A(z) = \frac{\sum b(k)z^{-k}}{1 + \sum a(k)z^{-k}}$ | $z$ on ROC, $\vert z\vert > r_{max}$ |
| 3 | Direct Form I delays | $N + M$ | Non-canonical |
| 4 | Direct Form II delays | $\max(N,M)$ | **Canonical** |
| 5 | Intermediate signal $w(n)$ | $w(n) = x(n) - \sum a(k)w(n-k)$ | Defines Direct Form II |
| 6 | Output of Direct Form II | $y(n) = \sum b(k)w(n-k)$ | Final stage |
| 7 | Mason's Gain | $T = \frac{1}{\Delta}\sum P_k \Delta_k$ | Any linear SFG |
| 8 | Graph Determinant | $\Delta = 1 - \sum L_i + \sum L_i L_j - \dots$ | Non-touching loop products only |
| 9 | Non-touching Loops | Loops sharing **no common node** | Required for $L_i L_j$ terms |
| 10 | Transposition Rule | Reverse all branches, swap I/O | Preserves $H(z)$ |
| 11 | FIR $H(z)$ | $H(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h(k) z^{-k}$ | All-pole at origin, $A(z)=1$ |
| 12 | Linear-Phase FIR | $h(k) = \pm\, h(N-1-k)$ | Symmetric / anti-symmetric |
| 13 | DFG Parallelism | Tasks w/ no path between them | Execute concurrently |
| 14 | CFG Back-edge | Indicates loop in control flow | Generates `for`/`while` |
| 15 | Canonical Form Delays | $\max(N, M)$ | Lower bound for realizable SFG |

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize Rows 7, 8, 9.** They appear in nearly every 14-mark KTU Part B question on this module.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation #1 — Direct Form II for a 2nd-Order IIR Section

**Given:** IIR filter with

$$
H(z) = \frac{b_0 + b_1 z^{-1} + b_2 z^{-2}}{1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2}}
$$

### Step A — Expand $H(z)$ as a product $H(z) = H_1(z) \cdot H_2(z)$

$$
H(z) = \underbrace{\frac{1}{1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2}}}_{H_1(z) \text{ – All-pole part}} \cdot \underbrace{(b_0 + b_1 z^{-1} + b_2 z^{-2})}_{H_2(z) \text{ – All-zero part}}
$$

### Step B — Define intermediate signal $W(z)$

Let $W(z)$ be the output of the all-pole part:

$$
W(z) = H_1(z)\, X(z) = \frac{X(z)}{1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2}}
$$

Cross-multiplying:

$$
W(z)\,(1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2}) = X(z)
$$

Inverse Z-transforming term by term:

$$
w(n) + a_1 w(n-1) + a_2 w(n-2) = x(n)
$$

Solving for $w(n)$:

$$
w(n) = x(n) - a_1 w(n-1) - a_2 w(n-2)
$$

### Step C — Express $Y(z)$ in terms of $W(z)$

$$
Y(z) = H_2(z)\, W(z) = (b_0 + b_1 z^{-1} + b_2 z^{-2})\, W(z)
$$

Inverse Z-transform:

$$
y(n) = b_0\, w(n) + b_1\, w(n-1) + b_2\, w(n-2)
$$

### Step D — Count delay elements

Only $w(n-1)$ and $w(n-2)$ require memory registers → **2 delay elements** = $\max(N, M) = \max(2, 2) = 2$. **Canonical** ✓.

### Step E — Final Direct Form II Block Diagram (textual layout)

```
x(n) ──►(+)──────────────────────────────►[b0]──┐
        ▲▲                                      │
        ││                                      ▼
       (-a1) ◄──[z⁻¹]── w(n-1) ◄──[z⁻¹]── w(n-2)
        │                                      
       (-a2) ◄──────────────────────────────┘
```

(The "▲▲" indicates a feedback path into the adder.)

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation #2 — Mason's Gain Formula on a Generic IIR SFG

**Consider the SFG for Direct Form II of the 2nd-order IIR filter above.**

### Step 1 — Identify Forward Paths

There is **one** forward path from $X(z)$ to $Y(z)$ passing through $b_0$:

$$
P_1 = b_0
$$

(plus contributions from the delays multiplied by $b_1, b_2$).

In full SFG form there are **three forward paths**:

$$
P_1 = b_0, \qquad P_2 = b_1 z^{-1}, \qquad P_3 = b_2 z^{-2}
$$

### Step 2 — Identify All Loops

Two feedback loops exist:

$$
L_1 = -a_1 z^{-1}, \qquad L_2 = -a_2 z^{-2}
$$

These two loops **touch** (they share the $W$ node), so the product $L_1 L_2$ is **NOT** a non-touching-loop pair.

### Step 3 — Compute Graph Determinant $\Delta$

Since $L_1$ and $L_2$ touch:

$$
\Delta = 1 - (L_1 + L_2) = 1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute Cofactors $\Delta_k$

Each forward path touches both loops → $\Delta_1 = \Delta_2 = \Delta_3 = 1$.

### Step 5 — Apply Mason's Formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(z) &= \frac{1}{\Delta}\sum_{k=1}^{3} P_k\, \Delta_k \\[4pt]
     &= \frac{1}{1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2}} \bigl(b_0 + b_1 z^{-1} + b_2 z^{-2}\bigr) \\[4pt]
     &= \frac{b_0 + b_1 z^{-1} + b_2 z^{-2}}{1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

✓ **Result matches $H(z)$ exactly** — confirming Mason's formula is internally consistent.

---

## 3.3 Worked Derivation #3 — Transposition of Direct Form II

Applying the **Transposition Theorem** to the SFG above:

| Rule | Action |
|---|---|
| 1 | Reverse every branch arrow direction |
| 2 | Swap the original source $X(z)$ with the original sink $Y(z)$ |
| 3 | Replace every **sum-node** by a **take-off (split) node** |
| 4 | Replace every **take-off node** by a **sum-node** |

The new graph has **identical** $H(z)$ — proven below.

### Verification

The transposed form realizes:

$$
w'(n) = b_0\, x(n) + w'(n-2)
$$

with two feedback paths carrying $b_1, b_2$ coefficients instead of $-a_1, -a_2$. The overall transfer function is invariant under transposition by the **Reciprocity Theorem** for linear networks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why prefer the transposed form?** In VLSI, all multiplications occur *outside* the feedback loop → shorter critical path → higher clock frequency.

---

## 3.4 FIR Direct Form — A Simpler Special Case

For an FIR filter ($A(z) = 1$):

$$
H(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h(k)\, z^{-k}
$$

Direct Form block diagram is a pure **tapped delay line**:

```
x(n)──►[z⁻¹]──[z⁻¹]──[z⁻¹]── ... ──►
         │       │       │
        [h1]    [h2]    [h3]
         ▼       ▼       ▼
         └───►(+)───►(+)───► ... ──► y(n)
         [+h0·x(n) at the front]
```

Number of delays = $N - 1$. Transposed FIR direct form has the multipliers on the *input side*, summing along the delay chain.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Board Favourite)

| Property | Direct Form I | Direct Form II | Transposed DF-II |
|---|---|---|---|
| Delays required | $N + M$ | $\max(N, M)$ | $\max(N, M)$ |
| Canonical? | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| Multiplications in feedback loop | Many | Many | **Zero** |
| Critical path | Long | Long | **Short** |
| VLSI suitability | Poor | Moderate | **Best** |
| Numerical sensitivity | High | High | Lower |
| Ease of drawing | Easiest | Moderate | Harder |
| Same $H(z)$? | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Generic IIR Block Diagram (Direct Form I)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["x(n)"] --> B["b0"]
    B --> C["Sum Node 1"]
    A2["x(n)"] --> D["z^-1"]
    D --> E["b1"]
    E --> C
    D --> F["z^-1"]
    F --> G["b2"]
    G --> C
    C --> H["Sum Node 2"]
    H --> I["y(n)"]
    I --> J["z^-1"]
    J --> K["-a1"]
    K --> H
    I --> L["z^-1"]
    L --> M["-a2"]
    M --> H
```

*Interpretation:* Input $x(n)$ passes through a tapped-delay line multiplied by $b_0, b_1, b_2$. Output $y(n)$ is fed back through another tapped-delay line scaled by $-a_1, -a_2$. Total delays = **4** (= N + M for N = M = 2). This is **not canonical**.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Direct Form II (Canonical) Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["x(n)"] --> S1((Sum))
    S1 --> M0["b0"]
    M0 --> OUT["y(n)"]
    S1 --> D1["z^-1"]
    D1 --> M1["b1"]
    M1 --> OUT
    D1 --> D2["z^-1"]
    D2 --> M2["b2"]
    M2 --> OUT
    D1 --> FB1["-a1"]
    FB1 --> S1
    D2 --> FB2["-a2"]
    FB2 --> S1
```

*Interpretation:* Only **2 delays** are used (canonical). The intermediate node after the first delay feeds back via $-a_1$ and $-a_2$ to the summing junction.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Transposed Direct Form II Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["x(n)"] --> T1((Tap))
    T1 --> M0["b0"]
    M0 --> S1((Sum))
    S1 --> OUT["y(n)"]
    T1 --> D1["z^-1"]
    D1 --> M1["b1"]
    M1 --> S2((Sum))
    S2 --> D2["z^-1"]
    D2 --> M2["b2"]
    M2 --> S3((Sum))
    S3 --> D3["z^-1"]
    D3 --> FB1["-a1"]
    FB1 --> S2
    S3 --> FB2["-a2"]
    FB2 --> S2
```

*Interpretation:* All multipliers are now on the **input side** of the delays. Feedback paths contain *only* $-a_1, -a_2$ multipliers and adders — no input-multiplication in the feedback loop. Critical path is minimized.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Signal Flow Graph (Mason) of Direct Form II

```mermaid
graph LR
    X["X(z) Source"] -->|"1"| W1((W))
    W1 -->|"b0"| Y["Y(z) Sink"]
    W1 -->|"z^-1"| W2((W1))
    W2 -->|"b1"| Y
    W2 -->|"z^-1"| W3((W2))
    W3 -->|"b2"| Y
    W2 -->|"-a1 z^-1"| W1
    W3 -->|"-a2 z^-2"| W1
```

*Forward Paths:* $P_1 = b_0$, $P_2 = b_1 z^{-1}$, $P_3 = b_2 z^{-2}$.
*Loops:* $L_1 = -a_1 z^{-1}$, $L_2 = -a_2 z^{-2}$ — they *touch* (share node $W$).
*Graph Determinant:* $\Delta = 1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2}$.

---

## 4.5 Diagram 5 — Data Flow Graph for an FIR Tapped Delay Line

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["x(n) input"] --> MUL0["x h0"]
    A --> D1["z^-1 delay"]
    D1 --> MUL1["x h1"]
    D1 --> D2["z^-1 delay"]
    D2 --> MUL2["x h2"]
    D2 --> D3["z^-1 delay"]
    D3 --> MUL3["x h3"]
    MUL0 --> ADD1((+))
    MUL1 --> ADD1
    ADD1 --> ADD2((+))
    MUL2 --> ADD2
    ADD2 --> ADD3((+))
    MUL3 --> ADD3
    ADD3 --> Y["y(n) output"]
```

*Interpretation:* Multiplications and additions can be **pipelined** — multiplications $h_0 x(n), h_1 x(n-1), h_2 x(n-2), h_3 x(n-3)$ are *independent* and can run in parallel on a VLIW DSP.

---

## 4.6 Diagram 6 — Control Flow Graph for an FIR Filter Loop

```mermaid
graph TD
    INIT["Initialize: n=0, acc=0"] --> CHECK{"n less than N?"}
    CHECK -->|Yes| FETCH["Fetch x(n-k)"]
    FETCH --> MAC["acc = acc + h(k) * x(n-k)"]
    MAC --> INCR["k = k + 1"]
    INCR --> CHECK
    CHECK -->|No| OUTPUT["OUTPUT y(n) = acc"]
    OUTPUT --> STOP["STOP / next sample"]
```

*Interpretation:* This is the classic **single-MAC DSP loop**. KTU often asks how this would change for a **block-FIR** (vectorized) or a **parallel MAC** architecture.

---

## 4.7 Diagram 7 — Master Mapping: Representation ⇄ Purpose

```mermaid
graph TD
    ALG["DSP Algorithm: H(z) = B(z)/A(z)"] --> BD["Block Diagram"]
    ALG --> SFG["Signal Flow Graph"]
    ALG --> DFG["Data Flow Graph"]
    ALG --> CFG["Control Flow"]
    BD -->|"Teaching, hardware intuition"| OUT1["Best for pedagogy"]
    SFG -->|"Mason's formula"| OUT2["Best for analytic H(z)"]
    DFG -->|"Parallel scheduling"| OUT3["Best for VLSI / DSP chips"]
    CFG -->|"Program listing"| OUT4["Best for software coding"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]**
*What is a canonical realization of an IIR filter? Why is Direct Form II called canonical?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A realization is called **canonical** if it uses the **minimum possible number of delay elements** to realize the given transfer function. For an IIR filter of order $N$ with $M$ non-zero numerator coefficients, the theoretical lower bound on the number of delays is $\max(N, M)$. **Direct Form II achieves exactly this bound** by sharing the delay line between the all-pole section and the all-zero section, eliminating the redundancy of Direct Form I which uses $N + M$ delays. Hence, Direct Form II is called the canonical form of an IIR realization.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
*State Mason's Gain Formula. Define the terms forward path and non-touching loop.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Mason's Gain Formula expresses the transfer function of a linear signal flow graph as:

$$
T = \frac{1}{\Delta}\sum_{k=1}^{K} P_k\, \Delta_k
$$

A **forward path** is a directed path from the input (source) node to the output (sink) node that does **not** traverse any node more than once. Its gain $P_k$ is the product of the branch transmittances along the path. **Non-touching loops** are two or more loops in the SFG that share **no common node** between them. They appear as products $L_i L_j$ in the graph determinant $\Delta$, which is computed as $\Delta = 1 - \sum L_i + \sum L_i L_j - \sum L_i L_j L_k + \dots$ — products restricted to non-touching pairs/triplets only.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Marks Lost Here Most Often:**
> 1. **Drawing the SFG with arrows in the wrong direction** — loses **2 marks** immediately.
> 2. **Forgetting to remove loops touching the forward path when computing $\Delta_k$** — loses **3 marks**.
> 3. **Confusing $a_k$ with $-a_k$ signs in the feedback loop** — loses **2 marks**.
> 4. **Not stating Mason's formula before applying it** — loses **1 mark** even if the final $H(z)$ is correct.
> 5. **Drawing Direct Form II with too many delay elements** — examiner will deduct marks for non-canonical realization.

---

### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** For the IIR filter described by the difference equation

$$
y(n) = 1.2\, y(n-1) - 0.5\, y(n-2) + x(n) + 0.6\, x(n-1)
$$

**(i)** Derive the transfer function $H(z)$.
**(ii)** Draw the **Direct Form I** block diagram.
**(iii)** Draw the **Direct Form II (canonical)** block diagram.
**(iv)** State the number of delay elements used in each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Convert the Direct Form II block diagram into a **Signal Flow Graph**, and using **Mason's Gain Formula**, verify that the transfer function is identical to that obtained in (a)(i). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to A(a)

**Step 1 — Take Z-transform of the LCCDE**

Taking the Z-transform of both sides of the given difference equation, with zero initial conditions:

$$
Y(z) = 1.2\, z^{-1} Y(z) - 0.5\, z^{-2} Y(z) + X(z) + 0.6\, z^{-1} X(z)
$$

**Step 2 — Collect $Y(z)$ terms on the LHS**

$$
Y(z) - 1.2\, z^{-1} Y(z) + 0.5\, z^{-2} Y(z) = X(z) (1 + 0.6\, z^{-1})
$$

$$
Y(z)\, (1 - 1.2\, z^{-1} + 0.5\, z^{-2}) = X(z)\, (1 + 0.6\, z^{-1})
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $H(z) = Y(z)/X(z)$**

$$
\boxed{H(z) = \frac{1 + 0.6\, z^{-1}}{1 - 1.2\, z^{-1} + 0.5\, z^{-2}}}
$$

Here $b_0 = 1, b_1 = 0.6, a_1 = -1.2, a_2 = 0.5$. So $N = 2$, $M = 1$.

**Step 4 — Direct Form I block diagram**

DF-I uses $N + M = 2 + 1 = 3$ delay elements (non-canonical):

```
x(n)──►[z⁻¹]──[z⁻¹]──
        │       │
       [0.6]   [0]      (only one non-zero b delay because M=1)
        ▼
   (+)──────►(+)────────► y(n)
        ▲       ▲
        │       │
   (-(-1.2))  (-0.5)
        ▲       ▲
        └──[z⁻¹]──[z⁻¹]◄──y(n)
```

The output $y(n)$ is fed back through a 2-tap delay line multiplied by $-1.2, +0.5$ and added to the input section. (Examiner accepts clean textual block diagrams.)

**Step 5 — Direct Form II block diagram**

Using the canonical structure with only $\max(N, M) = 2$ delays:

Define the intermediate signal:

$$
w(n) = x(n) + 1.2\, w(n-1) - 0.5\, w(n-2)
$$

$$
y(n) = 1.0\, w(n) + 0.6\, w(n-1)
$$

```
        ┌──(-0.5)──────────────┐
        │                      │
x(n)─►(+)─►[z⁻¹]─►[z⁻¹]─►(+)─► y(n)
        ▲             │     ▲
        │             ▼     │
        └─────(+1.2)──┘    [0.6]
```

**Step 6 — Number of delays**

- Direct Form I: **3 delays**
- Direct Form II: **2 delays (canonical)** ✓

---

#### Model Solution to A(b)

**Step 1 — Draw the Signal Flow Graph from Direct Form II**

Nodes: $X$, $W$, $W_1$, $Y$. Branches:

- $X \to W$: gain $1$
- $W \to Y$: gain $b_0 = 1$
- $W \to W_1$: gain $z^{-1}$
- $W_1 \to Y$: gain $b_1 z^{-1} = 0.6\, z^{-1}$ (i.e., $W \to W_1$ contributes $0.6\, z^{-1}$ to $Y$ via branch gain $0.6$)
- $W_1 \to W_2$: gain $z^{-1}$
- $W_2 \to Y$: gain $b_2 = 0$ (negligible since $M = 1$, $b_2 = 0$)
- $W_1 \to W$: gain $-a_1 = +1.2$ (feedback)
- $W_2 \to W$: gain $-a_2 = -(-0.5) = +0.5$ → actually $-a_2 z^{-2}$ = $-0.5\, z^{-2}$ in the loop gain expression with $a_2 = 0.5$ → feedback gain is $-a_2 = -0.5$.

(Examiner expects students to clearly label each branch with its transmittance.)

**Step 2 — Identify Forward Paths**

Since $M = 1$ and $b_2 = 0$, we have effectively **two forward paths**:

$$
P_1 = 1 \cdot 1 = 1, \qquad P_2 = 1 \cdot z^{-1} \cdot 0.6 = 0.6\, z^{-1}
$$

**Step 3 — Identify Loops**

Two feedback loops:

$$
L_1 = (-1.2)\, z^{-1}, \qquad L_2 = (+0.5)\, z^{-2}
$$

(Note: $a_1 = -1.2$ → $-a_1 z^{-1} = +1.2 z^{-1}$, but standard Mason's formulation uses the actual branch gain written on the diagram, which is $-a_k = -(-1.2) = +1.2$. Using $L_1 = +1.2 z^{-1}$, $L_2 = +0.5 z^{-2}$ — these touch via node $W_1$.)

Both loops **touch** (share node $W_1$).

**Step 4 — Compute Graph Determinant $\Delta$**

$$
\Delta = 1 - (L_1 + L_2) = 1 - 1.2\, z^{-1} - 0.5\, z^{-2}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Cofactors $\Delta_k$**

Each forward path touches both loops → $\Delta_1 = \Delta_2 = 1$.

**Step 6 — Apply Mason's Formula**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(z) &= \frac{P_1 \Delta_1 + P_2 \Delta_2}{\Delta} \\[4pt]
     &= \frac{1 + 0.6\, z^{-1}}{1 - 1.2\, z^{-1} + 0.5\, z^{-2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

✓ **Result matches Step 3 of A(a). Mason's Gain Formula is verified.**

---

### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram, the **transposed Direct Form II** realization of a general Nth-order IIR filter. State and prove the **Transposition Theorem**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the FIR filter with impulse response

$$
h(n) = \{0.5,\ 1.0,\ 0.5\}, \quad n = 0, 1, 2
$$

**(i)** Draw the **Direct Form** and **Transposed Direct Form** block diagrams.
**(ii)** Draw the corresponding **Data Flow Graph** and identify **two operations** that can be executed in parallel.
**(iii)** Write a **Control Flow description** (pseudocode) of how a single-MAC DSP would compute one output sample. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to B(a)

**Step 1 — Transposed Direct Form II Block Diagram**

For a general Nth-order IIR filter with transfer function

$$
H(z) = \frac{\sum_{k=0}^{M} b(k) z^{-k}}{1 + \sum_{k=1}^{N} a(k) z^{-k}}
$$

the transposed Direct Form II uses **N** delay elements (assuming $N \geq M$) and realises:

```
                       ┌────[-a(N)]◄──┐
                       │              │
x(n)─►[b0]─►(+)─►(+)─► ... ─►(+)─►[b(N-1)]─►...─► y(n)
        ▲    ▲    ▲             ▲
        │    │    │             │
        └─[z⁻¹]──┘              (feedback through delays)
```

In the transposed structure, **all input multipliers $b_k$ sit before the delays**, while the feedback multipliers $-a_k$ sit on the *output side* of the delays.

**Step 2 — Transposition Theorem (Statement)**

> *If a linear time-invariant network is realizable as a signal flow graph, then the graph obtained by reversing the direction of every branch, interchanging the roles of input and output nodes, and exchanging sum nodes with take-off nodes, has the **same transfer function** as the original.*

**Step 3 — Proof Sketch**

Consider a node $v_i$ in the original graph. Its signal value is the sum of all incoming branch values:

$$
v_i = \sum_{j} g_{ji}\, v_j
$$

where $g_{ji}$ is the gain of the branch $j \to i$. After transposition:

- The branch $j \to i$ becomes $i \to j$ with **reciprocal gain** (or the same gain, depending on the formulation).
- The set of equations for the new graph can be shown, by simple matrix algebra, to be the **transpose** of the original system matrix.
- The transfer function $H(z)$ is the ratio of output to input — this ratio is invariant under transposition of the system matrix because the determinant $\det(I - A)$ equals $\det(I - A^{T})$.

Hence $H_{transposed}(z) = H_{original}(z)$. ∎

**Engineering Utility:** The transposed form is preferred in **VLSI implementation** because all input-path multiplications occur *outside* the feedback loop, reducing the **critical path** and enabling higher clock rates.

---

#### Model Solution to B(b)

**Step 1 — Direct Form Block Diagram of the 3-tap FIR**

The transfer function is

$$
H(z) = 0.5 + 1.0\, z^{-1} + 0.5\, z^{-2}
$$

Direct Form (tapped delay line):

```
x(n)─►[z⁻¹]──[z⁻¹]──
        │       │
      [1.0]   [0.5]
        ▼       ▼
       (+)◄──[0.5]── x(n)
        │
        ▼
       y(n)
```

Three multipliers ($h_0 = 0.5, h_1 = 1.0, h_2 = 0.5$) and **two** delay elements.

**Step 2 — Transposed Direct Form**

```
x(n)─►(+)─►[z⁻¹]─►(+)─►[z⁻¹]─►(sink)
        ▲         ▲
       [0.5]     [1.0]
        ▲         ▲
       (...)     (...)
```

(Standard transposed FIR structure: input goes through a summing chain; the multipliers inject the input at each node.)

**Step 3 — Data Flow Graph & Parallel Operations**

```
   x(n) ──┬──►×h0──┐
          │         ├──►(+)─►×h1──┐
          │         │              ├──►(+)─► y(n)
          └──►[z⁻¹]─┼──►×h1'──┐    │
                       │       │    │
                       └──►[z⁻¹]──►×h2──►(...)
```

**Two operations that can run in parallel:**

1. **Multiply $h_0 \cdot x(n)$** and **delay $x(n)$ to produce $x(n-1)$** — these are independent.
2. **Multiply $h_1 \cdot x(n-1)$** and **delay $x(n-1)$ to produce $x(n-2)$** — independent of step 1 once $x(n-1)$ is ready.

On a dual-MAC DSP (e.g., SHARC), this allows the filter throughput to **double**.

**Step 4 — Control Flow Pseudocode**

```
INPUT  : x_new            // newest input sample
OUTPUT : y                // computed output sample

// Load previous samples from memory
x_old1 = mem[x_n_minus_1]
x_old2 = mem[x_n_minus_2]

// Single-MAC computation (sequential)
acc = h0 * x_new
acc = acc + h1 * x_old1
acc = acc + h2 * x_old2

y = acc

// Update delay line for next call
mem[x_n_minus_2] = x_old1
mem[x_n_minus_1] = x_new

RETURN y
```

This is the canonical **direct-form FIR loop** — three multiplications, two additions, executed sequentially on a single-MAC processor.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember 🧠

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 (DSP Algorithm Representations)**

- [x] A DSP algorithm can be drawn in **4 ways**: Block Diagram, Signal Flow Graph, Data Flow Graph, Control Flow — all represent the **same $H(z)$**.
- [x] The **atomic primitives** of every representation are: **Multiplier, Adder, Delay ($z^{-1}$), Take-off node**.
- [x] **Direct Form I** uses $N + M$ delays → **NOT canonical**.
- [x] **Direct Form II** uses $\max(N, M)$ delays → **CANONICAL** (most asked term).
- [x] **Transposed Direct Form II** has the same number of delays as DF-II but **fewer multipliers in the feedback loop** → best for VLSI.
- [x] **Mason's Gain Formula**: $T = \frac{1}{\Delta}\sum P_k \Delta_k$ — must be applied to a properly drawn SFG with **all branch directions marked**.
- [x] **Graph determinant** $\Delta = 1 - \sum L_i + \sum L_i L_j - \dots$ — products only for **non-touching** loops.
- [x] **Cofactor** $\Delta_k$ uses the *subgraph* obtained by **removing all loops that touch the $k^{th}$ forward path**.
- [x] **Two loops touch** if they share at least **one common node** (not just a branch).
- [x] **Transposition Theorem**: reverse arrows + swap I/O + exchange sum/tap nodes → **identical $H(z)$**.
- [x] **DFG captures parallelism**: independent nodes (no path between them) may execute concurrently.
- [x] **CFG captures program sequencing**: nodes are basic blocks, edges are control transfers (sequential, branch, loop-back).
- [x] **FIR canonical**: Direct Form with $N-1$ delays (no feedback → inherently stable).
- [x] **Linear-phase FIR** has symmetric impulse response: $h(k) = h(N-1-k)$ → exploits multiplier-sharing to halve hardware.
- [x] For **2nd-order IIR** ($N=M=2$), always expect the examiner to ask for: DF-I, DF-II, Transposed DF-II, and Mason's verification.
- [x] **Don't forget the sign convention**: a coefficient $a_k$ in the LCCDE corresponds to branch gain $-a_k$ in the SFG.
- [x] **Mason's formula is a verification tool**, not a design tool — design first, verify using Mason's afterwards.
- [x] Common KTU 14-mark question pattern: "Derive $H(z)$ → Draw DF-II → Convert to SFG → Apply Mason's → Verify."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Loop Bound, Iteration Bound, Critical Path
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST526 - DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Loop Bound, Iteration Bound, Critical Path

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Loop Bound (L∞)

**Definition:** The Loop Bound, denoted $L_{\infty}$, is the maximum time interval available between two consecutive iterations of a recursive algorithm when implemented in hardware. It is numerically equal to the **sampling period** $T$ of the discrete-time system.

$$L_{\infty} = T_s = \frac{1}{f_s}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Note:** The loop bound represents a *physical/architectural constraint* imposed by the input signal. No recursive loop in the data-flow graph may take longer than $T_s$ to complete, otherwise the system becomes *time-unfeasible* (i.e., it cannot keep up with the incoming samples).

### 🎯 Intuitive Analogy

> Think of a **revolving door** at a busy building entrance. People (input samples) arrive every 2 seconds. The door must complete **one full rotation in ≤ 2 seconds**, otherwise people will pile up. Here, the **2-second interval = Loop Bound**. If the door takes 3 seconds, the system fails — just like a DSP chip missing real-time deadlines.

---

## 1.2 Iteration Bound (B∞ or B)

**Definition:** The Iteration Bound is the *theoretical, architectural* lower limit on the *iteration period* of a recursive algorithm. It is computed purely from the **internal structure** of the data-flow graph (DFG) — its loops, computational operations, and delay elements.

$$B_{\infty} = \max_{l \in \text{all loops}} \left\{ \frac{T_{l}}{W_{l}} \right\}$$

where:
- $T_l$ = total computation time around loop $l$ (sum of execution times of all operations in $l$)
- $W_l$ = total number of delay elements in loop $l$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** The loop bound is an *external* constraint (from the application), while the iteration bound is an *internal* property (from the DFG). The *achievable iteration period* $T_{ach}$ must always satisfy:
> 
> $$B_{\infty} \;\leq\; T_{ach} \;\leq\; L_{\infty}$$
> 
> If $B_{\infty} > L_{\infty}$, the algorithm **cannot** be implemented in real time on this hardware.

### 🎯 Intuitive Analogy

> Imagine a **relay race team** where runners are arranged in a circular track with several hurdles (delays). The **iteration bound** is the absolute fastest time a runner can possibly complete one full lap, *regardless* of how fast the coach (clock) signals. Even if the coach wants the next sample every 2 seconds, the team physically cannot run a lap in less than 3 seconds — so the system fails.

---

## 1.3 Critical Path (CP or $T_{cp}$)

**Definition:** The Critical Path is the **longest combinational (delay-free) computation path** through the data-flow graph — i.e., the slowest path of multiply/add operations between any two successive delay elements. It determines the **minimum possible clock period** of the hardware.

$$T_{clk} \geq T_{cp}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why it matters:** A large critical path means the clock must be slowed down. The goal of pipelining and unfolding is precisely to *shorten* the critical path, thereby increasing the achievable clock rate.

### 🎯 Intuitive Analogy

> Think of an **assembly line** where each station performs a task. The **critical path** is the **slowest station in sequence** — that station's processing time dictates the speed of the entire line. To speed it up, you must split the slowest task across multiple stations (pipelining).

---

## 📊 Comparison Snapshot

| Parameter | Symbol | Set By | Limits |
|-----------|--------|--------|--------|
| Loop Bound | $L_{\infty}$ | Application (sampling rate) | Maximum allowed iteration time |
| Iteration Bound | $B_{\infty}$ | DFG topology + operator delays | Minimum possible iteration time |
| Critical Path | $T_{cp}$ | Longest combinational chain | Minimum clock period |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Framework

### 2.1.1 Computation of the Iteration Bound

The iteration bound is obtained by enumerating **every directed cycle** in the DFG and computing its **mean cycle cost** (also called the **loop bound of that loop**).

For each loop $l$ in the DFG:

$$\text{Loop bound of } l = \frac{T_l}{W_l}$$

where:
- $T_l = \sum_{i \in l} t_i$ → sum of execution times of all nodes (operations) in loop $l$
- $W_l = \sum_{i \in l} w_i$ → sum of delay elements in loop $l$

The **iteration bound** is then the **maximum** loop bound across all loops:

$$B_{\infty} = \max_{l} \left\{ \frac{T_l}{W_l} \right\}$$

### 2.1.2 Why Take the Maximum?

Each loop $l$ in the DFG requires at least $\frac{T_l}{W_l}$ time units per iteration (because the loop carries $W_l$ delays, and the data must traverse $T_l$ worth of computation between successive uses of the same delay). The overall algorithm cannot iterate faster than the **slowest** of its constituent loops.

### 2.1.3 Critical Path Computation

For a DFG, the critical path is found by:

1. Identifying all **acyclic** (delay-free) computation paths in the DFG.
2. Summing the execution times $t_i$ of each operation along these paths.
3. Taking the **maximum** sum.

$$T_{cp} = \max_{\text{path } p \in \text{acyclic paths}} \sum_{i \in p} t_i$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Loop bound vs. Critical path vs. Iteration bound — the golden inequality:**
> 
> $$T_{cp} \;\leq\; B_{\infty} \;\leq\; L_{\infty}$$
> 
> - The critical path is the lower bound on what a single cycle of execution *must* take (combinational delay).
> - The iteration bound is the *tightest* lower bound on the iteration period achievable via architectural transformations.
> - The loop bound is the *application-imposed upper limit*.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $L_{\infty} = T_s = \frac{1}{f_s}$ | Loop bound = sampling period | seconds |
| 2 | $\text{LB}_l = \frac{T_l}{W_l}$ | Loop bound of an individual loop $l$ | seconds |
| 3 | $B_{\infty} = \max_l \left\{ \frac{T_l}{W_l} \right\}$ | Iteration bound (DFG-wide) | seconds |
| 4 | $T_{cp} = \max_p \left\{ \sum_{i \in p} t_i \right\}$ | Critical path (longest combinational path) | seconds |
| 5 | $T_{clk,\min} = T_{cp}$ | Minimum clock period | seconds |
| 6 | $f_{clk,\max} = \frac{1}{T_{cp}}$ | Maximum clock frequency | Hz |
| 7 | $B_{\infty} \leq L_{\infty}$ | Feasibility condition for real-time | — |
| 8 | $W_l = \sum_i w_i$ | Total delays in loop $l$ | count (integer) |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Audio codecs (mp3, AAC)**: Sampling rates of 44.1 kHz impose $L_{\infty} \approx 22.7\;\mu s$. Designers must keep $B_{\infty}$ below this to enable real-time decoding.
- **5G baseband processing**: Sampling rates in the hundreds of MHz demand extremely tight $L_{\infty}$ values, requiring pipelining and parallel unfolding.
- **Hearing aids & wearables**: Ultra-low-power DSPs exploit high $B_{\infty}$ margins to run on slow clocks and conserve battery.
- **Radar / Software-Defined Radio**: Critical-path optimization enables GHz-class sample processing on FPGAs and ASICs.

---

## 2.4 Algorithmic Steps to Compute the Iteration Bound (KTU Exam Method)

1. **Draw the DFG** of the recursive algorithm, marking every node's execution time $t_i$ and every edge's delay $w_i$ (often 1 unit for $z^{-1}$).
2. **Enumerate all directed loops** in the DFG (use a directed-graph cycle detection).
3. For each loop, compute:
   - $T_l = \sum t_i$ along the loop
   - $W_l = \sum w_i$ along the loop
   - $\text{LB}_l = T_l / W_l$
4. Take the **maximum** LB$_l$ → that is the iteration bound $B_{\infty}$.
5. Compare $B_{\infty}$ with $L_{\infty}$ to check **real-time feasibility**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Example

## 3.1 Worked Example: Second-Order IIR Filter

Consider the second-order IIR difference equation:

$$y[n] = a_1\, y[n-1] + a_2\, y[n-2] + b_0\, x[n]$$

### Step 1 — Build the Data-Flow Graph (DFG)

Each **multiplication** takes $t_m$ time units; each **addition** takes $t_a$ time units.

- Multiplications: $a_1 \cdot y[n-1]$, $a_2 \cdot y[n-2]$, $b_0 \cdot x[n]$ → **3 multiplies** ($3 t_m$)
- Additions: 2 additions to sum the three products → **2 adds** ($2 t_a$)

> Assume typical values: $t_m = 20\;\text{ns}$, $t_a = 5\;\text{ns}$.

### Step 2 — Identify the Loops

**Loop 1** (via $a_1$ feedback): $y[n] \rightarrow \text{mult}(a_1) \rightarrow \text{add} \rightarrow z^{-1} \rightarrow y[n-1] \rightarrow y[n]$

- Operations: 1 multiply + 1 add
- Delays: 1 (the $z^{-1}$ storing $y[n-1]$)
- $T_{l_1} = t_m + t_a = 20 + 5 = 25\;\text{ns}$
- $W_{l_1} = 1$

**Loop 2** (via $a_2$ feedback): similar structure but uses $y[n-2]$

- $T_{l_2} = t_m + t_a = 25\;\text{ns}$
- $W_{l_2} = 1$

### Step 3 — Compute the Loop Bound of Each Loop

$$\text{LB}_{l_1} = \frac{T_{l_1}}{W_{l_1}} = \frac{25}{1} = 25\;\text{ns}$$

$$\text{LB}_{l_2} = \frac{T_{l_2}}{W_{l_2}} = \frac{25}{1} = 25\;\text{ns}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Iteration Bound

$$B_{\infty} = \max \left\{ \frac{25}{1},\; \frac{25}{1} \right\} = 25\;\text{ns}$$

### Step 5 — Compute the Critical Path

The longest delay-free path goes from $y[n-1]$ (output of $z^{-1}$) through the multiplier $a_1$, then the adder, with no delay in between:

$$T_{cp} = t_m + t_a = 20 + 5 = 25\;\text{ns}$$

### Step 6 — Feasibility Check

For an audio application with $f_s = 44.1\;\text{kHz}$:

$$L_{\infty} = T_s = \frac{1}{44.1 \times 10^{3}} \approx 22.68\;\mu s = 22{,}680\;\text{ns}$$

Compare:

$$B_{\infty} = 25\;\text{ns} \;\ll\; L_{\infty} = 22{,}680\;\text{ns} \quad \checkmark$$

✅ **Feasible.** Plenty of margin; we could even slow the clock.

---

## 3.2 Illustrative Algebraic Formulation (General Case)

For a DFG with $L$ directed loops, the iteration bound is formally:

$$B_{\infty} = \max_{l = 1, 2, \ldots, L} \left\{ \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{i \in l} t_i}{\displaystyle\sum_{i \in l} w_i} \right\}$$

If we let $u_l = T_l$ (numerator) and $v_l = W_l$ (denominator), this is a **linear-fractional optimization** problem. The Longest Path Matrix (LPM) algorithm and Karp's algorithm solve it systematically:

$$B_{\infty} = \max_{l} \left\{ \frac{u_l}{v_l} \right\} = \text{solution to } \max_{l} u_l - B_{\infty}\, v_l = 0$$

This is the equation whose **smallest non-negative root** gives $B_{\infty}$.

---

## 3.3 Detailed Numerical Example (Loops with Multiple Delays)

Consider a DFG with two loops:

- **Loop A:** $T_A = 40\;\text{ns}$, $W_A = 2$ delays
- **Loop B:** $T_B = 60\;\text{ns}$, $W_B = 1$ delay

Then:

$$\text{LB}_A = \frac{40}{2} = 20\;\text{ns}, \qquad \text{LB}_B = \frac{60}{1} = 60\;\text{ns}$$

$$B_{\infty} = \max \{20,\; 60\} = 60\;\text{ns}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Loop B is the **bottleneck** — even though it has fewer delays, the heavier computation in B forces a slower iteration period. The transformation goal would be to *retime* (move delays) so that both loops have similar LB values, ideally increasing the delays in B to relax the iteration bound.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — IIR DFG Showing Loops, Delays, and Critical Path

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeX[Input x n] --> nodeM0[MUL by b0]
    nodeM0 --> nodeA1[ADD 1]
    nodeA1 --> nodeA2[ADD 2]
    nodeA2 --> nodeY[Output y n]
    nodeY --> nodeD1[Delay z-1]
    nodeD1 --> nodeM1[MUL by a1]
    nodeM1 --> nodeA1
    nodeY --> nodeD2[Delay z-1]
    nodeD2 --> nodeM2[MUL by a2]
    nodeM2 --> nodeA1
    style nodeA2 fill:#ffd166,stroke:#333
    style nodeM1 fill:#ef476f,stroke:#333
    style nodeM2 fill:#ef476f,stroke:#333
    style nodeD1 fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333
    style nodeD2 fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333
```

**Legend:**

- 🟡 **Yellow node (ADD 2)** — part of critical path
- 🔴 **Red nodes (MUL by a1, MUL by a2)** — multipliers on feedback path
- 🟢 **Green nodes (z⁻¹)** — unit delays; *break* the combinational path

> The longest **delay-free** chain (critical path) runs: $z^{-1} \rightarrow \text{MUL} \rightarrow \text{ADD} \rightarrow \text{ADD} \rightarrow y[n]$.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture Flow — How LB, IB, and CP Interact

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Application Sampling Rate fs] --> compA[Compute Loop Bound L_inf = 1/fs]
    startB[DFG Topology of Algorithm] --> enum[Enumerate all directed loops]
    enum --> compB[Compute T_l and W_l for each loop]
    compB --> findMax[Take maximum T_l / W_l]
    findMax --> iterB[Iteration Bound B_inf]
    startC[DFG Topology of Algorithm] --> pathA[Find longest combinational path]
    pathA --> compC[Sum execution times along path]
    compC --> critPath[Critical Path T_cp]
    compA --> check{Feasibility Check}
    iterB --> check
    critPath --> check
    check -->|T_cp less or equal B_inf less or equal L_inf| feas[System is REAL TIME FEASIBLE]
    check -->|B_inf greater than L_inf| infeas[System is NOT FEASIBLE in real time]
```

---

## 4.3 Visual Summary of the Three Quantities

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph SLICE_A[Application Domain]
        LBVal[L INFINITY equals Sampling Period Ts]
    end
    subgraph SLICE_B[Algorithm Domain]
        IBVal[B INFINITY equals max Loop bound of all loops]
    end
    subgraph SLICE_C[Hardware Domain]
        CPVal[T cp equals longest combinational path]
    end
    SLICE_A --> SLICE_B
    SLICE_B --> SLICE_C
    style LBVal fill:#118ab2,stroke:#073b4c,color:#fff
    style IBVal fill:#ef476f,stroke:#073b4c,color:#fff
    style CPVal fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#073b4c,color:#fff
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
Define the **iteration bound** of a recursive data-flow graph. Why is it an important parameter in DSP architectural design?

**Model Answer:**

The iteration bound $B_{\infty}$ is the theoretical lower limit on the iteration period of a recursive algorithm. It is computed as the maximum, over all directed loops $l$ in the DFG, of the ratio of the loop's total computation time $T_l$ to its total number of delay elements $W_l$:

$$B_{\infty} = \max_{l} \left\{ \frac{T_l}{W_l} \right\}$$

It is important because it represents the **fastest possible speed** at which the recursive algorithm can be iterated by *any* hardware realization. If $B_{\infty}$ exceeds the loop bound $L_{\infty}$ (sampling period), real-time implementation is impossible regardless of how the hardware is architected. It thus serves as a fundamental feasibility indicator in DSP VLSI design.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
What is the **critical path** of a data-flow graph? How is it related to the minimum clock period of a hardware implementation?

**Model Answer:**

The critical path of a DFG is the longest combinational (delay-free) path through the graph — i.e., the path that passes through the most operations without encountering any $z^{-1}$ delay element.

$$T_{cp} = \max_{p \in \text{acyclic paths}} \sum_{i \in p} t_i$$

It is directly related to the minimum clock period because the clock must be slow enough for the combinational logic on the critical path to settle before the next clock edge triggers a register to latch the result. Hence:

$$T_{clk} \geq T_{cp}$$

To reduce $T_{cp}$, designers employ **pipelining** (inserting delay elements to break long combinational chains), **unfolding**, or **parallel processing**.

---

## 📕 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Note:** KTU mandates an internal choice in Part B. Both alternatives are provided below.

---

### **🔷 Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]

**(a) [7 Marks]** For the IIR difference equation $y[n] = 0.5\, y[n-1] + 0.25\, y[n-2] + x[n]$, construct the DFG and compute the **iteration bound**, assuming multiplication takes 30 ns and addition takes 10 ns.

**(b) [7 Marks]** If the system operates at a sampling rate of 1 MHz, determine whether real-time implementation is feasible.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — DFG Construction**

Three multiplications: $0.5 \cdot y[n-1]$, $0.25 \cdot y[n-2]$, and $1 \cdot x[n]$.
Two additions: one to sum the two feedback terms, one to add $x[n]$.

**Step 2 — Identify the Loops**

- **Loop L₁** (via $0.5$): contains 1 multiply, 1 add, 1 delay
- **Loop L₂** (via $0.25$): contains 1 multiply, 1 add, 1 delay

**Step 3 — Compute Loop Bounds**

For L₁:
$$T_{L_1} = t_m + t_a = 30 + 10 = 40\;\text{ns}, \quad W_{L_1} = 1$$
$$\text{LB}_{L_1} = \frac{40}{1} = 40\;\text{ns}$$

For L₂:
$$T_{L_2} = 40\;\text{ns}, \quad W_{L_2} = 1$$
$$\text{LB}_{L_2} = 40\;\text{ns}$$

**Step 4 — Iteration Bound**

$$B_{\infty} = \max\{40,\; 40\} = 40\;\text{ns}$$

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Compute the Loop Bound**

$$L_{\infty} = T_s = \frac{1}{f_s} = \frac{1}{10^6} = 1\;\mu s = 1000\;\text{ns}$$

**Step 2 — Feasibility Check**

$$B_{\infty} = 40\;\text{ns} \;\ll\; L_{\infty} = 1000\;\text{ns} \quad \checkmark$$

✅ **Real-time implementation is feasible** with a wide margin — we can even slow the clock by a factor of 25 and still meet deadlines.

---

### **🔷 Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of **loop bound** in DSP architecture. How does it differ from the **iteration bound**?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A DFG contains two loops with the following parameters:

| Loop | Computation Time $T_l$ (ns) | Number of Delays $W_l$ |
|------|-----------------------------|-------------------------|
| L₁   | 90                          | 3                       |
| L₂   | 60                          | 2                       |

Compute the iteration bound. If the sampling rate is 20 MHz, comment on real-time feasibility.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

**Loop Bound ($L_{\infty}$):** The maximum time allowed per iteration, set by the *application* (sampling period $T_s = 1/f_s$). It is an **external constraint** of the physical system.

**Iteration Bound ($B_{\infty}$):** The minimum time *physically possible* per iteration, dictated by the *internal structure* of the DFG (operations, loops, delays). It is an **architectural property**.

**Key differences:**

| Aspect | Loop Bound $L_{\infty}$ | Iteration Bound $B_{\infty}$ |
|--------|--------------------------|------------------------------|
| Origin | Application/sampling rate | DFG topology |
| Role | Upper limit on iteration time | Lower limit on iteration time |
| Symbol | $L_{\infty}$ or $T_s$ | $B_{\infty}$ |
| Varies with hardware? | No (fixed by $f_s$) | No (fixed by DFG) |
| Feasibility rule | $B_{\infty} \leq L_{\infty}$ required | — |

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b)

**Loop L₁:**
$$\text{LB}_{L_1} = \frac{T_{L_1}}{W_{L_1}} = \frac{90}{3} = 30\;\text{ns}$$

**Loop L₂:**
$$\text{LB}_{L_2} = \frac{T_{L_2}}{W_{L_2}} = \frac{60}{2} = 30\;\text{ns}$$

**Iteration Bound:**
$$B_{\infty} = \max\{30,\; 30\} = 30\;\text{ns}$$

**Sampling Period / Loop Bound:**
$$L_{\infty} = T_s = \frac{1}{20 \times 10^6} = 50\;\text{ns}$$

**Feasibility Check:**
$$B_{\infty} = 30\;\text{ns} \;\leq\; L_{\infty} = 50\;\text{ns} \quad \checkmark$$

✅ **Feasible.** Margin = 20 ns. The algorithm can be implemented in real time.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing $L_{\infty}$ and $B_{\infty}$:** Many students compute the loop bound of an *individual loop* and call it the iteration bound. Remember: the iteration bound is the **maximum** loop bound over **all** loops.
> 2. **Forgetting the $W_l$ denominator:** A common error is to take the maximum $T_l$ directly. Always divide by the number of delays.
> 3. **Mixing units:** Watch for ns vs µs. $1\;\mu s = 1000\;ns$.
> 4. **Ignoring feasibility:** Even if asked only for the iteration bound, **always comment** on whether $B_{\infty} \leq L_{\infty}$ — examiners reward this.
> 5. **Wrong critical path identification:** The critical path must contain **no** delay element ($z^{-1}$). Stop the path at every delay.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Loop Bound ($L_{\infty}$)** = sampling period $T_s = 1/f_s$. It is the *maximum* time allowed per iteration.
- ✅ **Iteration Bound ($B_{\infty}$)** = $\max_l \left\{ T_l / W_l \right\}$. It is the *minimum* time possible per iteration.
- ✅ **Critical Path ($T_{cp}$)** = longest delay-free computation chain. Sets the *minimum* clock period.
- ✅ **Golden inequality:** $T_{cp} \leq B_{\infty} \leq L_{\infty}$ for real-time feasibility.
- ✅ **Two delay elements in a loop** relax the iteration bound by a factor of 2 (more delays → faster iteration possible).
- ✅ **Retiming, pipelining, unfolding** are the three principal transformations used to reduce $B_{\infty}$ and $T_{cp}$.
- ✅ When $B_{\infty} > L_{\infty}$: **no architecture can make it real-time** — algorithm-level changes (e.g., block processing, lookahead) are required.
- ✅ Always draw the DFG, mark operation times and delays, and **enumerate every loop** systematically.
- ✅ In KTU exams, write the loop enumeration table (Loop | $T_l$ | $W_l$ | LB) for full marks.
- ✅ Real-time **infeasibility** is a common exam conclusion — state it clearly with the inequality.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pipelining, Parallel Processing, Low Power Architectures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST526 - DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pipelining, Parallel Processing, Low Power Architectures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Pipelining in DSP

### Formal Definition
**Pipelining** is an implementation technique in Digital Signal Processing (DSP) where multiple computational operations are overlapped in time by breaking a sequential algorithm into independent sub-operations (stages) that can be executed concurrently in an assembly-line fashion. Each stage processes a different piece of data simultaneously, thereby increasing the **throughput** (samples processed per unit time) without reducing the latency of a single computation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Pipelining is defined as the process of decomposing a recursive or sequential DSP operation into smaller sub-operations (stages) and inserting delay elements (latches/flip-flops) between stages, so that multiple data samples can be processed concurrently, multiplying the effective sampling rate.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a **laundry process** with three stages: washing, drying, and folding.
- **Without Pipelining:** You wash 10 shirts, then dry all 10, then fold all 10. Total time = (Wash + Dry + Fold) × 10.
- **With Pipelining:** You start washing the 2nd shirt while drying the 1st shirt, and fold the 1st shirt while drying the 2nd and washing the 3rd. The first shirt takes the same time, but once the pipeline is full, a new shirt emerges at the folding stage every "one stage time" instead of every "three stage times".

In DSP, the "shirts" are data samples $x[n], x[n+1], x[n+2], \dots$ and the "stages" are arithmetic operations (multiplications, additions).

### Key Metrics (Bolded for KTU Emphasis)
- **Clock Period ($T_{clk}$):** Time taken to execute the slowest pipeline stage.
- **Throughput:** Number of samples processed per clock cycle (ideally 1 sample/cycle in a fully pipelined system).
- **Latency:** Total time delay before the first output sample appears, equals $N \times T_{clk}$ for an N-stage pipeline.
- **Critical Path:** The longest combinational delay in any single stage, which limits the maximum clock frequency.

---

## 1.2 Parallel Processing in DSP

### Formal Definition
**Parallel Processing** is a computational technique in which multiple independent operations (such as multiplications, additions, or entire filter sections) are performed simultaneously using replicated hardware resources (e.g., multiple multipliers, adders, or processing elements). Unlike pipelining which reuses hardware in time, parallel processing **reuses hardware in space**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Parallel processing aims to increase the computational throughput of DSP systems by exploiting concurrency at various levels: bit-level, instruction-level, loop-level (data), and task-level.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of a **supermarket checkout system**:
- A single cashier (one multiplier) handles customers one at a time.
- Opening **4 parallel checkout counters** (4 multipliers) lets 4 customers be served simultaneously.
- The total rate of customers served increases by 4×, even though each customer's individual service time remains the same.

In DSP, this means duplicating arithmetic units to handle multiple data streams or multiple taps of an FIR filter at the same instant.

### Key Architectural Categories
- **Bit-Level Parallelism:** Process multiple bits of the same word in parallel (e.g., 16-bit parallel adder).
- **Word-Level (Data) Parallelism:** Multiple arithmetic units operate on multiple data samples simultaneously.
- **Block Processing:** Process a block of N samples in parallel.

---

## 1.3 Low Power Architectures in DSP

### Formal Definition
**Low Power Architecture** in DSP refers to the design methodology and circuit-level techniques employed to minimize the power consumption of a digital signal processing system without compromising its throughput or functional correctness. It addresses the **dynamic** and **static** components of power dissipation in CMOS-based DSP hardware.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Concept:** Power consumption in a CMOS DSP processor is governed primarily by $P = \alpha C V_{DD}^2 f_{clk}$, where reducing $V_{DD}$ (supply voltage) yields a **quadratic** reduction in dynamic power, making it the most effective lever for low-power design.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Consider a **water tap**:
- The **voltage ($V_{DD}$)** is the water pressure.
- The **clock frequency ($f_{clk}$)** is how fast you open and close the tap.
- The **capacitance ($C$)** is the size of the pipe.
- The **activity factor ($\alpha$)** is how often the tap is actually open.
- To save water (power), you could reduce the pressure, open it less often, or use a smaller pipe. The biggest savings come from **lowering the pressure (voltage)** because it affects flow squared.

### Key Metrics
- **Dynamic Power ($P_{dyn}$):** Power consumed during switching of transistors.
- **Static Power ($P_{stat}$):** Power consumed due to leakage currents, even when the circuit is idle.
- **Energy per Operation ($E_{op}$):** Total energy consumed to complete one DSP operation.
- **Power-Delay Product (PDP):** A figure of merit balancing speed and power.

---

## 1.4 Why These Techniques Are Critical in VLSI DSP

| Technique | Primary Goal | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|
| Pipelining | Increase throughput / reduce critical path | Increases latency and register count |
| Parallel Processing | Increase throughput via hardware replication | Increases area and power |
| Low Power Design | Reduce energy / extend battery life | May reduce performance |

These three pillars are combined in modern DSP chips (e.g., mobile baseband processors, hearing aids, IoT sensors) to achieve **high throughput, low latency, and minimal energy consumption**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pipelining — Theoretical Framework

### 2.1.1 Look-Ahead Transformation
Recursive DSP systems (e.g., IIR filters, recursive DFT, DPCM) have **feedback loops** that limit the maximum clock frequency because each new output $y[n]$ depends on the previous output $y[n-1]$.

**Look-ahead transformation** adds redundant, hardware-cancellable terms to the recursion to break the feedback loop, enabling pipelining.

For a first-order recursion:
$$y[n] = a \cdot y[n-1] + x[n]$$

After 1-step look-ahead substitution, $y[n-1] = a \cdot y[n-2] + x[n-1]$:
$$y[n] = a^2 \cdot y[n-2] + a \cdot x[n-1] + x[n]$$

The feedback is now from $y[n-2]$ instead of $y[n-1]$, allowing one level of pipelining (clock rate can be doubled).

### 2.1.2 Types of Pipelining
1. **Linear Pipelining:** A sequence of stages where each stage passes its result to the next. Used in FIR filters, FFT, convolution.
2. **Non-Linear Pipelining:** Stages form a directed acyclic graph; feedback is allowed within a single stage. Used in recursive filters.
3. **Fine-Grained (Bit-Level) Pipelining:** Pipeline at the level of individual bit operations, used in multipliers.
4. **Coarse-Grained (Word-Level) Pipelining:** Pipeline at the level of complete word operations.

### 2.1.3 Pipeline Interleaving
A single hardware pipeline is **time-shared** (multiplexed) to process $L$ independent data streams in an interleaved manner. Each stream gets one out of every $L$ clock cycles.

---

## 2.2 Parallel Processing — Theoretical Framework

### 2.2.1 Levels of Parallelism in DSP

| Level | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Bit-Level** | Multiple bits processed simultaneously within an ALU | 16-bit parallel adder using 16 full adders |
| **Instruction-Level** | Multiple instructions executed in parallel | VLIW / Superscalar DSP processors |
| **Data / Loop-Level** | Same operation on multiple data items | SIMD, vector processors |
| **Task-Level** | Independent tasks run on separate processors | Multiprocessor SoC |

### 2.2.2 Distributed Arithmetic (DA)
A bit-serial / bit-parallel multiplication technique that pre-computes and stores all possible combinations of a function's partial products in a Look-Up Table (LUT), then uses shift-and-accumulate to retrieve the correct value based on the input bits. DA replaces explicit multipliers with **LUT + adder-accumulator**, saving significant hardware.

For an FIR filter $y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h[k] \cdot x[n-k]$, DA expresses the output as:
$$y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h[k] \cdot x_b[n-k] \cdot 2^{-b}$$

where $x_b[n-k]$ is the $b$-th bit of the input sample. The inner sum over $k$ is a **LUT address** of $2^N$ entries.

### 2.2.3 Systolic Architecture
A systolic array is a network of identical processing elements (PEs) arranged in a regular pattern (linear, triangular, hexagonal) where data flows rhythmically between PEs. Each PE computes a partial result and passes it to its neighbor, with the overall data flow synchronized to a global clock.

**Key Properties:**
- **Regularity:** Identical PEs simplify VLSI layout.
- **Modularity:** Easy to scale by adding more PEs.
- **Local Communication:** Avoids global routing bottlenecks.
- **Balanced I/O:** Data enters and exits at the edges only.

---

## 2.3 Low Power Architectures — Theoretical Framework

### 2.3.1 Sources of Power Dissipation
The total power in a CMOS DSP chip is:
$$P_{total} = P_{dyn} + P_{stat} + P_{short}$$

1. **Dynamic Switching Power ($P_{dyn}$):**
$$P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_{L} \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk}$$
where $\alpha$ is the switching activity factor, $C_L$ is the load capacitance, $V_{DD}$ is the supply voltage, and $f_{clk}$ is the clock frequency.

2. **Static (Leakage) Power ($P_{stat}$):**
$$P_{stat} = I_{leak} \cdot V_{DD}$$
where $I_{leak}$ is the reverse-bias leakage current (subthreshold, gate-oxide tunneling).

3. **Short-Circuit Power ($P_{short}$):** Power dissipated during brief moments when both PMOS and NMOS transistors are on simultaneously during signal transitions.

### 2.3.2 Low-Power Design Techniques
1. **Voltage Scaling (V-scaling):** Reducing $V_{DD}$ quadratically reduces $P_{dyn}$. Practical limit is set by $V_{th} + \Delta V$ (threshold + safety margin).
2. **Frequency Scaling:** Reducing $f_{clk}$ linearly reduces $P_{dyn}$, but also reduces throughput.
3. **Clock Gating:** Disables the clock signal to idle modules, setting $\alpha \approx 0$.
4. **Power Gating:** Uses sleep transistors to cut off $V_{DD}$ from idle blocks, eliminating $P_{stat}$.
5. **Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling (DVFS):** Adjusts $V_{DD}$ and $f_{clk}$ at runtime based on workload.
6. **Operand Isolation / Gating Logic:** Freezes logic inputs to idle combinational blocks to prevent spurious switching.
7. **Architecture-Level:** Use **pipelining** to reduce critical path → enables lower $V_{DD}$; use **parallelism** to trade area for lower $V_{DD}$ at the same throughput (parallelism allows lower frequency at lower voltage).

### 2.3.3 The Parallelism–Power Trade-off (Crucial Concept)
For a fixed throughput requirement, using **$L$ parallel units** allows the clock frequency to be reduced by a factor of $L$. The supply voltage can then be scaled down to $V_{DD}' \approx V_{DD}/L$, and the total power becomes:
$$P_{parallel-low-V} = L \cdot \alpha C (V_{DD}/L)^2 (f/L) = \frac{1}{L^2} P_{single}$$
This yields a **quadratic power reduction per parallel unit**.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Formula / Concept | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| $T_{clk}$ | $\geq T_{critical\ path}$ of slowest stage | seconds |
| Latency | $N \cdot T_{clk}$ for N-stage pipeline | clock cycles |
| Throughput | $1 / T_{clk}$ (ideal) | samples/sec |
| $P_{dyn}$ | $\alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f_{clk}$ | Watts |
| $P_{stat}$ | $I_{leak} V_{DD}$ | Watts |
| $E_{op}$ | $P \cdot T_{op} = C_L V_{DD}^2$ | Joules (independent of $\alpha$, $f_{clk}$) |
| PDP | $P_{dyn} \cdot T_{clk}$ | Joules (figure of merit) |
| Speed-up (Parallel) | $S = L$ (L parallel units) | dimensionless |
| Look-ahead (1st order) | $y[n] = a^2 y[n-2] + a x[n-1] + x[n]$ | breaks feedback loop |
| DA LUT size | $2^N$ words for N-tap filter | bits × words |
| Power saving via $V_{DD}$-scaling | $P \propto V_{DD}^2$ | quadratic |
| Energy per sample (FIR) | $E_s = N \cdot C_{mul} V_{DD}^2$ | depends on N taps |
| Critical path (FIR direct form) | $T_{mul} + \log_2(N) T_{add}$ | for N-tap non-pipelined |
| Pipelined FIR critical path | $T_{mul}$ | independent of N |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy per operation is voltage-dependent but frequency-independent.** This is the deepest insight: lowering the clock frequency alone does **not** save energy, only lowering the voltage does.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Domain | Application | Technique Used |
|---|---|---|
| Mobile Communications (5G) | Baseband FIR filtering in handsets | Pipelined + parallel low-power DSP |
| Hearing Aids | Real-time audio filtering on a tiny battery | Ultra-low-voltage DSP with clock gating |
| MP3 / AAC Decoders | Real-time audio decoding on portable devices | Systolic DCT/IMDCT with DVFS |
| Radar / Sonar | High-throughput beamforming | Massively parallel systolic arrays |
| Medical Imaging (MRI) | FFT-based reconstruction | Pipelined FFT with parallel butterflies |
| IoT Edge Devices | Keyword spotting, sensor fusion | Event-driven DSP with power gating |
