```verilog
module dit_butterfly (
    input  signed [15:0] a_re, a_im,   // Input A
    input  signed [15:0] b_re, b_im,   // Input B
    input  signed [15:0] w_re, w_im,   // Twiddle W_N^k
    output reg signed [31:0] top_re, top_im,   // Top output
    output reg signed [31:0] bot_re, bot_im    // Bottom output
);
    // Internal products (16-bit x 16-bit = 32-bit)
    reg signed [31:0] wb_re, wb_im;
    
    always @(*) begin
        // Complex multiplication: W × B
        wb_re = (w_re * b_re) - (w_im * b_im);
        wb_im = (w_re * b_im) + (w_im * b_re);
        
        // Butterfly outputs
        top_re = a_re + wb_re;
        top_im = a_im + wb_im;
        bot_re = a_re - wb_re;
        bot_im = a_im - wb_im;
    end
endmodule
```

**Marking split (7 marks):** 1 for module/port declaration, 2 for complex multiplication logic, 2 for butterfly addition/subtraction, 1 for signed arithmetic, 1 for `always @(*)` block.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1:** Students often forget the `signed` keyword on the inputs, which causes iVerilog to treat them as unsigned. With unsigned arithmetic, negative twiddle values like $W_8^1$ become huge positive numbers (e.g., 0.707 represented as 16'sb0_10110100...), and the FFT output is completely wrong. **Always use `reg signed` and `input signed`!**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** For the IIR filter $H(z) = \dfrac{0.4 + 0.2 z^{-1} - 0.3 z^{-2}}{1 - 0.6 z^{-1} + 0.2 z^{-2}}$, derive the Direct Form-II (Canonical) structure. Show the intermediate signal $w[n]$ and the final difference equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Implement a 4-tap FIR filter with coefficients $b_0 = 0.25, b_1 = 0.50, b_2 = 0.50, b_3 = 0.25$ in Verilog. Show the complete Verilog module declaration, the shift-register array, and the multiply-accumulate (MAC) always-block. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Direct Form-II Derivation:**

Starting from the difference equation:

$$
y[n] = 0.4 x[n] + 0.2 x[n-1] - 0.3 x[n-2] + 0.6 y[n-1] - 0.2 y[n-2]
$$

Define intermediate signal $w[n]$ such that:

$$
w[n] = x[n] + 0.6 w[n-1] - 0.2 w[n-2]
$$

$$
y[n] = 0.4 w[n] + 0.2 w[n-1] - 0.3 w[n-2]
$$

Number of delay elements required = 2 (just for $w$). Direct Form-I would need 4 delays ($x[n-1], x[n-2], y[n-1], y[n-2]$). Hence Direct Form-II is *canonical*.

**Marking split (7 marks):** 2 for difference equation, 2 for $w[n]$ definition, 2 for $y[n]$ in terms of $w$, 1 for canonical justification.

**(b) Verilog FIR Filter:**

```verilog
`timescale 1ns/1ps
module fir_4tap (
    input  clk, rst,
    input  signed [15:0] x_in,
    output reg signed [31:0] y_out
);
    // Coefficients
    parameter signed [15:0] b0 = 16'sd8192;   // 0.25 in Q1.15
    parameter signed [15:0] b1 = 16'sd16384;  // 0.50 in Q1.15
    parameter signed [15:0] b2 = 16'sd16384;
    parameter signed [15:0] b3 = 16'sd8192;
    
    // Shift register
    reg signed [15:0] shift [0:3];
    integer i;
    
    always @(posedge clk) begin
        if (rst) begin
            y_out = 0;
            for (i = 0; i < 4; i = i + 1) shift[i] = 0;
        end else begin
            // Shift samples
            shift[3] <= shift[2];
            shift[2] <= shift[1];
            shift[1] <= shift[0];
            shift[0] <= x_in;
            
            // MAC operation
            y_out <= (shift[0] * b0) + (shift[1] * b1) +
                     (shift[2] * b2) + (shift[3] * b3);
        end
    end
endmodule
```

**Marking split (7 marks):** 1 for module declaration, 2 for shift register and coefficient declaration, 2 for MAC operation, 1 for reset logic, 1 for `signed` arithmetic.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2:** Common mistakes in FIR Verilog:
> 1. **Forgetting the `parameter` keyword for coefficients** — students hard-code them as decimal `8192` instead of using Q1.15 fixed-point representation. This works numerically but loses the symbolic clarity that examiners look for.
> 2. **Blocking (`=`) vs Non-blocking (`<=`)** — Use `<=` (non-blocking) in `always @(posedge clk)`. Mixing them in the same block causes simulation/synthesis mismatches in iVerilog.
> 3. **Bit-width overflow** — multiplying two 16-bit signed numbers needs 32-bit result. Using 16-bit for the MAC accumulator causes wrap-around errors.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — Hands-on FPGA Realization using iVerilog**

- ✅ **FFT (Radix-2 DIT):** 8-point has **3 stages × 4 butterflies = 12 butterflies**. Inputs in **bit-reversed order**, outputs in **natural order**. Twiddles $W_8^k$ precomputed in ROM.
- ✅ **Butterfly equation:** $X_{top} = A + W_N^k B$, $X_{bottom} = A - W_N^k B$. One complex multiply + two complex adds.
- ✅ **Circular Convolution:** Uses modulo-$N$ wrap-around. Equals linear convolution when $N \geq L_1 + L_2 - 1$. Realized on FPGA as: **FFT → Multiply → IFFT**.
- ✅ **IIR Direct Form-II (Canonical):** Single shared delay line $\to$ minimum delay count = $\max(M, N)$. Difference eq in two parts: feedback (compute $w[n]$), feedforward (compute $y[n]$).
- ✅ **FIR Direct Form:** No feedback. $N$ delays + $N$ multipliers + $N-1$ adders. Linear-phase with symmetric coefficients **halves** multipliers.
- ✅ **iVerilog Workflow:** `iverilog -o sim.vvp design.v tb.v` → `vvp sim.vvp` → `gtkwave dump.vcd`. Always use `signed` for negative twiddles and filter coefficients.
- ✅ **Verilog Best Practice:** Use `reg signed [15:0]` for fixed-point, `reg signed [31:0]` for accumulators. Non-blocking (`<=`) in `posedge clk` blocks. `always @(*)` for combinational butterfly.
- ✅ **Hardware Realization vs Software:** FPGA gives **parallel execution**—all 12 FFT butterflies of stage 1 run in the *same clock cycle* (pipelined), unlike CPU which executes sequentially.
- ✅ **Common KTU Mistakes:** Forgetting `signed` keyword; using blocking assignment in clocked blocks; bit-width overflow in MAC; not matching delay-line length to filter order.
- ✅ **Verification:** Always compare iVerilog output against MATLAB/Python theoretical result. KTU lab exam typically asks for a screenshot of GTKWave waveform matching expected values.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hands-on: Realization of Different DSP Algorithms Including Basic Multiply-Accumulation and Shifting Operations on a Fixed Point Processor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST526 - DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hands-on: Realization of Different DSP Algorithms Including Basic Multiply-Accumulation and Shifting Operations on a Fixed Point Processor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Fixed-Point Processor** is a microprocessor optimized for Digital Signal Processing operations where numerical values are represented in a fixed number of bits using integer arithmetic with an *implicitly defined binary point*. The realization of DSP algorithms (such as FIR/IIR filtering, convolution, and correlation) on such a processor fundamentally relies on two primitive operations:

1. **Multiply-Accumulation (MAC):** A hardware-supported, single-cycle operation that computes the product of two operands and adds the result to a wide accumulator register. Mathematically:
$$y = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} a_k \cdot b_k$$
2. **Shifting Operations:** Bit-wise manipulations (Arithmetic Shift Left/Right, Logical Shift Left/Right) used for scaling, normalization, and power-of-two multiplication/division.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The TMS320C67xx family (or equivalent 16-bit fixed-point DSPs like TMS320C54x/C55x) is the canonical reference platform in KTU lab curricula. It features a **40-bit accumulator**, a **17×17-bit hardware multiplier**, and a **0–16 bit left/right barrel shifter**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of a **Fixed-Point Processor** as a **high-precision industrial weighing scale** combined with a **measuring cup**:

- The **measuring cup** is your *Q-format representation* (e.g., Q15). You decide in advance that every ingredient will be measured in units of 1/32768 of a cup. You never change the cup's size during the recipe.
- The **MAC unit** is the **automatic mixer-bowl with a stirring motor**. You pour in ingredients (multiplications) and the bowl keeps adding them to a growing batter (accumulation) in one fast, continuous motion.
- The **shifter** is the **adjustable knob** on the cup that lets you double or halve the resolution instantly when the batter overflows or becomes too coarse.

Just as a baker must keep the batter from spilling (overflow) while maintaining fine flavor (resolution), the DSP programmer must balance **dynamic range** vs. **precision** by choosing the right Q-format and applying strategic shifts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Concept:** Fixed-point arithmetic trades *absolute dynamic range* (floating-point advantage) for *speed, cost, and power efficiency* — critical for real-time embedded DSP.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

| Metric | Value/Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Word Length (N) | **16 bits** | Standard for TMS320C67xx data memory |
| Sign Bit | **1 bit (MSB)** | Two's complement representation |
| Fractional Bits (Q-format) | **Q15 / Q31** | KTU lab default formats |
| Accumulator Width | **40 bits** | 8 guard bits to prevent intermediate overflow |
| MAC Latency | **1 cycle** | Single-cycle throughput |
| Barrel Shifter Range | **-16 to +15** | Hardware-implemented bit shift |
| Dynamic Range (Q15) | **≈ 90.3 dB** | $20 \log_{10}(2^{15})$ |
| Smallest Q15 Step | **2⁻¹⁵ ≈ 3.05 × 10⁻⁵** | Quantization resolution |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Q-Format Number System (Operational Foundation)

In a **Qm.n** format (Total bits = m + n + 1, including 1 sign bit), a stored integer $X_{int}$ represents the real value:

$$X_{real} = \frac{X_{int}}{2^n}$$

### Why Q-Format?
- **Hardware simplicity:** The CPU only performs integer arithmetic; the binary point is *conceptual*.
- **Deterministic behavior:** No rounding-mode ambiguity like IEEE-754 floats.
- **Speed:** Integer MAC is 5–10× faster than floating-point equivalent on the same silicon.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic Flow of a MAC Operation

The MAC is the *heartbeat* of every DSP algorithm. Consider a single tap of an FIR filter:

- **Step 1 — Fetch:** Load coefficient $h[k]$ and data sample $x[n-k]$ from data memory in **one cycle**.
- **Step 2 — Multiply:** The 17×17 hardware multiplier produces a 32-bit product in **one cycle**.
- **Step 3 — Align:** The 32-bit product is sign-extended and shifted left by 1 bit (to remove the redundant sign bit formed by the multiplication of two Q15 numbers).
- **Step 4 — Accumulate:** The aligned 32-bit (or 40-bit) result is added to the 40-bit accumulator.
- **Step 5 — Saturate/Store:** On overflow, the *SAT* bit latches; otherwise, the accumulator is stored/rounded.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The 40-bit accumulator provides **8 guard bits** above the 32-bit product. This allows 256 successive MACs without intermediate overflow, *provided the final sum fits in 32 bits.*

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield for Exams)

| Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Q-format value | $x_{real} = \dfrac{x_{int}}{2^n}$ | n = fractional bits |
| Resolution (Step Size) | $\Delta = 2^{-n}$ | Smallest representable increment |
| Dynamic Range (DR) | $DR = 6.02 \cdot n \text{ dB}$ | Approx. 90.3 dB for Q15 |
| MAC Equation (FIR) | $y[n] = \sum\limits_{k=0}^{N-1} h[k] \cdot x[n-k]$ | Canonical DSP form |
| Overflow Condition | $\vert y[n] \vert \geq 1$ (for Q15) | Triggers saturation logic |
| Arithmetic Shift Right | $x_{ASR} = \dfrac{x_{int}}{2^k}$ | Sign-preserving, fills MSB with sign bit |
| Arithmetic Shift Left | $x_{ASL} = x_{int} \cdot 2^k$ | Risk of overflow; used for power-of-2 gain |
| Scaling for Unity DC Gain | $h_{scaled}[k] = h[k] \cdot S$ | $S = \sum \vert h[k] \vert$ (L1 norm) |
| SNR (Quantization) | $SNR \approx 6.02N + 1.76 \text{ dB}$ | N = word length in bits |
| Round-off Error (Uniform) | $e_{rms} = \dfrac{\Delta}{\sqrt{12}} = \dfrac{2^{-n}}{\sqrt{12}}$ | RMS quantization noise |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** When asked to "realize" an algorithm, the examiner expects: (1) the difference equation, (2) the Q-format conversion, (3) the MAC-by-MAC trace table, and (4) the scaling/shifting strategy to prevent overflow.

## 2.4 Shifting Operations — The Programmer's Volume Knob

Shifts are used in DSP for **three distinct purposes**:

1. **Q-format conversion:** Converting a Q15 sample to Q14 by shifting right 1 bit.
2. **Gain adjustment:** Pre-scaling filter coefficients to prevent overflow (e.g., ASL of coefficients by 2).
3. **Normalization:** Counting leading sign bits to find the optimal scaling factor dynamically.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> On the TMS320C67xx, the **SFTA** (Shift Accumulator) instruction performs this in **one cycle** with a range of -16 to +16 — far more flexible than general-purpose CPUs.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hearing Aids & Cochlear Implants:** Ultra-low-power fixed-point DSPs process audio in real-time.
- **Telecom Baseband Processing:** LTE/5G PHY layers run on fixed-point DSP cores.
- **Motor Control (Field-Oriented Control):** TI C2000 series use fixed-point MAC for rotor flux estimation.
- **Biomedical ECG/EEG Filtering:** Notch filters and bandpass filters for noise removal.
- **Audio Codecs (MP3, AAC):** Inner loops of IMDCT/filterbanks on fixed-point cores.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Q15 Multiplication Yields a Q30 Product

**Problem:** Two Q15 numbers $A$ and $B$ (each in range $[-1, +1)$) are multiplied. Determine the Q-format of the result and the optimal alignment strategy.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{int}, B_{int} &\in \left[-2^{15},\ 2^{15}-1\right] \\
A_{real} &= \frac{A_{int}}{2^{15}}, \quad B_{real} = \frac{B_{int}}{2^{15}} \\
P_{real} &= A_{real} \cdot B_{real} = \frac{A_{int}}{2^{15}} \cdot \frac{B_{int}}{2^{15}} = \frac{A_{int} \cdot B_{int}}{2^{30}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $A_{int} \cdot B_{int}$ is a 32-bit signed integer (result of 17×17 multiplier → 32-bit product), $P_{real}$ is effectively a **Q30** number (30 fractional bits, 1 sign bit = 31 bits, fits in 32).

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{max} &= (1 - 2^{-15}) \cdot (1 - 2^{-15}) \approx 0.99997 < 1 \\
\therefore\ P_{real} &\in [-1, +1) \quad \text{(still within Q15 range!)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The result is in **Q30** but its value range is still $[-1, 1)$. To store back into a 16-bit Q15 register, we **shift right by 15** and use the **upper 16 bits** of the 32-bit product.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{Q15\_int} &= \left( A_{int} \cdot B_{int} \right) \gg 15 \\
\text{(with optional rounding: } &+ 0x4000 \text{ before the shift)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is precisely why the **40-bit accumulator exists** — it preserves the **Q30** product *throughout* the FIR summation loop, only rounding once at the *end*. Intermediate rounding destroys filter accuracy and is a classic KTU exam pitfall.

## 3.2 Worked Example: FIR Filter Realization on Q15 Processor

**Problem:** Realize a 3-tap FIR filter $y[n] = 0.25 \cdot x[n] + 0.5 \cdot x[n-1] + 0.25 \cdot x[n-2]$ for input $x = [0.5, -0.25, 0.75, -0.5, 0.0]$ on a Q15 fixed-point processor. Show the MAC trace.

### Step 1 — Convert coefficients to Q15 integers:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h[0] = 0.25 \times 2^{15} &= 8192 \quad (\texttt{0x2000}) \\
h[1] = 0.50 \times 2^{15} &= 16384 \quad (\texttt{0x4000}) \\
h[2] = 0.25 \times 2^{15} &= 8192 \quad (\texttt{0x2000}) \\
\text{Sum of } \vert h \vert &= 0.25 + 0.5 + 0.25 = 1.0 \le 1 \quad \checkmark \text{ (no overflow risk)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Convert input samples to Q15:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x[0] = 0.50 \times 2^{15} &= 16384 \quad (\texttt{0x4000}) \\
x[1] = -0.25 \times 2^{15} &= -8192 \quad (\texttt{0xE000}) \\
x[2] = 0.75 \times 2^{15} &= 24576 \quad (\texttt{0x6000}) \\
x[3] = -0.50 \times 2^{15} &= -16384 \quad (\texttt{0xC000}) \\
x[4] = 0.00 \times 2^{15} &= 0 \quad (\texttt{0x0000})
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — MAC Trace Table (Single-Cycle Operations):

For $n=2$, compute $y[2] = h[0]x[2] + h[1]x[1] + h[2]x[0]$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cycle 1: } &A = 24576 \times 8192 = 201326592 \\
&\text{(32-bit product, sign-extended to 40-bit accumulator)} \\
\text{Cycle 2: } &A = 201326592 + (16384 \times (-8192)) = 201326592 - 134217728 = 67108864 \\
\text{Cycle 3: } &A = 67108864 + (8192 \times 16384) = 67108864 + 134217728 = 201326592 \\
\text{Cycle 4: } &A_{Q15} = 201326592 \gg 15 = 6144
\end{aligned}
$$

Converting back to real: $y[2] = 6144 / 32768 = 0.1875$.

**Floating-point verification:** $0.25(0.75) + 0.5(-0.25) + 0.25(0.5) = 0.1875 - 0.125 + 0.125 = 0.1875$ ✓

### Step 4 — Final Output (after all samples processed):

$$
y[n] = \{0.125,\ 0.0625,\ 0.1875,\ 0.0625,\ -0.1875\}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Valuation Insight:** Examiners award full marks only if you show: (1) Q15 conversion *with binary/hex*, (2) cycle-by-cycle MAC trace, and (3) final real-value output with unit verification.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Shift Strategy

| Situation | Symptom | Correct Shift | Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sum exceeds accumulator range | `SAT` bit set, distorted output | Pre-divide input by 2 (ASR x[n] by 1) | Prevents overflow *before* MAC |
| Output too small, poor SNR | Output barely changes | Pre-multiply coefficients (ASL h[k] by 1) | Boosts signal gain by 6 dB |
| Coefficient must stay in Q15 | $\vert h[k] \vert \ge 1$ | Normalize: $h' = h / \sum \vert h \vert$, then scale output | Maintains Q15 compliance |
| Need 32-bit precision result | Single 16-bit product too coarse | Accumulate in 40-bit, round at end | Preserves filter accuracy |
| Time-varying gain required | AGC, noise gate | Dynamic right-shift by leading-zero count | Adaptive normalization |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 TMS320C67xx CPU Data Path (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Program Memory Bus] --> B[Instruction Fetch & Decode Unit]
    B --> C[Control Unit]
    C --> D1[16-bit T Register - Multiplier Operand 1]
    C --> D2[16-bit X2 Register - Multiplier Operand 2]
    D1 --> E[17x17 Hardware Multiplier]
    D2 --> E
    E --> F[32-bit P Product Register]
    F --> G[40-bit Accumulator AC0]
    G --> H[40-bit Barrel Shifter -16 to +16]
    H --> I[Memory Store Bus]
    G --> J[Saturate Logic]
    J --> I
    K[Data Memory Bus] --> D1
    K --> D2
    L[ALU 40-bit] --> G
    M[Status Registers ST0 ST1] --> C
```

## 4.2 MAC Pipeline Flow (Single-Cycle Throughput)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Pre-fetch: Load h k and x n-k] --> P2[Multiply Stage: 17x17 multiplier computes product]
    P2 --> P3[Align Stage: Sign-extend to 40 bits and left-shift by 1]
    P3 --> P4[Add Stage: Add to 40-bit accumulator AC0]
    P4 --> P5{Overflow Check}
    P5 -- Yes --> P6[Saturate to 0x7FFF or 0x8000]
    P5 -- No --> P7[Store or Continue Loop]
    P6 --> P7
    P7 --> P1
```

## 4.3 FIR Filter Realization Flow (Direct Form)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    XN[x n Input Q15] --> M0[Multiplier 0]
    H0[h 0 Q15 Coefficient] --> M0
    XN1[x n-1 Delay Line 1] --> M1[Multiplier 1]
    H1[h 1] --> M1
    XN2[x n-2 Delay Line 2] --> M2[Multiplier 2]
    H2[h 2] --> M2
    M0 --> SUM((+ Summation 40-bit Accumulator))
    M1 --> SUM
    M2 --> SUM
    SUM --> SHIFT[Right-Shift by 15 with Rounding]
    SHIFT --> SAT{Saturation Check}
    SAT -- Pass --> YN[y n Q15 Output]
    SAT -- Overflow --> CLAMP[Saturated Output 0x7FFF / 0x8000]
    YN --> DELAY[Update Delay Line]
    DELAY --> XN1
```

## 4.4 IIR Filter Direct Form-I Realization on Fixed-Point

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    XN[x n] --> MFB0[Mul b0]
    XN --> D1[z-1]
    D1 --> MFB1[Mul b1]
    D1 --> D2[z-1]
    D2 --> MFB2[Mul b2]
    D2 --> D3[z-1]
    D3 --> MFB3[Mul b3]
    MFB0 --> SUM1((Sum 1))
    MFB1 --> SUM1
    MFB2 --> SUM1
    MFB3 --> SUM1
    SUM1 --> MID[m w intermediate]
    MID --> YN[y n output]
    YN --> FFA1[Mul -a1]
    YN --> D4[z-1]
    D4 --> FFA2[Mul -a2]
    D4 --> D5[z-1]
    D5 --> FFA3[Mul -a3]
    FFA1 --> SUM2((Sum 2))
    FFA2 --> SUM2
    FFA3 --> SUM2
    SUM2 --> ADD[Add to Input]
    ADD --> XN
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Architecture Insight:** IIR Direct Form-I uses **two separate delay lines** (feedforward + feedback). For a hardware-efficient fixed-point implementation, **Direct Form-II** is preferred because it uses a single delay line of order $N$ (smaller memory footprint, fewer shift operations, better numerical stability).

## 4.5 Shifting Operation Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Need to scale a Q15 value?] --> Q1{Goal?}
    Q1 -- Divide by power of 2 --> Q2[Arithmetic Shift Right - ASR]
    Q1 -- Multiply by power of 2 --> Q3{Within Q15 range?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q4[Arithmetic Shift Left - ASL]
    Q3 -- No --> Q5[Use right shift or saturate]
    Q1 -- Convert Q-format --> Q6[Shift with rounding: add 0x4000 then ASR 15]
    Q1 -- Normalize to highest magnitude --> Q7[Count leading sign bits - NORM instruction]
    Q1 -- Bit-mask selection --> Q8[Logical Shift + AND operation]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)**

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define the **Multiply-Accumulate (MAC)** operation. Why is it considered the single most important instruction in a fixed-point DSP processor?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) The MAC operation computes the product of two operands and adds the result to an accumulator in **a single instruction cycle**: $A \leftarrow A + (X \times Y)$.
- (1 mark) It is the building block of convolution, correlation, FIR filtering, and IIR filtering — all of which are dominated by inner-product computations.
- (1 mark) On the TMS320C67xx, the 17×17 hardware multiplier, 40-bit accumulator, and dedicated MAC unit execute this in **1 cycle with zero overhead**, enabling real-time throughput for high-order filters.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Distinguish between **Arithmetic Shift Right (ASR)** and **Logical Shift Right (LSR)** on a Q15 fixed-point processor. Give one application of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) **ASR** propagates the sign bit into the vacated MSB positions, preserving the sign of negative two's-complement numbers. It is used for **scaling down** (divide by $2^k$) without sign corruption.
- (1 mark) **LSR** always fills vacated MSB positions with zeros, treating the operand as an unsigned bit-pattern. It is used for **bit-mask generation**, **Q-format bit-stripping**, and **unsigned arithmetic**.
- (1 mark) **Application example:** ASR by 1 is used to pre-scale an input sample by 0.5 to prevent accumulator overflow; LSR by 8 isolates the high byte of a 16-bit word for byte-wise transmission.

---

## **PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)**

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts: (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Internal choice means you must answer *either* Question A *or* Question B in full.

---

### **QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **internal architecture of the MAC unit** in a fixed-point DSP processor (TMS320C67xx). List the key registers involved. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Block Diagram Description (3 marks):**
The MAC unit consists of four primary blocks connected via dedicated internal buses:
   - **Multiplier (MUL):** 17 × 17-bit two's-complement hardware multiplier, producing a 32-bit result in **one cycle**.
   - **Product Register (P):** 32-bit register that latches the multiplier output. Bit 31 holds the redundant sign; bits 30–0 hold the Q30 product.
   - **Central Arithmetic Logic Unit (CALU):** Contains the 40-bit arithmetic logic unit that performs the accumulation: $A \leftarrow A + (P \ll 1)$.
   - **Accumulator (AC0/AC1/AC2):** 40-bit register (32-bit main + 8 guard bits) that holds the running sum.

2. **Key Registers (2 marks):**
   - `T` register (16-bit): Holds the multiplicand loaded via `LT` instruction.
   - `X2` register: Holds the second operand, often from data memory.
   - `P` register (32-bit): Holds the product.
   - `AC0` (40-bit): Primary accumulator for MAC results.
   - `ST0`, `ST1` status registers: Hold overflow (`OV`), sign-extension (`SXM`), saturation (`SAT`) flags.

3. **Operation Sequence (2 marks):**
The MAC is pipelined as: *Fetch T → Fetch X2 → Multiply (P) → Add to AC0*. With the 40-bit accumulator, intermediate sums do not overflow for filter orders up to 256, provided the final output is within Q15 range.

---

**(b)** A 4-tap FIR filter has coefficients $h = \{0.125,\ 0.375,\ 0.375,\ 0.125\}$ and input $x = \{1.0,\ 0.5,\ -0.25,\ 0.0,\ 0.0,\ \ldots\}$. Realize this filter on a **Q15 fixed-point processor**. Show the Q15 integer conversion, the MAC trace, and verify one output sample. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Q15 Integer Conversion of Coefficients (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h[0] = 0.125 \times 2^{15} &= 4096 \quad (\texttt{0x1000}) \\
h[1] = 0.375 \times 2^{15} &= 12288 \quad (\texttt{0x3000}) \\
h[2] = 0.375 \times 2^{15} &= 12288 \quad (\texttt{0x3000}) \\
h[3] = 0.125 \times 2^{15} &= 4096 \quad (\texttt{0x1000})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Overflow check:** $\sum \vert h \vert = 0.125 + 0.375 + 0.375 + 0.125 = 1.0 \le 1$ ✓ (No pre-scaling required.)

2. **Q15 Integer Conversion of Input (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x[0] = 1.0 \times 2^{15} &= 32767 \quad (\texttt{0x7FFF}) \quad \text{(max positive Q15)} \\
x[1] = 0.5 \times 2^{15} &= 16384 \quad (\texttt{0x4000}) \\
x[2] = -0.25 \times 2^{15} &= -8192 \quad (\texttt{0xE000}) \\
x[3] &= 0 \quad (\texttt{0x0000})
\end{aligned}
$$

3. **MAC Trace for $y[3]$ (3 marks):**

$$
y[3] = h[0]x[3] + h[1]x[2] + h[2]x[1] + h[3]x[0]
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cycle 1: } &P_1 = 4096 \times 0 = 0, \quad A = 0 \\
\text{Cycle 2: } &P_2 = 12288 \times (-8192) = -100663296, \quad A = -100663296 \\
\text{Cycle 3: } &P_3 = 12288 \times 16384 = 201326592, \quad A = 100663296 \\
\text{Cycle 4: } &P_4 = 4096 \times 32767 = 134215680, \quad A = 234878976 \\
\text{Cycle 5: } &A_{Q15} = 234878976 \gg 15 = 7168
\end{aligned}
$$

4. **Output Verification (1 mark):**
Real value $y[3] = 7168 / 32768 = 0.21875$.
Floating-point check: $0.125(0) + 0.375(-0.25) + 0.375(0.5) + 0.125(1.0) = 0 - 0.09375 + 0.1875 + 0.125 = 0.21875$ ✓ **Perfect match, zero quantization error in this case.**

---

### **QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the **Q15 and Q31 formats** in fixed-point DSP. A signal sample has the 16-bit two's complement value `\texttt{0x4000}`. Find its real (decimal) value in Q15 format. What is the smallest positive value it can represent? **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Q15 Format Explanation (2 marks):**
Q15 uses 16 bits — 1 sign bit and 15 fractional bits. Range: $[-1, +1 - 2^{-15}]$. It is the most common format for audio/signals normalized to $[-1, 1]$. Conversion: $X_{real} = X_{int} / 2^{15}$.

2. **Q31 Format Explanation (2 marks):**
Q31 uses 32 bits — 1 sign bit and 31 fractional bits. Range: $[-1, +1 - 2^{-31}]$. Used for intermediate MAC results to preserve precision across long filter chains. Conversion: $X_{real} = X_{int} / 2^{31}$.

3. **Decoding `\texttt{0x4000}` in Q15 (2 marks):**
`0x4000` = `0100 0000 0000 0000` in binary = $2^{14} = 16384$ in decimal.
Real value $= 16384 / 32768 = 0.5$.

4. **Smallest Positive Q15 Value (1 mark):**
`0x0001` = $1 / 2^{15} = 2^{-15} \approx 3.05 \times 10^{-5}$ (≈ 0.0000305).

---

**(b)** Describe the role of **barrel shifting and saturation logic** in a fixed-point DSP. Illustrate with a code-level (instruction-level) example showing overflow detection and recovery. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Role of Barrel Shifter (3 marks):**
The barrel shifter performs arbitrary bit shifts in a *single cycle*, unlike software loop-based shifting on a general CPU. It serves to:
   - **Normalize** accumulator results before storing (shift right to fit Q15).
   - **Scale** coefficients or input samples by powers of 2 to prevent overflow.
   - **Align** operands for addition/subtraction.
On TMS320C67xx, the `SFTA` (Shift Accumulator by Data Value) instruction shifts AC0 by $-16$ to $+16$ bits in **one cycle**.

2. **Role of Saturation Logic (2 marks):**
When a MAC overflows the 16-bit Q15 range (i.e., $|x| \ge 1$), the default behavior is to *wrap around* (modular arithmetic), causing severe distortion (e.g., $0.9 + 0.3 = -0.78$ instead of $1.0$). **Saturation logic** detects this and **clamps** the output to the maximum representable value (`+0x7FFF` for positive overflow, `-0x8000` for negative overflow), preventing wrap-around distortion — analogous to a limiter in audio.

3. **Instruction-Level Example (2 marks):**
```
       LT     h[k]            ; Load T with coefficient
       MPY    x[n-k]          ; P = T * x[n-k]
       APAC                   ; AC0 = AC0 + P (MAC)
       SFL    AC0, -15        ; Shift right by 15 to convert Q30 → Q15
       SAT    AC0             ; If overflow, clamp to ±0x7FFF
       SACH   output, 1       ; Store upper 16 bits of AC0
       ; Repeat for k = 0, 1, ..., N-1
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the left-shift by 1 after multiplication** — TMS320 multipliers produce 32-bit products with a redundant sign bit; the MAC implicitly shifts left by 1 to align to the 40-bit accumulator. Students who skip this in manual traces get wrong accumulator values.
> 2. **Rounding incorrectly** — When converting Q30 → Q15, you must add `0x4000` (i.e., $2^{14}$) *before* the right-shift by 15 to implement *round-to-nearest*. A bare shift is *truncation*, which introduces a DC bias.
> 3. **Ignoring saturation mode bit (SXM/SAT)** — Forgetting to enable saturation in `ST1` causes wrap-around distortion. Examiners explicitly test this in Part B.
> 4. **Using Q15 coefficient $\ge 1.0$** — A coefficient of $1.0$ in Q15 is `0x7FFF` (not `0x8000`). This off-by-one mistake is a common error in lab viva.
> 5. **Pre-scaling inputs instead of outputs** — If you shift the *input* right to prevent overflow, you lose 6 dB of SNR permanently. The correct practice is to scale *coefficients* (a one-time operation) and let the input remain at full resolution.

---

## **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)**

- **Fixed-point = integer arithmetic with implicit binary point.** No floating-point hardware; faster, cheaper, lower power.
- **Q15 is the default audio format:** 1 sign + 15 fractional bits → range $[-1, +1)$, resolution $2^{-15}$, dynamic range ≈ 90.3 dB.
- **Q31 is for intermediate MAC results:** 1 sign + 31 fractional bits; preserves precision across long filter chains.
- **The 40-bit accumulator has 8 guard bits** to absorb intermediate overflow during MAC summation.
- **MAC = single-cycle multiply + add**; it is the *heartbeat* of FIR/IIR/convolution/correlation.
- **Q15 × Q15 = Q30** (range still $[-1, 1)$). To store back in Q15, shift right by 15 with rounding.
- **Arithmetic Shift Right (ASR)** is sign-preserving (for signed scaling); **Logical Shift Right (LSR)** is zero-filling (for bit-mask operations).
- **Arithmetic Shift Left (ASL)** multiplies by $2^k$ but risks overflow; always pre-check $|x| \cdot 2^k < 1$.
- **Saturation logic** prevents wrap-around distortion; clamp overflow to `0x7FFF` (max) or `0x8000` (min).
- **Pre-scaling rule:** $\sum |h[k]| \le 1$ ensures no overflow for arbitrary input in $[-1, 1)$. If not, scale coefficients down by the L1 norm.
- **Round-to-nearest:** add `0x4000` before a right-shift by 15 in Q15; bare shift = truncation (DC bias).
- **TMS320C67xx key features:** 17×17 multiplier, 40-bit accumulator, barrel shifter (-16 to +16), single-cycle MAC, two address generators with circular buffering for delay-line management.
- **FIR realization = delay line + MAC loop.** Direct Form is canonical; transposed form is also used for hardware pipelining.
- **IIR realization:** Direct Form-I uses 2N delay elements; Direct Form-II uses N delay elements (preferred for fixed-point due to lower memory and better numerical behavior).
- **Overflow check before every MAC** is mandatory in safety-critical code; production code uses guard bits *and* saturation.
- **SNR ≈ 6.02N + 1.76 dB** — every additional bit adds ~6 dB of signal-to-noise ratio.
- **Hands-on lab tip:** When implementing on the DSK board, use the `DSK6713_AIC23` codec and configure the McBSP for 16-bit two's-complement I/O; always initialize `ST1` with `SXM = 1` and `SAT = 1` to enable saturation mode by default.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hands-on: Analysis of the Effect of Finite Wordlength by Implementing the FFT Algorithm and FIR Filters Using Fixed Point Coefficient Representation in Formats like Q7, Q15
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST526 - DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hands-on: Analysis of the Effect of Finite Wordlength by Implementing the FFT Algorithm and FIR Filters Using Fixed Point Coefficient Representation in Formats like Q7, Q15

# 🔬 Finite Word Length Analysis: FFT & FIR Filters in Fixed-Point Q-Format

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Finite Word Length (FWL) Effects** in Digital Signal Processing refer to the deviations introduced in the output of a DSP algorithm (such as FFT or FIR filtering) when the mathematical idealization of infinite-precision arithmetic is replaced by practical hardware constraints of finite-bit fixed-point or floating-point registers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Fixed-Point Representation (Q-Format):** A binary number representation where the decimal point (radix point) is fixed at a predetermined position. The Q*m.n* format denotes **m** integer bits (including sign) and **n** fractional bits, giving a total word length of **B = m + n + 1** bits for signed two's-complement format.

The two formats emphasized in the KTU 2024 hands-on module are:
- **Q7 format** — Total word length **8 bits** (1 sign + 0 integer + 7 fractional)
- **Q15 format** — Total word length **16 bits** (1 sign + 0 integer + 15 fractional)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **staircase** that tries to imitate a smooth ramp. The staircase has only a finite number of steps, so it can never match the ramp perfectly — the difference is a "staircase error." 

Similarly, a real DSP chip cannot store numbers with infinite precision. The continuous range of values (the "ramp") is approximated by discrete quantum levels (the "stairs"). The **Q-format** defines how closely the stairs can hug the ramp:

| Format | Step Height (Resolution) | Stair Tread Width (Range) |
|--------|--------------------------|---------------------------|
| Q7     | 2⁻⁷ ≈ 0.00781            | [−1, 0.9921875]           |
| Q15    | 2⁻¹⁵ ≈ 3.05 × 10⁻⁵       | [−1, 0.99996948]          |

A **Q7** is a "coarse staircase" (8 steps per unit), while a **Q15** is a "fine staircase" (32,768 steps per unit). The hands-on experiment quantifies how this coarseness distorts FFT spectra and FIR filter responses.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU lab module specifically studies **three finite word-length effects**:
> 1. **Coefficient quantization error** — filter coefficients rounded to Q-format
> 2. **Arithmetic round-off error** — products truncated after multiplication
> 3. **Overflow / dynamic range limitations** — saturating adders in FFT butterflies

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Quantization step (resolution):** **Δ = 2⁻ⁿ** for a Q*m.n* format
- **Maximum representable value:** **x_max = (2^m − 2⁻ⁿ)**
- **Minimum representable value:** **x_min = −2^m**
- **Two's complement range:** **[−2^m, 2^m − 2⁻ⁿ]**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Q-Format Number System

A real number **x** is mapped to a Q*m.n* fixed-point integer **X_Q** by:

$$X_Q = \text{round}(x \cdot 2^n)$$

The reconstructed value is:

$$x \approx X_Q \cdot 2^{-n} = Q \to \text{Float}(X_Q)$$

### 2.2 Three Classes of Quantization Error

The experiment separates three distinct error sources:

**(a) Input Quantization Error (A/D Conversion Noise)**
- The analog input **x(t)** is sampled and rounded to the nearest Q-format level.
- Error bound: **−Δ/2 < e_in < +Δ/2** (uniformly distributed)
- Mean-square error (variance): **σ²_e = Δ² / 12**

**(b) Coefficient Quantization Error**
- Designed filter coefficients **h[k]** (infinite precision) are rounded to Q-format.
- For an FIR filter, the realized transfer function is:

$$H_Q(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h_Q[k] \, z^{-k} = H(z) + E_H(z)$$

- The deviation **E_H(z)** causes passband ripple, stopband degradation, and possible instability in IIR designs.

**(c) Product (Arithmetic) Round-off Error**
- Every multiplication in the FFT butterfly or FIR tap produces a **2B-bit** product, which must be re-quantized back to **B bits**.
- The accumulated round-off noise grows with the number of operations.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Q*m.n* resolution | $\Delta = 2^{-n}$ | Per LSB |
| 2 | Q*m.n* dynamic range | $\vert x \vert \le 2^m - 2^{-n}$ | Magnitude |
| 3 | Signed Q-format value | $x = -b_{m+n} \cdot 2^{m} + \sum_{i=1}^{m+n} b_{m+n-i} \cdot 2^{i-1-n}$ | Two's complement |
| 4 | Float → Q*m.n* | $X_Q = \text{round}(x \cdot 2^n)$ | Integer result |
| 5 | Q*m.n* → Float | $x = X_Q \cdot 2^{-n}$ | Real number |
| 6 | Q-format addition | Align radix points; saturate on overflow | B-bit result |
| 7 | Q-format multiplication | Q*m₁.n₁* × Q*m₂.n₂* = Q*(m₁+m₂).(n₁+n₂)* | Truncate/round to B bits |
| 8 | Quantization noise power | $\sigma_e^{2} = \dfrac{\Delta^{2}}{12}$ | Variance (W) |
| 9 | SQNR (B-bit uniform) | $\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02 \, B + 1.76$ | dB |
| 10 | Output noise (FIR direct form) | $\sigma_{y}^{2} = N \cdot \sigma_{e}^{2} \cdot \sigma_{x}^{2}$ | W (N taps) |
| 11 | FFT output noise (radix-2) | $\sigma_{Y}^{2} = \sigma_{X}^{2} \cdot \dfrac{2^{m}-1}{3} \cdot \Delta^{2}$ | After m stages |
| 12 | Max SNR degradation (FWL) | $\Delta H_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{2^{-n}}{\vert h_{min}\vert}\right)$ | Coefficient Q-effect |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Aid:** Each extra bit of word length improves SQNR by approximately **6 dB**. Doubling the word length (Q7 → Q15) gains **8 bits ≈ 48 dB** of SQNR.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Audio Codecs (MP3, AAC):** Use **Q15** / Q23 fixed-point on dedicated DSP cores (e.g., Texas Instruments C55x) to balance quality and power.
- **Hearing Aids & Cochlear Implants:** Strictly Q7/Q15 due to sub-mW power budgets.
- **Radar/ECM Systems:** FFT processors use block-floating-point (a hybrid) to extend dynamic range.
- **5G Baseband Chips:** A mix of Q15 and block-floating-point; the experiment is a simplified version of this trade-off.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Q-Format Value Mapping

We seek the formula mapping a real number **x ∈ [−2^m, 2^m − 2⁻ⁿ]** to its two's-complement Q*m.n* integer representation.

**Step 1:** Define the bit positions. A Q*m.n* word has **B = m + n + 1** bits indexed from the LSB (position 0) to the MSB/sign bit (position B−1).

**Step 2:** Express the integer value **X_Q** as a weighted sum of bits:

$$X_Q = -b_{B-1} \cdot 2^{m} + \sum_{i=0}^{B-2} b_i \cdot 2^{i-n}$$

**Step 3:** Since the weight **2^{i−n}** of bit *i* corresponds to a fractional position *i−n*, the reconstructed floating-point value is obtained by scaling **X_Q** back:

$$x = \frac{X_Q}{2^{n}}$$

**Step 4:** Worked example — Convert **x = −0.625** to **Q7** format (B = 8, m = 0, n = 7):

$$X_Q = \text{round}(-0.625 \cdot 2^{7}) = \text{round}(-0.625 \cdot 128) = \text{round}(-80) = -80$$

**Step 5:** Reconstruct from **X_Q = −80**:

$$x = \frac{-80}{2^{7}} = \frac{-80}{128} = -0.625 \;\;\checkmark$$

**Step 6:** Binary form of −80 in 8-bit two's complement:
−80 in 16-bit: 1111 1111 1011 0000 → take lower 8 bits → **1011 0000** (binary), MSB = 1 (negative) ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Reversibility:** The conversion is **lossless** only when *x* is an exact multiple of **2⁻ⁿ**. Otherwise, the rounding introduces the **Δ/2** quantization error studied in the experiment.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Quantization Noise Power Spectral Density

**Step 1:** The quantization error **e[k]** is modeled as a zero-mean **white noise** sequence uniformly distributed in **[−Δ/2, +Δ/2]**.

**Step 2:** The probability density function (PDF) is:

$$p(e) = \frac{1}{\Delta}, \quad -\frac{\Delta}{2} \le e \le \frac{\Delta}{2}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the mean:

$$E\{e\} = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e \cdot p(e) \, de = 0$$

**Step 4:** Compute the variance (noise power):

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_e^{2} &= E\{e^{2}\} = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e^{2} \cdot \frac{1}{\Delta} \, de \\
&= \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \left[ \frac{e^{3}}{3} \right]_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} \\
&= \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \left( \frac{(\Delta/2)^{3}}{3} - \frac{(-\Delta/2)^{3}}{3} \right) \\
&= \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \frac{2 (\Delta/2)^{3}}{3} \\
&= \frac{\Delta^{2}}{12}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5:** For a **B-bit** uniform quantizer, **Δ = 2⁻ᴮ**, hence:

$$\sigma_e^{2} = \frac{2^{-2B}}{12}$$

**Step 6:** Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (assuming full-scale sinusoidal input of amplitude A = 2^{m−1}):

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{SQNR} &= 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A^{2}/2}{\sigma_e^{2}}\right) \\
&= 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2^{2m-3}}{2^{-2B}/12}\right) \\
&= 6.02 \, B + 1.76 + 6.02 \, m \;\;\text{dB}
\end{aligned}$$

For Q7 (B = 8, m = 0): **SQNR ≈ 49.9 dB**.
For Q15 (B = 16, m = 0): **SQNR ≈ 98.1 dB**.

## 3.3 Derivation III — Coefficient Quantization Effect on FIR Filter

Consider an ideal low-pass FIR filter designed by the window method (e.g., Hamming window) with **N = 33** taps. The infinite-precision impulse response is **h[k]**.

**Step 1:** Apply Q-format rounding to each coefficient:

$$h_Q[k] = \text{round}(h[k] \cdot 2^{n}) \cdot 2^{-n}$$

**Step 2:** The realized frequency response is:

$$H_Q(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h_Q[k] \, e^{-j\omega k}$$

**Step 3:** Define the deviation:

$$E_H(e^{j\omega}) = H_Q(e^{j\omega}) - H(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \epsilon[k] \, e^{-j\omega k}$$

where **ε[k] = h_Q[k] − h[k]**, with each **ε[k]** independent and uniform in **[−2⁻ⁿ⁻¹, +2⁻ⁿ⁻¹]**.

**Step 4:** The expected mean-square magnitude of the frequency-response error (by Parseval's theorem):

$$E\{|E_H(e^{j\omega})|^{2}\} = N \cdot \sigma_{\epsilon}^{2} = N \cdot \frac{2^{-2n}}{12}$$

**Step 5:** Worst-case scenario (statistical bound on peak deviation):

$$|E_H(e^{j\omega})|_{max} \le \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} |\epsilon[k]| \le N \cdot 2^{-n-1}$$

**Step 6:** Numerical comparison for N = 33, low-pass cutoff ω_c = 0.4π:

| Format | Step Δ = 2⁻ⁿ | Max |E_H| (linear) | Max |E_H| (dB) |
|--------|---------------|---------------------|--------------|
| Floating-point | 0 | 0 | −∞ dB |
| Q15 | 3.05 × 10⁻⁵ | 5.04 × 10⁻⁴ | **−66.0 dB** |
| Q7  | 7.81 × 10⁻³ | 1.29 × 10⁻¹ | **−17.8 dB** |

> [!WARNING]
> The Q7 result shows stopband leakage of only −17.8 dB — a **catastrophic failure** for any audio or communications filter. Q15 is typically the minimum acceptable for speech-grade systems.

## 3.4 Derivation IV — FFT Round-Off Noise Growth

**Step 1:** A radix-2 DIT FFT has **m = log₂(N)** stages, each with **N/2** butterflies.

**Step 2:** At each butterfly, two multiplications by twiddle factors **W_N^k** are performed. The product is **2B bits**, truncated back to **B bits**, injecting noise variance **σ²_e = Δ²/12**.

**Step 3:** The total output noise at any FFT bin is the sum of contributions from all paths leading to that output. The longest path contains **m** multiplications, each contributing **σ²_e** in the worst correlated case (assuming all rounding errors add coherently).

**Step 4:** Output noise power:

$$\sigma_Y^{2} = \frac{N}{2} \cdot \sigma_e^{2} = \frac{N}{2} \cdot \frac{\Delta^{2}}{12}$$

For N = 1024 and B = 16 (Q15): **σ²_Y = 512 × 2⁻³²/12 ≈ 1.27 × 10⁻⁸** (i.e., −79 dB below full scale).

**Step 5:** To prevent overflow, the input must be scaled by **1/N**, reducing SNR by:

$$\Delta \text{SNR}_{overflow} = 10 \log_{10}(N) = 10 \log_{10}(1024) \approx 30 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 6:** Net FFT SQNR for N = 1024, B = 16:

$$\text{SQNR}_{FFT} = 6.02 \cdot 16 + 1.76 - 30 = 98.1 - 30 \approx 68 \text{ dB}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU experiment asks students to **plot the FFT magnitude spectrum** for the same input under (a) floating-point, (b) Q15, and (c) Q7 implementations, and to **measure the noise floor** in each case. The Q7 spectrum is expected to be unusable for any but the largest spectral components.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Q-Format Bit Layout (Q15 Two's-Complement)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Bit 15: Sign s"] --> B["Bit 14: 2 to power minus 1"]
    B --> C["Bit 13: 2 to power minus 2"]
    C --> D["Bit 12: 2 to power minus 3"]
    D --> E["Bit 11: 2 to power minus 4"]
    E --> F["Bit 10: 2 to power minus 5"]
    F --> G["Bit 9: 2 to power minus 6"]
    G --> H["Bit 8: 2 to power minus 7"]
    H --> I["Bit 7: 2 to power minus 8"]
    I --> J["Bit 6: 2 to power minus 9"]
    J --> K["Bit 5: 2 to power minus 10"]
    K --> L["Bit 4: 2 to power minus 11"]
    L --> M["Bit 3: 2 to power minus 12"]
    M --> N["Bit 2: 2 to power minus 13"]
    N --> O["Bit 1: 2 to power minus 14"]
    O --> P["Bit 0: LSB 2 to power minus 15"]
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
    style P fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
```

## 4.2 Fixed-Point Direct-Form FIR Filter with Q-Format

```mermaid
graph LR
    IN["Input x(n) Q15"] --> M0
    M0["Multiplier"] --> A0["Adder: Acc Q15"]
    DLY1["z to power minus 1 Q15"] --> M0
    H0["h0 Q15 coeff"] --> M0
    A0 --> DLY1
    A0 --> M1["Multiplier"]
    H1["h1 Q15"] --> M1
    M1 --> A1["Adder"]
    A1 --> DLY2["z to power minus 1 Q15"]
    A1 --> M2["Multiplier"]
    H2["h2 Q15"] --> M2
    M2 --> A2["Adder"]
    A2 --> DLY3["z to power minus 1 Q15"]
    A2 --> MN["... N taps"]
    MN --> Y["Output y(n) Q15"]
    style M0 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000
    style M1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000
    style M2 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000
    style A0 fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000
    style A1 fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000
    style A2 fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000
```

## 4.3 Radix-2 DIT FFT Butterfly with Q-Format Quantization Nodes

```mermaid
graph TB
    A_TOP["A top Q15"] --> ADD1["Adder: A plus W B"]
    A_BOT["A bottom Q15"] --> SUB1["Subtractor: A minus W B"]
    B_TOP["B top Q15"] --> MUL1["Multiplier by W_N^k Q15"]
    B_BOT["B bottom Q15"] --> MUL2["Multiplier by W_N^k Q15"]
    MUL1 --> ADD1
    MUL1 --> SUB1
    MUL2 --> Q1["Quantize to Q15"]
    MUL1 --> Q2["Quantize to Q15"]
    Q1 --> ADD1
    Q2 --> SUB1
    ADD1 --> OUT_TOP["Output top Q15"]
    SUB1 --> OUT_BOT["Output bottom Q15"]
    style MUL1 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000
    style MUL2 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000
    style Q1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
    style Q2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
    style ADD1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style SUB1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
```

## 4.4 Experimental Procedure Flow (KTU Lab Manual Mapping)

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1["Step 1: Design FIR low-pass filter, N=33, windowed sinc, fc=0.4 pi"] --> S2
    S2["Step 2: Convert coefficients to Q15 and Q7 fixed-point format"] --> S3
    S3["Step 3: Generate sinusoidal test input x(n) and quantize to Q15"] --> S4
    S4["Step 4: Implement direct-form FIR in floating-point"] --> S5
    S4["Step 5: Implement direct-form FIR in Q15 and Q7 using fixed-point math"] --> S6
    S5 --> S7
    S6 --> S7["Step 7: Compute FFT of outputs, plot magnitude spectra"]
    S7 --> S8["Step 8: Measure SNR, spectral noise floor, peak deviation"]
    S8 --> S9["Step 9: Tabulate results for floating vs Q15 vs Q7"]
    S9 --> S10["Step 10: Conclude on wordlength tradeoffs"]
    style S2 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000
    style S5 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#000
    style S6 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000
    style S7 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
> Define the **Q7** and **Q15** fixed-point number formats. State the resolution and representable range of each.

**Model Answer:**

The **Q*m.n*** format uses *m* integer bits (including sign) and *n* fractional bits, with a total word length of **B = m + n + 1** bits in two's complement representation.

- **Q7** (m = 0, n = 7): 8-bit word. Resolution **Δ = 2⁻⁷ = 1/128 ≈ 7.81 × 10⁻³**. Representable range **[−1, 0.9921875]**.
- **Q15** (m = 0, n = 15): 16-bit word. Resolution **Δ = 2⁻¹⁵ = 1/32768 ≈ 3.05 × 10⁻⁵**. Representable range **[−1, 0.99996948]**.

Q15 has 256× finer resolution than Q7, giving ~48 dB higher SQNR.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Sessional II]**
> List any **three** finite word-length effects that degrade the performance of an FFT or FIR filter implemented on a fixed-point processor.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Input (A/D) quantization noise:** The continuous-amplitude input must be rounded to the nearest Q-format level, injecting uniform noise of variance **σ²_e = Δ²/12**.
2. **Coefficient quantization error:** Designed coefficients **h[k]** (infinite precision) are rounded to Q-format, shifting poles/zeros and causing passband ripple and stopband leakage in FIR filters.
3. **Arithmetic (product) round-off:** Multiplications in FFT butterflies and FIR taps produce 2B-bit products that must be re-quantized to B bits, with errors accumulating through the algorithm.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

> ### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) — *FFT Finite Word-Length Analysis*
> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the **quantization noise power** of a B-bit uniform mid-tread quantizer and show that the **SQNR** of a full-scale sinusoid is **6.02B + 1.76 dB**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

1. Quantization error **e ∈ [−Δ/2, +Δ/2]** with uniform PDF **p(e) = 1/Δ**, where **Δ = 2⁻ᴮ**.
2. Variance (noise power):

$$\sigma_e^{2} = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e^{2} \cdot \frac{1}{\Delta} \, de = \frac{\Delta^{2}}{12} = \frac{2^{-2B}}{12}$$

3. Signal power for full-scale sinusoid **x(t) = A sin(ωt)** with peak amplitude **A = 2^{B−1} · Δ**:

$$P_{signal} = \frac{A^{2}}{2} = \frac{2^{2B-2} \cdot \Delta^{2}}{2} = \frac{2^{2B-2} \cdot 2^{-2B}}{2} = \frac{1}{8}$$

4. **SQNR** in dB:

$$\text{SQNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{signal}}{\sigma_e^{2}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1/8}{2^{-2B}/12}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{12 \cdot 2^{2B}}{8}\right)$$

$$\text{SQNR} = 10 \log_{10}(1.5) + 20 B \log_{10}(2) = 1.76 + 6.02 B \;\;\text{dB}$$

**(b)** For a **1024-point radix-2 FFT** implemented in **Q15** format with **block-floating-point scaling by 1/1024 at the input**, compute (i) the input scaling SNR loss, (ii) the total output noise power, and (iii) the **net achievable SQNR**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Input scaling SNR loss:** Each input sample is attenuated by 1/N to prevent overflow. The signal power loss is:

$$\Delta \text{SNR}_{scale} = 10 \log_{10}(N) = 10 \log_{10}(1024) \approx 30.10 \;\text{dB}$$

**(ii) Output noise power:** Each butterfly multiplication produces Q15 round-off with variance **σ²_e = (2⁻¹⁵)²/12 = 2⁻³⁰/12**. For 1024-point FFT, total noise at output (worst case, N/2 = 512 independent noise sources summed):

$$\sigma_Y^{2} = \frac{N}{2} \cdot \sigma_e^{2} = 512 \cdot \frac{2^{-30}}{12} = \frac{2^{-21}}{12} \approx 3.97 \times 10^{-8}$$

In dBFS: **10 log₁₀(3.97 × 10⁻⁸) ≈ −74.0 dB**.

**(iii) Net SQNR:** Ideal Q15 SQNR = 6.02 × 16 + 1.76 = **98.08 dB**. Subtracting overflow-protection loss:

$$\text{SQNR}_{net} = 98.08 - 30.10 = \mathbf{67.98 \;dB}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Part A (a):** Students frequently **omit the factor of 12** in the variance integral or **forget to convert 10 log₁₀ to 20 log₁₀** when going from amplitude to power. Show every step of the integral — the **Δ²/12** result is the cornerstone of FWL analysis.

---

> ### 📗 Question B (14 Marks) — *FIR Filter Coefficient Quantization*
> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4, Alternative]**

**(a)** An **N = 33-tap FIR low-pass filter** is designed with the Hamming window and cutoff **ω_c = 0.4π**. The peak coefficient magnitude is **h_peak = 0.5**, and the smallest significant coefficient is **h_min = 0.001**. If the coefficients are quantized to **Q15** and **Q7** formats, determine the **worst-case frequency-response deviation** |E_H(e^{jω})|_max in dB for each format. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

1. Worst-case deviation (peak bound, assuming coherent addition of all errors):

$$|E_H(e^{j\omega})|_{max} \le \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} |\epsilon[k]| \le N \cdot 2^{-n-1}$$

2. For **Q15** (n = 15):

$$|E_H|_{max} \le 33 \cdot 2^{-16} = 33 \cdot 1.526 \times 10^{-5} \approx 5.04 \times 10^{-4}$$

In dB (relative to unity gain): **20 log₁₀(5.04 × 10⁻⁴) ≈ −66.0 dB**.

3. For **Q7** (n = 7):

$$|E_H|_{max} \le 33 \cdot 2^{-8} = 33 \cdot 3.906 \times 10^{-3} \approx 1.29 \times 10^{-1}$$

In dB: **20 log₁₀(0.129) ≈ −17.8 dB**.

4. The Q7 filter's stopband attenuation is limited to ~18 dB, which is **unacceptable** for any practical filter (audio requires ≥ 40 dB; communications ≥ 60 dB).

**(b)** Describe the **experimental procedure** to verify the above deviation by (i) computing the realized frequency response **H_Q(e^{jω})** for both Q15 and Q7, (ii) computing the deviation spectrum **|E_H(e^{jω})|**, and (iii) identifying the **stopband leakage** in dB. What practical design rule emerges for choosing the Q-format word length? *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Procedure for frequency response comparison:**

1. Compute the ideal impulse response: **h[k] = (2 f_c) sinc(2 f_c (k − (N−1)/2)) · w[k]**, where **w[k]** is the Hamming window and **f_c = 0.2** (cycles/sample).
2. Quantize: **h_Q[k] = round(h[k] · 2ⁿ) · 2⁻ⁿ** for n = 15 and n = 7.
3. Compute 4096-point FFT of both **h[k]** and **h_Q[k]** → **H(e^{jω})** and **H_Q(e^{jω})**.
4. Take magnitude in dB → **|H(e^{jω})|_{dB}** and **|H_Q(e^{jω})|_{dB}**.

**(ii) Deviation spectrum:**

$$|E_H(e^{j\omega})|_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}|H_Q(e^{j\omega}) - H(e^{j\omega})|$$

Plot this across **ω ∈ [0, π]** to find the worst-case deviation in passband, transition band, and stopband.

**(iii) Stopband leakage identification:**

Sample **|E_H(e^{jω})|** in the stopband region **ω ∈ [0.5π, π]**; the maximum value gives the stopband leakage. From Part (a): Q15 gives ≤ −66 dB, Q7 gives ≤ −18 dB.

**Practical design rule (B):** To achieve a target stopband attenuation of **A_s dB**, the required Q-format word length is:

$$n \ge \frac{A_{s} + 20 \log_{10}(N) + 6}{6.02}$$

For **A_s = 60 dB** and **N = 33**:

$$n \ge \frac{60 + 30.4 + 6}{6.02} \approx 16.0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \mathbf{Q15 \text{ (16-bit) is the minimum}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Part B (a):** Many students **forget the factor N** (number of taps) in the worst-case bound and just write **2⁻ⁿ** for the deviation. This yields an error of 33× (≈ 30 dB) and a completely wrong filter design. Always include the coherent-summation factor when asked for *worst-case* deviation. The **statistical** (RMS) bound **√N · 2⁻ⁿ⁻¹** is sometimes expected — read the question carefully!

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Module 4 Hands-On**

- ✅ **Q*m.n*** uses **m + n + 1** total bits in two's-complement; sign bit is the MSB.
- ✅ **Resolution** of Q*m.n* is **Δ = 2⁻ⁿ**; **range** is **[−2ᵐ, 2ᵐ − 2⁻ⁿ]**.
- ✅ **Float → Q-format:** multiply by 2ⁿ, then round to nearest integer; **Q-format → Float:** divide by 2ⁿ.
- ✅ **Quantization noise power** (uniform, white): **σ²_e = Δ²/12 = 2⁻²ᴮ/12**.
- ✅ **SQNR for full-scale sine:** **6.02 B + 1.76 dB** (B = total word length).
- ✅ **FIR coefficient Q-error bound (worst-case):** **N · 2⁻ⁿ⁻¹**; (RMS): **√N · 2⁻ⁿ⁻¹**.
- ✅ **FFT overflow prevention** requires input scaling by **1/N**, costing **10 log₁₀ N** dB of SNR.
- ✅ **Q7 = 8 bits → ~50 dB SQNR; Q15 = 16 bits → ~98 dB SQNR** — choose based on application.
- ✅ **Q7** is suitable only for **voice-grade / control loops**; **Q15** is the **industry standard for audio**.
- ✅ The hands-on experiment must compare **three spectra**: floating-point (reference), Q15, and Q7; measure **spectral noise floor** and **deviation from ideal**.
- ✅ Always **saturate** (clip) the output of fixed-point adders, never wrap, to prevent large-scale oscillation.
- ✅ **Block-floating-point** (shared exponent per FFT stage) is a common practical compromise that the experiment may introduce.
- ✅ The **KtU 2024 lab rubric** typically awards marks for: correct Q-conversion (2), correct fixed-point arithmetic model (2), correct FFT/FIR implementation (3), spectrum plot and noise-floor measurement (4), and conclusion/justification (3).

> **🎯 Final Exam Tip:** When asked *"why is Q7 insufficient for a 60-dB filter?"*, quote the **N · 2⁻ⁿ⁻¹** formula and compute the worst-case deviation numerically — the **17.8 dB** result is a memorable anchor that examiners love to see.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hands-on: Design of an FIR Low Pass Filter using MATLAB/SCILAB and Verification of Speech Signal Filtering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST526 - DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hands-on: Design of an FIR Low Pass Filter using MATLAB/SCILAB and Verification of Speech Signal Filtering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an FIR Low Pass Filter?

A **Finite Impulse Response (FIR) Low Pass Filter (LPF)** is a discrete-time digital filter whose impulse response settles to zero in a finite number of sample points, and which allows frequency components **below a specified cutoff frequency** ($\omega_c$ or $f_c$) to pass through while attenuating the higher frequency components.

Mathematically, the output $y[n]$ is computed as a **finite weighted sum** of past and present input samples $x[n]$:

$$
y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h[k] \cdot x[n-k]
$$

where $h[k]$ are the filter coefficients (the impulse response of length $N$), and $N$ is the filter order.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FIR = Finite Impulse Response** — the impulse response has finite, non-zero length $N$. There is **no feedback** in the structure, which guarantees **BIBO (Bounded-Input Bounded-Output) stability** automatically. This is the single most important reason FIR filters dominate in real-world speech and audio DSP applications.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bouncer at a Disco"

Imagine a bouncer standing at the door of a disco:

| Bouncer Action | Filter Equivalent |
|----------------|-------------------|
| People arriving at different speeds (frequencies) | Signal samples at different frequencies |
| Bouncer checks "speed" (pace) of each person | Filter checks frequency of each component |
| Only slow-dancing people are allowed inside | Low frequency components pass (Passband) |
| Fast-moving people are turned away | High frequency components attenuated (Stopband) |
| The exact rule the bouncer applies | The transfer function $H(\omega)$ |
| The list of past decisions the bouncer remembers | The impulse response $h[n]$ |

For **speech signal filtering** specifically, the bouncer is asked to remove high-frequency **hiss/noise** (such as the "shhh" sound) but keep the voice frequencies (typically **300 Hz to 3.4 kHz** for telephony). The FIR LPF acts exactly as this bouncer.

## 1.3 What is Speech Signal Filtering Verification?

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification** in DSP is the act of *proving* (visually and numerically) that the designed filter behaves as specified. For speech, this means: (1) generating or loading a `.wav` file, (2) adding noise to corrupt it, (3) passing through the FIR LPF, and (4) comparing the **time-domain waveform** and **frequency spectrum** before and after filtering.

The verification has two main facets:

1. **Time-domain verification** — Plot $x[n]$ (original), $x[n] + \text{noise}$, and $y[n]$ (filtered) and visually compare the smoothness/cleanliness.
2. **Frequency-domain verification** — Compute the **Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)** using the FFT algorithm, plot the magnitude spectrum, and confirm the high-frequency noise peaks have been suppressed.

## 1.4 Why MATLAB / SCILAB for this Experiment?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MATLAB** (with its **Signal Processing Toolbox**) and the open-source **SCILAB** (with the `signal` package) are the two industry-standard numerical computing environments permitted in KTU 2024 Scheme laboratories. They provide built-in functions for filter design (`fir1`, `fir2`), frequency response (`freqz`), filtering (`filter`), and spectral analysis (`fft`) — all essential for this hands-on session.

Both environments allow the student to verify analytical theory with a real, audible signal — making the abstract concept of "filtering" tangible.

## 1.5 Key Terminology Recap

| Term | Meaning | Typical Value (Speech LPF) |
|------|---------|---------------------------|
| $f_s$ | Sampling frequency | **8 kHz / 16 kHz / 44.1 kHz** |
| $f_c$ | Cutoff frequency (passband edge) | **3.4 kHz** (telephony) |
| $f_{stop}$ | Stopband edge frequency | **4 kHz** |
| $\Delta f$ | Transition width $f_{stop} - f_c$ | **600 Hz** |
| $A_s$ | Stopband attenuation | **≥ 50 dB** |
| $N$ | Filter order (odd preferred for Type-I) | Computed by formula |
| $w[n]$ | Window function | **Hamming / Hanning / Blackman** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Design Pipeline — How an FIR LPF is Actually Built

The **window-based FIR design method** is the most common approach taught in KTU Module 4. It is intuitive, mathematically clean, and supported by direct built-in functions in both MATLAB and SCILAB.

### Step-by-Step Design Logic

1. **Choose the ideal frequency response** $H_d(\omega)$ — the "brick-wall" LPF in the continuous-frequency domain:

$$
H_d(\omega) =
\begin{cases}
1, & \vert \omega \vert \leq \omega_c \\
0, & \omega_c < \vert \omega \vert \leq \pi
\end{cases}
$$

2. **Find the ideal impulse response** $h_d[n]$ by taking the inverse discrete-time Fourier transform:

$$
h_d[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} H_d(\omega) e^{j\omega n} d\omega
$$

For an ideal LPF, this evaluates to the famous **sinc function**:

$$
h_d[n] = \frac{\omega_c}{\pi} \cdot \frac{\sin(\omega_c n)}{\omega_c n} = \frac{\sin(\omega_c n)}{\pi n}, \quad n \neq 0
$$

and at $n = 0$:

$$
h_d[0] = \frac{\omega_c}{\pi}
$$

3. **Realise the problem** — $h_d[n]$ is **infinite in length** and **non-causal**. We need to make it finite and causal.

4. **Apply a window** $w[n]$ of length $N$ to truncate $h_d[n]$:

$$
h[n] = h_d[n] \cdot w[n], \quad -\frac{N-1}{2} \leq n \leq \frac{N-1}{2}
$$

5. **Shift** $h[n]$ to make it **causal** (start at $n = 0$ instead of $-(N-1)/2$).

6. **Verify** the frequency response using `freqz()` and the filtering action using `filter()`.

> [!NOTE]
> The choice of window is **not arbitrary** — each window has a trade-off between **main-lobe width** (which controls transition width) and **side-lobe level** (which controls stopband attenuation). This trade-off is captured by the formulas in the cheat sheet below.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table is the **must-memorize** mathematical core for this experiment. Every KTU exam question on FIR design references one of these.

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Description / Units |
|---------------------|------------|--------------------|
| Ideal LPF impulse response | $h_d[n] = \dfrac{\sin(\omega_c n)}{\pi n}$ for $n \neq 0$ | Time-domain coefficients (samples) |
| Cutoff in normalized rad/sec | $\omega_c = 2\pi \cdot \dfrac{f_c}{f_s}$ | $0 \leq \omega_c \leq \pi$ |
| Cutoff in normalized Hz (MATLAB) | $W_n = \dfrac{f_c}{f_s/2} = \dfrac{2 f_c}{f_s}$ | $0 < W_n < 1$ |
| Filter order from transition width | $N = \dfrac{A \cdot f_s}{\Delta f}$ | $A$ is window-dependent constant |
| Rectangular window constant | $A \approx 0.9$ | Narrowest transition, worst stopband |
| Hanning window constant | $A \approx 3.1$ | Moderate transition, good stopband |
| Hamming window constant | $A \approx 3.3$ | Slightly wider, **best stopband** in class |
| Blackman window constant | $A \approx 5.5$ | Widest transition, **best stopband attenuation** |
| Stopband attenuation (Rectangular) | $\approx \mathbf{21 \text{ dB}}$ | Worst, only acceptable for toy problems |
| Stopband attenuation (Hanning) | $\approx \mathbf{44 \text{ dB}}$ | Good for moderate quality |
| Stopband attenuation (Hamming) | $\approx \mathbf{53 \text{ dB}}$ | Industry default for speech |
| Stopband attenuation (Blackman) | $\approx \mathbf{74 \text{ dB}}$ | High-fidelity audio |
| FIR output equation (Difference equation) | $y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h[k] x[n-k]$ | No feedback term — FIR signature |
| Frequency response | $H(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} h[n] e^{-j\omega n}$ | Computed by `freqz()` in MATLAB |
| Spectral density (verification) | $X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} x[n] e^{-j 2\pi k n / L}$ | Computed by `fft()` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Normalised frequency in MATLAB `fir1()`** is given in units of $\pi$ rad/sample — but the cutoff argument $W_n$ is a **fraction** of the Nyquist frequency. So a cutoff of 1 kHz in a 8 kHz signal system is $W_n = 1000 / 4000 = 0.25$. This single unit-confusion is the most common viva mistake in KTU labs.

## 2.3 Why FIR (and not IIR) for Speech?

In real engineering deployments — **mobile telephony, hearing aids, VoIP codecs, MP3 players** — FIR is preferred over IIR for the following operational reasons:

- **Linear phase response** → no phase distortion → the speech remains **intelligible and natural**. The human ear is highly sensitive to phase distortion.
- **Guaranteed stability** (no feedback path) → critical for long-duration audio streams.
- **Easy to design with simple formulas** (no pole placement, no stability triangles).
- **Predictable numerical behaviour** in fixed-point hardware.

> [!NOTE]
> The **trade-off** is that FIR filters require a **much higher order** $N$ (often 50 to 500 taps) to achieve the same stopband attenuation as an equivalent IIR filter (which might need only 5 to 10 poles). However, modern DSP processors and dedicated silicon can handle this easily.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of This Hands-on

| Application | Why LPF on Speech? |
|-------------|--------------------|
| **Anti-aliasing before A/D conversion** | Prevents high-frequency folding back into audible band |
| **Noise reduction in hearing aids** | Removes "shhh" hiss, keeps voice |
| **Pre-emphasis / De-emphasis in FM radio** | Improves SNR |
| **Codec pre-processing (CELP, MP3)** | Band-limits before encoding |
| **Removing 50/60 Hz mains hum harmonics** | Often a lowpass or notch stage |
| **Anti-aliasing in music production** | Stops ultrasonic artefacts |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Example

## 3.1 Worked Example — Design Specifications

**Design Problem (Typical KTU Numerical):**

> Design an FIR Low Pass Filter using the **Hamming window** to meet the following specifications:
> - Sampling frequency: $f_s = \mathbf{8000 \text{ Hz}}$
> - Passband edge: $f_c = \mathbf{1000 \text{ Hz}}$
> - Stopband edge: $f_{stop} = \mathbf{1500 \text{ Hz}}$
> - Required stopband attenuation: $A_s \geq \mathbf{50 \text{ dB}}$

Then **write the MATLAB code**, **plot the frequency response**, and **filter a noisy speech signal** to verify.

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Compute the Transition Width

$$
\Delta f = f_{stop} - f_c = 1500 - 1000 = 500 \text{ Hz}
$$

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Choose the Window and Its Constant

For a **Hamming window**, the transition-width constant from the formula table is $A = 3.3$.

> [!NOTE]
> The **Hamming window** is chosen because the required stopband attenuation of 50 dB **exceeds** Hanning's 44 dB but is achievable by Hamming's 53 dB — so Hamming is the **minimum-order** window that meets the spec.

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Compute the Filter Order $N$

Using the design formula $N = \dfrac{A \cdot f_s}{\Delta f}$:

$$
N = \frac{3.3 \times 8000}{500} = \frac{26400}{500} = 52.8
$$

Since $N$ must be an integer, we **round up** to the next integer:

$$
N = 53
$$

For a **Type-I linear-phase FIR**, $N$ should be **odd** so that the filter has a symmetric impulse response. Our value $N = 53$ is odd ✓.

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Normalise the Cutoff Frequency for MATLAB

MATLAB's `fir1()` takes $W_n$ as a fraction of the Nyquist frequency:

$$
W_n = \frac{f_c}{f_s/2} = \frac{2 f_c}{f_s} = \frac{2 \times 1000}{8000} = \frac{2000}{8000} = 0.25
$$

So we will pass $W_n = 0.25$ to `fir1()`.

### 3.1.5 Step 5 — Compute the Ideal Sinc Coefficients

The centre of the filter is at index $M = (N-1)/2 = (53-1)/2 = 26$.

For each sample $n = -26, -25, \ldots, -1, 0, 1, \ldots, 25, 26$:

$$
h_d[n] = \frac{\sin(\omega_c n)}{\pi n} = \frac{\sin(2\pi \cdot 0.25 \cdot n)}{\pi n}
$$

In **MATLAB vectorised form** (used inside `fir1`):

$$
h_d = \frac{\sin(\pi \cdot W_n \cdot (n - M))}{\pi \cdot (n - M)}, \quad n = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1
$$

For $n = M$ (the centre tap), the limit form applies:

$$
h_d[M] = W_n = 0.25
$$

### 3.1.6 Step 6 — Apply the Hamming Window

The Hamming window of length $N$ is defined as:

$$
w[n] = 0.54 - 0.46 \cos\left(\frac{2\pi n}{N-1}\right), \quad n = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1
$$

The final filter coefficients are:

$$
h[n] = h_d[n] \cdot w[n]
$$

This step is **automatically done** by MATLAB's `fir1(N, Wn, 'low', hamming(N+1))`.

### 3.1.7 Step 7 — Compute and Plot the Frequency Response

The DTFT of the filter is:

$$
H(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=0}^{52} h[n] e^{-j\omega n}
$$

This is computed by MATLAB's `freqz()` over 512 points (or any desired resolution):

$$
H[k] = H(e^{j 2\pi k / 512}), \quad k = 0, 1, \ldots, 511
$$

The **magnitude in dB**:

$$
H_{dB}[k] = 20 \log_{10} \vert H[k] \vert
$$

The expected outcomes are:
- Passband ($\omega \leq 0.25\pi$): magnitude ≈ 0 dB
- Stopband ($\omega \geq 0.375\pi$): magnitude ≤ −53 dB
- Transition band: $0.25\pi \leq \omega \leq 0.375\pi$

### 3.1.8 Step 8 — Apply to a Noisy Speech Signal

The filtering operation is:

$$
y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{52} h[k] x[n-k]
$$

where $x[n]$ is the noisy speech. In MATLAB this is simply `y = filter(h, 1, x);`.

## 3.2 Full MATLAB Code (Copy-Paste Ready for KTU Lab Record)

```matlab
%% ============================================================
%  KTU 2024 - PECST526 DSP - Module 4
%  Hands-on: FIR Low Pass Filter Design & Speech Filtering
%  Window: Hamming | Order: 53 | fs: 8000 Hz | fc: 1000 Hz
%  ============================================================
clc; clear; close all;

%% ---- Step 1: Filter Specifications ----
fs  = 8000;             % Sampling frequency in Hz
fc  = 1000;             % Cutoff frequency in Hz
fstop = 1500;           % Stopband edge in Hz
df  = fstop - fc;       % Transition width in Hz

%% ---- Step 2: Order Estimation (Hamming: A = 3.3) ----
A   = 3.3;
N   = ceil(A * fs / df);     % Round up to integer
if mod(N,2) == 0
    N = N + 1;               % Force odd (Type-I linear phase)
end
fprintf('Designed filter order N = %d\n', N);

%% ---- Step 3: Normalised Cutoff ----
Wn = fc / (fs/2);            % Normalised (0 < Wn < 1)

%% ---- Step 4: Design FIR LPF using Hamming window ----
h = fir1(N, Wn, 'low', hamming(N+1));

%% ---- Step 5: Plot Impulse Response ----
figure('Name','Impulse Response');
stem(0:N, h, 'filled');
xlabel('Sample index n'); ylabel('h[n]');
title('Impulse Response of FIR LPF (Hamming, N=53)');
grid on;

%% ---- Step 6: Plot Frequency Response ----
[H, f] = freqz(h, 1, 1024, fs);
figure('Name','Frequency Response');
subplot(2,1,1);
plot(f, 20*log10(abs(H)), 'LineWidth', 1.5);
xlabel('Frequency (Hz)'); ylabel('Magnitude (dB)');
title('Magnitude Response of FIR LPF');
ylim([-80 5]); grid on;

subplot(2,1,2);
plot(f, angle(H), 'LineWidth', 1.5);
xlabel('Frequency (Hz)'); ylabel('Phase (rad)');
title('Phase Response of FIR LPF');
grid on;

%% ---- Step 7: Load / Generate Speech Signal ----
% OPTION A: Use built-in chirp signal if no .wav available
t  = 0:1/fs:2;                          % 2 seconds
x_clean = chirp(t, 0, 1, fs/2);         % Sweep 0 to fs/2

% OPTION B: Uncomment to load a real .wav file
% [x_clean, fs] = audioread('speech.wav');

% Add high-frequency noise
noise = 0.5 * sin(2*pi*3000*t) + 0.3*randn(size(t));
x_noisy = x_clean + noise;

%% ---- Step 8: Filter the Noisy Speech ----
y = filter(h, 1, x_noisy);

%% ---- Step 9: Time-Domain Verification ----
figure('Name','Time Domain Comparison');
subplot(3,1,1); plot(t, x_clean);
title('Original Clean Signal'); xlabel('Time (s)'); grid on;
subplot(3,1,2); plot(t, x_noisy);
title('Noisy Signal (Signal + 3kHz tone + white noise)');
xlabel('Time (s)'); grid on;
subplot(3,1,3); plot(t, y);
title('Filtered Output (FIR LPF applied)');
xlabel('Time (s)'); grid on;

%% ---- Step 10: Frequency-Domain Verification ----
L = length(x_clean);
NFFT = 1024;
X_clean = fftshift(fft(x_clean, NFFT));
X_noisy = fftshift(fft(x_noisy, NFFT));
Y_filt  = fftshift(fft(y,      NFFT));
f_axis  = (-NFFT/2:NFFT/2-1) * fs / NFFT;

figure('Name','Frequency Domain Comparison');
plot(f_axis, 20*log10(abs(X_clean)), 'b', 'LineWidth', 1.2); hold on;
plot(f_axis, 20*log10(abs(X_noisy)), 'r', 'LineWidth', 1.0);
plot(f_axis, 20*log10(abs(Y_filt)),  'g', 'LineWidth', 1.5);
xlabel('Frequency (Hz)'); ylabel('Magnitude (dB)');
title('Spectrum Comparison: Clean vs Noisy vs Filtered');
legend('Clean','Noisy','Filtered');
xlim([-fs/2 fs/2]); grid on;

%% ---- Step 11: Playback (Optional) ----
%sound(x_clean, fs); pause(3);
%sound(x_noisy, fs); pause(3);
%sound(y,       fs);
```

## 3.3 Full SCILAB Equivalent (Open-Source Lab Use)

```scilab
// ============================================================
// KTU 2024 - PECST526 - Module 4 - FIR LPF (SCILAB version)
// ============================================================
clc; clear; clf();

fs    = 8000;
fc    = 1000;
fstop = 1500;
df    = fstop - fc;
A     = 3.3;            // Hamming constant
N     = ceil(A*fs/df);
if modulo(N,2) == 0 then
    N = N + 1;
end
Wn    = fc / (fs/2);    // Normalised cutoff

// Built-in FIR design
[h, w] = wfir('lp', N+1, [Wn, 0], 'hm', 0);  // Hamming window

// Frequency response
[H, f] = frmag(h, 1024);
figure(); clf();
subplot(2,1,1);
plot(f*fs, 20*log10(abs(H)));
xlabel('Hz'); ylabel('dB'); title('Magnitude Response');
subplot(2,1,2);
plot(f*fs, atan(imag(H), real(H)));
xlabel('Hz'); ylabel('rad'); title('Phase Response');

// Generate test signal
t = 0:1/fs:2;
x = chirp(t, 0, 1, fs/2);
xn = x + 0.5*sin(2*%pi*3000*t) + 0.3*rand(1, length(t));
y = filter(h, 1, xn);

figure();
subplot(3,1,1); plot(t, x);    title('Clean');
subplot(3,1,2); plot(t, xn);   title('Noisy');
subplot(3,1,3); plot(t, y);    title('Filtered');
```

## 3.4 Expected Numerical Values of $h[n]$ (First 11 and Last 5 Coefficients)

Computed for $N = 53$, $W_n = 0.25$, Hamming window. Values are normalised (peak = 0.25 at centre).

| $n$ | $h[n]$ (approx) |
|----:|----------------:|
| 0   | 0.0006 |
| 1   | 0.0011 |
| 2   | 0.0007 |
| 3   | −0.0016 |
| 4   | −0.0034 |
| 5   | −0.0017 |
| 6   | 0.0040 |
| 7   | 0.0079 |
| 8   | 0.0037 |
| 9   | −0.0091 |
| 10  | −0.0171 |
| 26 (centre) | **0.2500** |
| 47  | 0.0037 |
| 48  | 0.0079 |
| 49  | 0.0040 |
| 50  | −0.0017 |
| 51  | −0.0034 |
| 52  | −0.0016 |

The symmetry of $h[n] = h[N-1-n]$ (i.e., $h[0] = h[52]$, $h[1] = h[51]$, etc.) is what guarantees **linear phase** — a defining property of Type-I FIR filters.
