# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Hands-on Workflow (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: KTU Lab Session] --> B[Step 1: Define Filter Specs - fs, fc, fstop, As]
    B --> C[Step 2: Compute Transition Width df]
    C --> D[Step 3: Choose Window from Spec - Rect / Hann / Hamm / Blackman]
    D --> E[Step 4: Compute Filter Order N]
    E --> F{Is N odd?}
    F -- No --> G[N = N + 1]
    F -- Yes --> H[Step 5: Normalise Cutoff Wn = 2fc/fs]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Step 6: Call fir1 to Generate h n]
    I --> J[Step 7: freqz to Compute H omega]
    J --> K[Step 8: Plot Magnitude in dB]
    K --> L[Step 9: Verify Passband Ripple and Stopband Attenuation]
    L --> M{Specs Met?}
    M -- No --> D
    M -- Yes --> N[Step 10: Load or Generate Speech .wav]
    N --> O[Step 11: Add High Frequency Noise]
    O --> P[Step 12: Apply filter h to Noisy Signal]
    P --> Q[Step 13: y n = sum h k x n minus k]
    Q --> R[Step 14: Time Domain Plot x vs xn vs y]
    R --> S[Step 15: FFT Spectrum Comparison]
    S --> T[Step 16: Listen using sound or save as wav]
    T --> U[End: Lab Record Complete]
```

## 4.2 Internal Functional Architecture of MATLAB `fir1`

The following diagram shows how MATLAB's `fir1()` function internally performs the design — exactly mimicking the manual procedure we derived in Section 3.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input: N, Wn, type, window] --> B[Generate Centered Index Vector n = 0 to N]
    B --> C[Compute Ideal Sinc h d n = sin pi Wn n minus M divided by pi n minus M]
    C --> D[Evaluate Limit at Centre h d M = Wn]
    D --> E[Generate Window w n = 0.54 minus 0.46 cos 2 pi n over N minus 1]
    E --> F[Multiply h = h d element wise times w]
    F --> G[Return Filter Coefficients h n]
```

## 4.3 FIR Filter Direct-Form Structure (Hardware / Realisation View)

This is the **hardware-level architecture** of an FIR filter — the actual taps, multipliers, and adders that compute $y[n]$:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    x0[x n] --> m0[Multiply by h 0] --> A((Add))
    x1[x n minus 1] --> m1[Multiply by h 1] --> A
    x2[x n minus 2] --> m2[Multiply by h 2] --> A
    xN1[x n minus N plus 1] --> mN[Multiply by h N minus 1] --> A
    A --> y[Output y n]
    x0 --> z1[Z minus 1 delay] --> x1
    x1 --> z2[Z minus 1 delay] --> x2
    x2 --> zN[Z minus 1 delay chain] --> xN1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice the **delay line** $z^{-1}$ blocks (the unit-sample delay elements). Each successive input sample is delayed by one sample before being multiplied by the corresponding coefficient. This is the **direct-form FIR** structure — the simplest realisation and the one used in fixed-point DSP chips like the **Texas Instruments TMS320** family.

## 4.4 Verification Flowchart — Comparing Signals

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Original Speech x clean] --> B[Add Noise]
    B --> C[Corrupted x noisy]
    C --> D[FIR LPF h n]
    D --> E[Filtered y n]
    A --> F[FFT X clean omega]
    C --> G[FFT X noisy omega]
    E --> H[FFT Y omega]
    F --> I[Overlay 3 Spectra]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J{Stopband Attenuation > 50 dB?}
    J -- Yes --> K[PASS: Filter Verified]
    J -- No --> L[FAIL: Increase Order or Change Window]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define an FIR filter. Why is it preferred over IIR for speech signal processing?**

**Model Answer:**

An **FIR (Finite Impulse Response) filter** is a discrete-time filter whose impulse response $h[n]$ has **finite duration** (settles to exactly zero after $N$ samples), and whose output is computed purely as a weighted sum of past and present input samples — with **no feedback** path:

$$
y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h[k] x[n-k]
$$

It is preferred over IIR for speech signal processing because:

1. **Linear phase response** — Symmetric coefficients give constant group delay, preserving the **temporal shape and intelligibility** of speech. IIR filters have non-linear phase, distorting the speech waveform.
2. **Guaranteed stability** — With no feedback, the system is **BIBO stable for any coefficient set**. IIR can become unstable if poles move outside the unit circle.
3. **No phase distortion** — Critical because the human ear is sensitive to phase; IIR introduces phase warping especially near the cutoff.
4. **Easier design and verification** — Closed-form formulas (window method) and straightforward stability checks.

> **Viva Tip:** Always mention "**linear phase**" as the first reason — examiners expect it.

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**State the Kaiser formula for estimating FIR filter order. What is the role of the window function in FIR design?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Kaiser formula** for estimating the minimum FIR filter order required to meet a specification of stopband attenuation $A_s$ (in dB) and transition width $\Delta f$ (in Hz) at sampling rate $f_s$ is:

$$
N \approx \frac{A_s - 8}{2.285 \cdot \Delta \omega}
$$

where the normalised transition width is $\Delta \omega = 2\pi \Delta f / f_s$.

A simpler equivalent used in KTU labs is the **windowed-design order formula**:

$$
N = \frac{A \cdot f_s}{\Delta f}
$$

where $A$ is a constant depending on the chosen window (e.g., $A = 3.3$ for Hamming).

**Role of the window function** $w[n]$:

- The **ideal** (brick-wall) LPF impulse response $h_d[n] = \sin(\omega_c n)/(\pi n)$ is **infinite in length** and **non-causal**, and is **not absolutely summable** for implementation.
- Multiplying $h_d[n]$ by a finite-length window $w[n]$ **truncates** it to length $N$ and **makes it causal** by shifting.
- The choice of window controls the **trade-off** between **main-lobe width** (transition sharpness) and **side-lobe level** (stopband attenuation).
- Examples: Rectangular → narrowest transition but worst stopband (21 dB); Blackman → widest transition but best stopband (74 dB).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Design an FIR Low Pass Filter using the **Hamming window** for the following specifications: sampling frequency $f_s = 8$ kHz, passband edge $f_c = 1.2$ kHz, stopband edge $f_{stop} = 2$ kHz. Compute the filter order $N$, normalise the cutoff, and **write the MATLAB code** to plot the impulse response and magnitude response. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **how you would verify the filter** by applying it to a noisy speech signal. Show the **time-domain and frequency-domain comparison plots** and describe what conclusions can be drawn. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — Transition width:**

$$
\Delta f = f_{stop} - f_c = 2000 - 1200 = 800 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 2 — Order** (Hamming constant $A = 3.3$):

$$
N = \lceil 3.3 \times 8000 / 800 \rceil = \lceil 33 \rceil = 33
$$

$N$ is odd ✓ (Type-I linear phase).

**Step 3 — Normalised cutoff:**

$$
W_n = \frac{2 f_c}{f_s} = \frac{2 \times 1200}{8000} = 0.30
$$

**Step 4 — MATLAB code:**

```matlab
fs = 8000; fc = 1200; fstop = 2000;
df = fstop - fc;
A = 3.3;
N = ceil(A*fs/df);
if mod(N,2)==0, N = N+1; end
Wn = 2*fc/fs;
h = fir1(N, Wn, 'low', hamming(N+1));

subplot(2,1,1);
stem(0:N, h, 'filled');
xlabel('n'); ylabel('h[n]');
title('Impulse Response (N=33)');

[H, f] = freqz(h, 1, 1024, fs);
subplot(2,1,2);
plot(f, 20*log10(abs(H)));
xlabel('Hz'); ylabel('dB');
title('Magnitude Response');
ylim([-80 5]); grid on;
```

**Expected results:**
- Passband (0 to 1.2 kHz): magnitude ≈ 0 dB, ripple within 0.1 dB.
- Stopband (above 2 kHz): magnitude ≤ −53 dB.
- Transition band: 1.2 kHz to 2 kHz.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Verification procedure:**

1. **Load a speech signal** (e.g., `[x, fs] = audioread('speech.wav');`) or generate a synthetic one with `chirp`.

2. **Add high-frequency noise** above the cutoff, for example:

```matlab
t = (0:length(x)-1)'/fs;
noise = 0.5*sin(2*pi*3000*t) + 0.3*randn(size(x));
xn = x + noise;
```

3. **Apply the filter** to the noisy signal:

```matlab
y = filter(h, 1, xn);
```

4. **Time-domain verification** — Plot $x$, $x_n$, and $y$ in three stacked subplots. Observation: $y[n]$ should look smoother, with the high-frequency oscillations removed.

5. **Frequency-domain verification** — Compute FFT of all three:

```matlab
NFFT = 1024;
X  = fftshift(fft(x,  NFFT));
Xn = fftshift(fft(xn, NFFT));
Y  = fftshift(fft(y,  NFFT));
f  = (-NFFT/2:NFFT/2-1)*fs/NFFT;

plot(f, 20*log10(abs(X)),  'b', ...
     f, 20*log10(abs(Xn)), 'r', ...
     f, 20*log10(abs(Y)),  'g', 'LineWidth', 1.2);
xlabel('Hz'); ylabel('dB');
legend('Clean','Noisy','Filtered');
```

6. **Observations / Conclusions:**
- The 3 kHz tonal peak visible in $|X_n(\omega)|$ is **eliminated** in $|Y(\omega)|$ by at least 53 dB.
- Spectral components above 2 kHz are attenuated.
- Components below 1.2 kHz are preserved with negligible loss.
- Audible verification: playing $y$ sounds cleaner than $x_n$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Compare the **Rectangular, Hanning, Hamming, and Blackman windows** in terms of main-lobe width, side-lobe level, and the **trade-off** between transition width and stopband attenuation. Use a table. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a digital audio system with $f_s = 44.1$ kHz, design an FIR LPF with cutoff $f_c = 10$ kHz and stopband edge $f_{stop} = 11$ kHz using the **Blackman window**. Compute $N$, write the SCILAB code to design the filter, and **plot the magnitude response**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Comparison Table:**

| Window | Main-lobe Width (bins) | Peak Side-lobe Level (dB) | Stopband Attenuation (dB) | Transition Sharpness |
|--------|-----------------------:|--------------------------:|--------------------------:|---------------------|
| **Rectangular** | 2 (narrowest) | **−13** dB (worst) | ≈ **21 dB** | Sharpest, but most ripple |
| **Hanning** | 4 | −31 dB | ≈ **44 dB** | Moderate |
| **Hamming** | 4 | **−41** dB (best in class for this lobe level) | ≈ **53 dB** | Moderate |
| **Blackman** | 6 (widest) | −58 dB | ≈ **74 dB** | Widest transition |

**Trade-off explanation:**

- **Main-lobe width** determines the **transition width** of the filter. A narrower main-lobe → sharper transition but more leakage to nearby frequencies.
- **Side-lobe level** determines the **stopband attenuation** and **passband ripple**. Lower side-lobe level → cleaner stopband.
- **Rectangular** has the narrowest main-lobe (best transition) but the **highest side-lobe level** (worst stopband). It is the **identity** window — equivalent to no windowing at all.
- **Blackman** has the **widest main-lobe** (worst transition — needs higher $N$) but the **lowest side-lobe level** (best stopband attenuation). Used in high-fidelity audio.
- **Hamming** is the **best practical compromise** — almost as good stopband as Blackman with a transition nearly as sharp as Hanning. **Industry default.**

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Transition width:**

$$
\Delta f = f_{stop} - f_c = 11000 - 10000 = 1000 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 2 — Order** (Blackman constant $A = 5.5$):

$$
N = \lceil 5.5 \times 44100 / 1000 \rceil = \lceil 242.55 \rceil = 243
$$

$N = 243$ is odd ✓.

**Step 3 — Normalised cutoff:**

$$
W_n = \frac{2 f_c}{f_s} = \frac{2 \times 10000}{44100} = 0.4535
$$

**Step 4 — SCILAB code:**

```scilab
fs = 44100; fc = 10000; fstop = 11000;
df = fstop - fc;
A = 5.5;
N = ceil(A*fs/df);
if modulo(N,2) == 0 then N = N + 1; end
Wn = 2*fc/fs;

// Blackman window design
[h, w] = wfir('lp', N+1, [Wn 0], 'bm', 0);

[H, fr] = frmag(h, 1024);
figure();
plot(fr*fs, 20*log10(abs(H)));
xlabel('Hz'); ylabel('dB');
title('Magnitude Response of FIR LPF (Blackman, N=243)');
xlim([0 fs/2]);
```

**Expected outcomes:**
- Passband (0–10 kHz): 0 dB with < 0.01 dB ripple.
- Stopband (above 11 kHz): ≤ −74 dB — **excellent for CD-quality audio**.
- Transition: 10–11 kHz.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting to round $N$ up**: Using `floor` instead of `ceil` gives a filter that **fails** the stopband spec by a few dB. Always **round up** and re-check.
> 2. **Mixing up normalised frequency conventions**: MATLAB's `fir1` uses $W_n = f_c/(f_s/2)$ (range 0 to 1). The DTFT formula uses $\omega_c$ in radians (range 0 to $\pi$). Using one in the other gives a **completely wrong** cutoff.
> 3. **Forgetting to force $N$ odd**: A Type-II FIR (even $N$) has a zero at $\omega = \pi$, making it **impossible to use as a high-pass filter** and causing asymmetric response for LPF near Nyquist.
> 4. **Using `sound` with a wrong `fs`**: Speech sounds like chipmunks or is unintelligibly slow — common viva-losing mistake.
> 5. **Not verifying in frequency domain**: A filter may *look* like it works in time domain but actually be **completely wrong** (wrong coefficients). Always show the magnitude plot in dB.
> 6. **Mixing up MATLAB and SCILAB syntax**: `fir1(N, Wn)` vs SCILAB's `wfir('lp', N+1, [Wn 0], 'hm', 0)`. The +1 in N+1 is **mandatory** in SCILAB.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Before walking into the KTU lab exam or viva, ensure you can confidently tick every box below:**

- [ ] **FIR filter definition** — finite-length impulse response, **no feedback**, always stable.
- [ ] **The five steps of the window method** — ideal $H_d(\omega)$ → $h_d[n]$ sinc → window → truncate → shift → causal.
- [ ] **Ideal LPF impulse response formula** — $h_d[n] = \sin(\omega_c n)/(\pi n)$ for $n \neq 0$, and $h_d[0] = \omega_c/\pi$.
- [ ] **Order formula** — $N = A \cdot f_s / \Delta f$, with $A = 0.9, 3.1, 3.3, 5.5$ for Rect, Hann, Hamm, Blackman.
- [ ] **Always round $N$ up** to the nearest integer; **force odd** for Type-I linear phase.
- [ ] **MATLAB's $W_n = 2 f_c / f_s$** — NOT $f_c / f_s$ (a classic blunder).
- [ ] **MATLAB `fir1` syntax** — `h = fir1(N, Wn, 'low', hamming(N+1))` — note the `N+1` in the window.
- [ ] **SCILAB `wfir` syntax** — `h = wfir('lp', N+1, [Wn 0], 'hm', 0)`.
- [ ] **Output equation** — $y[n] = \sum h[k] x[n-k]$ — no feedback term.
- [ ] **Stopband attenuations** — Rect 21 dB, Hanning 44 dB, Hamming 53 dB, Blackman 74 dB.
- [ ] **Why FIR for speech** — linear phase, BIBO stable, no phase distortion → natural-sounding audio.
- [ ] **Two verifications** — time-domain (smoothness) AND frequency-domain (spectrum peaks gone).
- [ ] **FFT syntax** — `Y = fftshift(fft(y, NFFT))`; magnitude in dB = `20*log10(abs(Y))`.
- [ ] **Adding noise** — tonal `sin(2πf t)` + `randn` (white Gaussian).
- [ ] **Linear phase property** — symmetric $h[n] = h[N-1-n]$ gives **constant group delay** = $(N-1)/2$ samples.
- [ ] **Play back with `sound(y, fs)`** to hear the difference — qualitative check.
- [ ] **Type-I, II, III, IV FIR** — distinguished by symmetry and $N$ parity. Type-I (odd $N$, symmetric) is most common.
